column5: Millipore-Sigma Steritest Symbio Laminar Flow Hood (LFH) pump
column29:
The Food and Drug
Administration (FDA), Irvine Medical Products Laboratory (IRVLMP) is a regulatory
testing laboratory that is ISO 17025 accredited. The laboratory is responsible for
microbiological testing of multiple pharmaceutical dosage forms which require sterility
testing to determine if they comply with regulations. The laboratory performs sterility
testing using Steritest ISL pumps manufactured by Millipore-Sigma. In order to remove
existing bottlenecks in suitability testing which precedes sterility testing, the laboratory
requires an additional closed-filtration sterility pump that is a “Brand Name or Equal”
Millipore-Sigma Steritest Symbio Laminar Flow Hood (LFH) pump, to be used in a
laminar air flow hood. The acquisition of a “Brand Name or Equal” Millipore-Sigma
Steritest LFH pump will ensure compatibility with existing Millipore-Sigma Steritest
ISL test kits, allowing their continued use with the new closed-filtration pump and
enabling the laboratory to meet the suitability requirements specified in USP <71>, the
official compendial method for sterility testing.

column17: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF STANDARDS AND TECHNOLOGY
column20: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF STANDARDS AND TECHNOLOGY
column29:
Silicon carbide field effect transistors (FETs) have emerged as a superior solution to silicon FETs for high power semiconductor applications. In
recent years, the silicon carbide FET industry has grown at a significant rate with applications in photovoltaics, the power grid, electric
vehicles, energy storage, and more. Despite the commercial success of these devices, the industry is plagued by reliability problems, often
leading to the need for redundancy in integrated systems. Arguably most important among these issues is the extrinsic breakdown
characteristics of the FET gate oxide. Studying this problem requires a deep statistical understanding of device behavior, and innovative
solutions for pre-screening from the factory. A large-scale, multi-faceted reliability testing platform is required in order to find solutions.
The NIST Physical Measurement Laboratory, Nanoscale Device Characterization Division, Advanced Electronics Group (AEG) requires a 300
mm wafer probing station which will facilitate the study of transistor characteristics, leakage currents, and breakdown behavior of silicon
carbide FET gate dielectrics. This purchase aligns with the CHIPS R&D Programs Office’s mission to standardize new materials, processes, and equipment for microelectronics research. The resulting data will be compared to statistical breakdown data obtained through our effort to
create a massively parallel testing platform. The data will also be compared to that of industry standard pre-screening tests on identical
samples in order to verify or dispute their accuracy. Similar methodology may be applied to other gate oxide reliability problems in the future.

column17: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF STANDARDS AND TECHNOLOGY
column20: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF STANDARDS AND TECHNOLOGY
column29: The NIST Physical Measurement Laboratory, Microsystems and Nanotechnology Division, Photonics and Optomechanics Group (POG) develops micromechanical resonators for precision sensing (e.g., acceleration, ultrasound, mass sensing) and quantum information science (e.g., quantum memory, quantum frequency conversion) where the resonance frequencies of interest are often in the microwave range and in excess of 1 GHz. A microwave digital lock-in amplifier is needed for the measurement of microwave acoustic resonators that are being developed for real-time mass measurements within material deposition processes, among several other applications. The system must be able to operate at frequencies up to 1.8 GHz, be capable of measuring the amplitude and phase of signals at these frequencies, and provide a phase locked loop with proportional-integral-derivative feedback control so that mechanical resonances can be tracked and characterized.

column17: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF STANDARDS AND TECHNOLOGY
column20: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF STANDARDS AND TECHNOLOGY
column29:
The NIST Material Measurement Laboratory, Applied Chemicals and Materials Division, Fatigue and Fracture Group (FFG) is
developing new metrology to measure the micromechanical properties, to fracture, of thin-film electroplated copper (Cu) and
micro-sized lead-free solder. These materials are critical as interconnects for heterogeneous integration for advanced packaging.
Cu is used for Cu-to-Cu hybrid bonding, the most advanced interconnect technology today and in the near future, while Sn-Ag-Cu
(SAC) solder is used for all levels of packaging. Mechanical property data, to fracture, are lacking or sparse for these materials with
size scales, chemical composition and microstructure similar to that of the interconnects, but are needed by the semiconductor
industry to improve their models. The characteristic length for Cu hybrid bonds is 1 micron, and that of solder can range from 1
micron to over 100 microns. A state-of-the-art technique is to use nanoindentation to measure mechanical properties, however,
nanoindentation tests are typically not to fracture, especially for ductile materials such as metals. NIST is therefore developing new
techniques to measure various mechanical properties (such as tensile, shear, bending, fatigue) of freestanding micro-sized metal
specimens with NIST-designed geometries, and requires a suitable mechanical testing instrument to load the micro specimens to
fracture, as well as to measure the force and displacement outputs of NIST’s Microelectromechanical Systems (MEMS)-based test
chips. Therefore, NIST requires a micromechanical testing instrument for two disparate functions: (a) materials testing such as micro
tensile testing, and (b) force calibration of MEMS actuators.

The NIST Communications Technology Laboratory, RF Technology Division, Guided Wave
Electromagnetics Group (GWEG) requires five 220 GHz on-wafer probes for a 220 GHz Anritsu
VectorStar. The probes will be used for the manufacturing of on-wafer standards for S-parameters and
Power to 330 GHz. The objective of the requirement is to provide known permittivity measurements
for on-wafer traceability for S-parameters, Power, and Phase. This requirement supports the NIST
CHIPS program objective to develop and disseminate NIST best practices and algorithms for complex
permittivity and on-wafer measurement. The required work provides probes for picking and placing
fused silica coupons for thickness and permittivity measurements.

Members of the public may view and comment on the draft waiver at: www.MadeInAmerica.gov (direct link to be inserted once published).

This Special Notice is not a Request for Proposal. However, the Government intends to issue a restricted solicitation to Stuewe & Sons, Inc.—the exclusive authorized U.S. distributor—for Styroblock seedling containers, concurrent with the administrative review of the Made‑in‑America waiver by the USDA Head of Contracting (HCA).

Award will not be made until the waiver is approved by the HCA. For BAA‑compliant transactions, this nonavailability waiver must be approved before contract execution.

column7: 6515 - MEDICAL AND SURGICAL INSTRUMENTS, EQUIPMENT, AND SUPPLIES
column29: This one-time requirement is for an optical biometer with Total Keratometry to serve as a secondary backup to the primary OCT at the Atlanta VA Hospital for the Ophthalmology Department. An optical biometer with Total Keratometry uses Swept Source Optical Coherence Tomography technology which offers comprehensive data to improve intraocular lens (IOL) selection and surgical outcomes by directly measuring the posterior corneal surface. By combining telecentric keratometry measurements of the anterior corneal surface with measurements of the posterior corneal surface simultaneously can calculate “total keratometry.” In a study against standard keratometry, it was determined that total keratometry “appeared to provide an accurate, objective measure of corneal power that closely tracked the refractive change in corneal refractive surgery,” as opposed to standard keratometry which “may lead to underestimation.” Not only does an optical biometer with Total Keratometry provide groundbreaking technology with respect to more accurate measurements, but it does also do so by measuring both eyes in less than 45 seconds with alignment assistance functions making each result largely independent of the user and therefore easier to delegate."

column5: Digital Transcutaneous Blood Gas Monitoring System
column7: 6515 - MEDICAL AND SURGICAL INSTRUMENTS, EQUIPMENT, AND SUPPLIES
column29:
The VA Northern California Health Care System (VANCHCS) has requested the procurement of 2 new Digital Transcutaneous Blood Gas Monitoring Systems. There is a strong need for transcutaneous monitoring in the management of Veterans on non-invasive respiratory support (particularly biPAP and high-flow nasal) at VANCHCS. Current trends show that carbon dioxide levels play a growing role in ventilation, outcomes, and cost management in hospitals. A Transcutaneous Blood Gas Monitoring System ensures proactive, real-time clinical decision-making for Veterans without the need for a more invasive Arterial Blood Gas Test. 

The Sentec tCOM+ Hospital Monitor in conjunction with the V-Sign Sensor 2 and accessories is a portable stand-alone Transcutaneous Blood Gas Monitoring System intended for continuous and noninvasive patient monitoring of carbon dioxide partial pressure, oxygen partial pressure, functional oxygen saturation, and pulse rate. The system is appropriate for use in adult, pediatric, and neonatal populations; it is both reusable and waterproof.

Sole source procurement for the acquisition of Calcium (Ca) Isotopes Ca-43 and Ca-44, which will be used in a clinical research project. The project, titled “Modulation of the gut microbiome by dietary fiber to improve calcium absorption and bone health in older men”, will investigate the effects of a dietary fiber supplement (soluble corn fiber) on intestinal calcium absorption in older male Veterans. In doing so, it will explore the role of the gut microbiome in modulating the effects. Male Veterans over the age of 60 will be enrolled in a double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover study to receive soluble corn fiber supplements and a placebo sequentially, with a washout period. The primary outcome is the measurement of intestinal fractional calcium absorption. The gold-standard for measuring intestinal fractional calcium absorption is using a dual stable calcium isotopic tracer method, where participants are given Ca-44 orally and Ca-43 intravenously simultaneously. The project will contribute significantly to shaping the clinical care of older male Veterans, inform recommendations for dietary fiber intake and bone health, and guide future scientific investigations into the relationship between the gut microbiome, nutrient handling, and bone biology. Ultimately, the knowledge gained may decrease the risk of painful and debilitating fractures among older Veterans.

column23:
This waiver fulfills the urgent national security mandate outlined in the Presidential Memorandum, “Construction of Arctic Security Cutters,” dated October 8, 2025. The current Coast Guard (CG) icebreaker fleet is insufficient to control, secure, and defend U.S. territories and address emerging threats in the Arctic. In response, the President directed a comprehensive effort to restore America’s maritime dominance, including rebuilding the nation’s maritime manufacturing capabilities. To meet immediate national security priorities, the President directed the CG to construct up to four ASCs abroad by temporarily waiving statutory requirements under 14 U.S.C. § 1151(b) and 10 U.S.C. § 8679(b) for four years. These statutes generally prohibit the construction of CG vessels or major components in foreign shipyards absent a Presidential waiver. The waiver bridges a critical capability gap in the U.S. shipbuilding industrial base. Concurrently, the President directed CG to develop a plan to transition future production to domestic shipyards and to build additional ASCs to meet long-term national security requirements. 

On October 9, 2025, the President signed a Memorandum of Understanding with the Republic of Finland recognizing Finland’s expertise in icebreaker construction and establishing a collaboration to build up to four ASCs in Finnish shipyards. This partnership is designed to accelerate delivery while transferring icebreaker construction expertise and knowledge to the U.S. These actions support Executive Order 14269, Restoring America’s Maritime Dominance. Section 18 of this Order directs agencies to develop a strategy to secure Arctic waterways, promote American prosperity, expand participation in the U.S. shipbuilding industrial base, and pursue acquisition reforms that foster innovation and reduce delays. In support of these objectives, the CG’s plan includes a trilateral “Icebreaker Collaboration Effort” with Canada and Finland to transfer icebreaker construction expertise to the U.S. industrial base.
column29:
Arctic Security Cutters (ASCs): These vessels will control, secure, and defend U.S. borders and maritime priorities, facilitate and secure commerce that is vital to economic prosperity; and ensure the United States Coast Guard and the Nation are prepared to respond to crises or contingencies in the region. This waiver includes components, systems, and materials that are integral to the baseline Canadian Multi-Purpose Icebreaker (MPI) and Finland Multi-Purpose Polar Ship (MPPS)-100 designs. These designs will be utilized to procure four (4) ASCs — two (2) each from Rauma Marine Constructions (Rauma, Finland) and Davie Defense/Helsinki Shipyard (Helsinki, Finland). Additionally, these designs will be used for domestic shipbuilding and will include the same foreign sourced components. The items covered by this waiver are highly specialized and integrated systems that collectively define the vessel’s icebreaking and operational capabilities and cannot be easily substituted. Key categories of foreign-sourced items covered by this waiver include, but are not limited to: 
• Propulsion and Maneuvering Systems: Core icebreaking components such as main diesel-electric engines, azimuthing thrusters, ice-strengthened propellers, and associated power generation and management systems. These are sourced from a limited, specialized global market and are central to the proven MPI design. 
• Navigation and Mission Systems: Integrated bridge systems, dynamic positioning equipment, advanced sonar and radar arrays, and other critical electronics pre-integrated into the parent design. 
• Specialized Structural Materials: Specific grades of high-tensile, low-temperature steel and other alloys required for the icebreaking hull and superstructure, which may not be available domestically in the required specifications or timeframe. 
• Auxiliary and Deck Machinery: Heavy-duty cranes, specialized scientific and towing winches, and anchoring systems designed for extreme Arctic conditions. 

Additionally, sample specifications requested by Rauma Marine Constructions and Davie Defense/Helsinki Shipyard include but are not limited to: MPI/MPPS vessel specifications, general and topside arrangements, lines, offsets, hull appendage drawings, seakeeping and icebreaking analysis, weight reports, main propulsion, electric and auxiliary systems, and navigation and communications system architecture.

Remote Oil Watcher (ROW) – Oil Detector: Non-contact petroleum sensor for installation in the N2 wet well at the Wastewater Treatment Plant. Provides real-time monitoring and alarming capabilities, eliminating the need for extensive maintenance and reducing the risk of contamination compared to traditional sampling methods.

Salient Features: Optical non-contact sensor for detection of oils & Hydrocarbon-based fluids using UV LED; Power Supply: 12VDC; Range to 8m directly above surface; Operating Temp: -30oC to +60oC; Automated Detection Sequence every 1-20Hz (user selectable); Adjustable alarm boundaries; and RS-485 ModBus Output.

NASA Glenn Research Center (GRC) On-
Site Fabrication of Extreme Environment-Durable integrated circuits (ICs). GRC plays a pivotal
role in advancing the capabilities of planned NASA missions, including Venus landers, through
the on-site fabrication of ICs capable of withstanding extreme environments. These ICs are
fabricated on 4H-SiC wafers with custom epitaxial layers, which are essential to meet the
stringent requirements of these missions. The epi-layered wafers used in IC fabrication are
sourced externally. In this instance, the project requires 100mm diameter wafers, with the
substrate manufactured in the United States and the custom epitaxial layers manufactured in
Sweden. Until a few years ago, GRC sourced these custom epi-layered wafers domestically from
Wolfspeed. However, Wolfspeed discontinued production of custom epi-layered wafers and is
only now restarting production under the Creating Helpful Incentives to Produce Semiconductors
(CHIPS) for America Act. Unfortunately, these wafers will not be available for at least six
months. The immediate availability of these wafers is critical to the success of ongoing projects
under The Hot Operating Temperature Technology (HOTTech) 21 program. The inability to
procure these wafers in a timely manner could significantly hinder the progress and completion
of the program’s core deliverables.
NASA’s Glenn Research Center (GRC) plays a crucial role in the HOTTech 21 program, a
research initiative aimed at developing and demonstrating advanced technologies capable of
operating in extreme high-temperature environments. Here's how NASA GRC ties into this
program:
 NASA GRC specializes in developing materials, electronics, and systems designed to
endure extreme conditions, such as high temperatures and pressures found on planetary
surfaces like Venus.
 HOTTech leverages GRC's expertise in thermal management and durable materials for
harsh environments.
 GRC houses state-of-the-art facilities to test components and systems under simulated
conditions, such as the high temperatures and corrosive atmospheres anticipated on
Venus.
 These facilities help validate HOTTech components for use in missions targeting extreme
environments.
 GRC contributes by advancing high-temperature materials (e.g., ceramics and
superalloys) and electronics that can function at temperatures exceeding 450°C.
 This innovation ensures HOTTech systems remain operational on missions where
conventional materials and electronics would fail.
 The HOTTech program aligns with NASA GRC's role in enabling missions to Venus and
other extreme environments, such as Jupiter’s moon Io or Mercury. These missions
require technologies that can withstand and operate efficiently in extreme heat.
 GRC collaborates with other NASA centers, industry partners, and academic institutions
involved in HOTTech. It ensures that the developed technologies are integrated and
tested for real-world applications.
 By advancing the technology readiness levels (TRLs) of high-temperature components,
GRC helps ensure the success of future NASA missions to some of the harshest
environments in our solar system.
The success of the project is heavily dependent on the fabrication of custom circuits using
specialized epi-layered wafers. Without the ability to procure these wafers, the team will face
significant challenges in meeting the core deliverables, potentially jeopardizing the completion
of the project’s objectives.

column5: Aanderaa Oceanographic Current Meter - National Marine Fisheries Service
column6: 334519 - Other Measuring and Controlling Device Manufacturing
column29:
The government requires an oceanographic recording current meter to track populations of porpoises and dolphins as a part of the National Marine Fisheries Service
mandate to study populations of protected marine mammals. The Aanderaa Seaguard II recording current meter uses Z-pulse multi-frequency Doppler current sensor technology to increase sampling speed while reducing power consumption. This means the unit can be deployed for up to one (1) year in the marine environment recording ocean conditions and storing the data for future recovery. No other recording current meter has the capability to last a full year, which is essential for our
mission. The variants measured are average ocean currents (speed and direction), temperatures, dissolved oxygen levels, and water pressure levels at multiple depths. Researchers use the data to ascertain optimal conditions for odontocete populations’ survival and reproduction. This recording current meter is not produced or manufactured in the United States in sufficient and reasonable
available commercial quantities of a satisfactory quality.

The Southeast Fisheries Science Center (SEFSC) urgently requires two Yamaha Outboard Engines, models F150XB and LF150XB, with controls, to replace the existing engines on the R/V Whaler, a small boat based in Pascagoula, Mississippi. The R/V Whaler is vital for conducting marine mammal abundance surveys and collecting biopsy samples for stock structure studies in inshore and coastal waters, supporting the Marine Mammal Protection Act and multiple Deepwater Horizon Restoration (DWH) project objectives. Keeping the vessel fully operational is critical to these missions.The current engines, Yamaha F150XCA models, have accumulated over 900 hours, primarily from intensive fieldwork for the DWH Alabama Estuarine Dolphin Population and Health Restoration Project over three years. Although these engines retain roughly half their serviceable hours, replacing them with updated Yamaha F150XB and LF150XB models ensures seamless compatibility with existing systems. These new engines are interchangeable with the current ones, allowing a single engine swap in case of a malfunction, which minimizes downtime compared to replacing both engines and controls with those from another manufacturer. Switching brands would also waste the remaining serviceable hours of the current engines.Yamaha is a leading brand in the U.S. outboard motor market and the standard for most NOAA Fisheries outboards, offering proven reliability and performance. However, these engines are not manufactured in the United States in sufficient commercial quantities or quality. Yamaha marine outboards are produced in Japan, primarily at the Fukuroi South Factory in Shizuoka Prefecture and Yamaha Kumamoto Products Co., Ltd. in Kumamoto Prefecture.The urgency of this need stems from the necessity to keep the R/V Whaler operational for critical research missions. Any delays or equipment failures could disrupt data collection and compliance with federal requirements. Acquiring these specific Yamaha engines and controls ensures operational continuity, efficiency, and minimal disruption to ongoing and future marine mammal research efforts.

The Government anticipates sole source award of a Firm-Fixed-Price Purchase Order to FLYHT, Inc for WVSS-II sensors to enhance the National Weather Service (NWS), Office of Observations mission of acquiring upper-air data that is essential for weather forecasts, research, and climatology.
The WVSS-II sensors shall be installed on existing commercial aircraft to increase meteorological data available to the NWS. The WVSS-II sensors provide vertical profiles of wind speed and direction, temperature, pressure, and related meteorological variables at high vertical resolution in the atmosphere from takeoff to cruise level, and multiple vertical heights, similar to radiosondes, adding to the existing aircraft data. The WVSS-II sensor uses tunable diode laser absorption spectroscopy technologies to measure water vapor concentrations along the aircraft flight path, with data communications to the ground by commercial carriers using existing aircraft communications pathways. FLYHT, Inc. is the exclusive distributor and manufacturer of the WVSS-II. Due to its proprietary nature, the WVSS-II is the only water vapor sensor approved by the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) to be installed on commercial aircraft to mirror the capabilities of radiosondes (weather balloons). There are no known alternate sensors feasible due to the proprietary nature of the WVSS-II.

The GML requires a UV Spectroradiometer for continuing and enhancing the long-term monitoring of ozone into the future. The Gigahertz-Optik BTS-Solar-1901
UV Spectroradiometer is the only known product capable of meeting the needs of the GML and the instrument is not manufactured or produced in sufficient and reasonably available commercial quantities of a satisfactory quality in the US. The spectroradiometer must be capable of measuring solar UV light with spectral resolution that can be used to derive total column ozone. It also must measure spectral irradiance, irradiance, peak wavelength, and center wavelength with a minimum of 2048 pixels with a pixel resolution of not more than 0.14nm/Pixel; Temporal resolution or measurement time of 0.1-6000ms; and integration times ranging from 2μs up to 60s. The instrument must show that it can achieve (with more than one peer reviewed article) an overall mean bias of measured total ozone not more than 1% when compared to fully calibrated Dobson or Brewer spectrophotometers.

column6: 334519 - Other Measuring and Controlling Device Manufacturing
column29:
The GML requires radiosondes to be used during launches of balloonborne ozonesonde and water vapor/aerosol instruments. These radiosondes are not manufactured
or produced in sufficient and reasonably available commercial quantities of a satisfactory quality in the US. The InterMet IMET-54-AS is the only radiosonde that meets the Government requirements. The IMET-54-AS radiosonde interfaces with the ozonesonde, water vapor, and aerosol instruments and measures air temperature with a small glass bead thermistor. Its small size minimizes effects caused by long and short-wave radiation and ensures fast response times. The humidity sensor is a thin-film capacitive polymer that responds directly to relative humidity. Radiosonde height is provided by the integrated Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)  receiver, and pressure is derived from the reported altitude, combined with the temperature and humidity readings. Wind speed and direction reporting during the sounding is derived from the GNSS position information, with processing algorithms ensuring that wind vectors are not affected by swinging of the radiosonde
beneath the balloon. Packet transmission uses error correction techniques to ensure reliable, accurate data reception for all expected link conditions.  

The InterMet IMET-54-AS includes the following components/specifications and is the only known product capable of meeting the needs of the GML.  The GML conducts balloon launches from up to nine (9) sites around the world, launching three to four ozonesondes per month. A necessary component of the ozonesonde instrument is the meteorological radiosonde that interfaces with the unique ozonesonde electronics board to merge and transmit data during flight to the receiving station running SkySonde telemetry software. The InterMet IMET-54-AS is the only method for obtaining high-resolution vertical profile measurements with necessary meteorological data from the surface through the stratospheric ozone layer to eventual balloon-burst at approximately 20 miles altitude

The Pacific Islands Fisheries Science Center requires the procurement of a Photosynthesis Yield Analyzer System. This instrument will enable NOAA scientists to generate critical data on the thermal tolerance of Endangered Species Act (ESA)-listed coral species, directly supporting the Coral Reef Conservation Program’s mission and NOAA’s statutory responsibilities under the Endangered Species Act.  

This instrument is not produced or manufactured in the US in sufficient and reasonably available quantities or of satisfactory quality.

Market research was conducted to identify potential sources for a specialized photosynthesis yield analyzer capable of measuring sub-lethal stress in ESA-listed corals. The team reviewed multiple procurement platforms including GSA Advantage, Grainger, NOAA internal acquisition tools (e.g., DOC ES Vehicles, ProTech), and other GSA-approved vendors. The MINI-PAM-II by Walz GmbH was consistently identified as the standard instrument for coral photophysiology, and no equivalent commercial systems were found that meet NOAA’s scientific requirements for precision, underwater deployment, and compatibility with both ongoing research protocols and existing equipment.

The instrument is not listed on any NOAA, DOC, or government-wide contract vehicles such as GSA, NASA SEWP, or NOAALink. No vendors on those platforms offered equivalent or comparable systems for this scientific application. Bay Instruments is the only authorized U.S. distributor of the MINI-PAM-II system. The manufacturer does not sell directly to U.S. government entities, nor are other authorized domestic resellers available, per correspondence with Heinz Walz GmbH. Procurement through other channels would violate distributor agreements and potentially void warranties or support services.

column5: PromethION Flow Cell Packs and Ligation Sequencing Kits
column29:
The NOAA, NMFS, Pacific Islands Fisheries Science Center (PIFSC) requires the procurement of PromethION Flow Cell packs and ligation sequencing kits for the development of a mission-critical molecular tool to genetically sequence environmental and wildlife tissue samples.  This procurement will support the development of a novel molecular technique (DNA methylation sex-markers) to identify the sex of hatchling sea turtles; this groundbreaking scientific research will allow for a non-invasive, large-scale, rapid, and real-time assessment of live hatchling sex ratios of all NMFS-managed green sea turtle populations for the first time ever. Since the early 1980s, the sea turtle research community has tried and failed to identify a method to determine sea turtle sex because sea turtle hatchling sex is impossible to determine non-lethally and their sex cannot be determined externally until maturity (as sea turtles do not have sex chromosomes). The resulting data and establishment of a viable molecular technique to determine sea turtle hatchling sex can be transferable to all sea turtle species and could substantially accelerate management decisions. The Government currently owns Nanopore sequencers, therefore all Flow Cells and kits will need to be compatible with existing equipment.

The purchase will include 56 PromethION Flow Cells, which contain up to 2,675 Nanopore channels, and associated kits which will be used to sequence DNA. The PromethION Flow Cell Packs and ligation sequencing kits are not produced or manufactured in the United States in sufficient and reasonable available commercial quantities of a satisfactory quality.

column5: Rigid Inflatable Boat (RIB), 13-Foot Class with center console for steering and trailer
column17: FARM PRODUCTION AND CONSERVATION BUSINESS CENTER
column29:
To gauge industry interest and availability, a Request for Information (RFI) was posted to SAM.gov on May 13, 2025, for a period of eight calendar days. The Government received one response from Zodiac. Research indicated that Zodiac and Fluid Watercraft could potentially meet the technical requirements. Other manufacturers such as Yamaha and Highfield Boats were identified as capable of meeting the specifications but were not domestically manufactured.
The findings concluded that the required RIB is unlikely to be sourced through GSA Schedule vendors due to its unique size and features. While a few vessels were identified that could potentially meet the 55 percent domestic content threshold under FAR 52.225-2 Buy American Certificate, no vendor proposed a technically acceptable domestically manufactured vessel during the competitive Request for Quote (RFQ) process.
To promote competition, a combined synopsis/solicitation was posted on SAM.gov on July 1, 2025, set aside 100 percent for small businesses. The solicitation required compliance with FAR 52.225-1 Buy American – Supplies and FAR 52.225-2 Buy American Certificate. The basis for award was Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA), with an award to be made to the vendor who met all minimum technical requirements at the lowest price. The solicitation closed on July 14, 2025, and twelve vendor responses were received. Proposed vessels included the Highfield CL 380 Hypalon, Fluid Watercraft 13 Swift Water Rescue, Gala A400HL, and Ribcraft 4.8 Professional.
Among the twelve responses, Fluid Watercraft was the only vendor that met the domestic manufacturing requirement. However, the proposed 16-foot vessel exceeded the specified size and was unsuitable for accessing coastal lagoons. The Fluid 13 Swift Water Rescue model did not meet the technical specifications, as it lacked a center console and was designed for tiller steering, making it inappropriate for the intended use.
Based on the results of this comprehensive market research and competitive solicitation, it was determined that no technically acceptable, domestically manufactured RIB meeting the 13-foot class and center console steering requirement is currently available.

column5: Cable and Wire for Research Trawl Nets
column29:
The NOAA Fisheries AFSC Resource Assessment and Conservation Engineering (RACE) Division, located in Seattle, Washington, has a requirement to purchase cable and wire to construct and repair trawls in preparation for surveys and research conducted in Alaskan waters. The AFSC conducts five distinct bottom trawl surveys in Alaska; some are performed annually and some biennially under the Magnuson–Stevens Fishery Conservation and Management Act (2007), 16 U.S.C. 1881 MSA § 401. The data collected on these fishery-independent surveys are integral to the stock assessments of all commercial fish and crab species in Alaska, and successful completion of these surveys is a core function of the AFSC. This requires both fishery-independent surveys and scientific research fieldwork in which trawls are often used. The trawls suffer damage and wear during these activities and need repair and eventual replacement to continue operating as designed. In order for the trawl surveys to provide reliable and unbiased information, the fishing gear and methods are strictly standardized so that observations are consistent and unbiased from location to location and from year to year so that the change in materials or methods does not bias abundance estimates. A variety of materials are used to construct and repair these trawls, including wire rope, cable, and assorted hardware. These materials must be the same from year to year, and the specifications must remain the same over time to ensure standardization. The AFSC RACE Research Fishing Gear Program is responsible for the construction and repair of all trawls used for both survey and research purposes. 
There are a variety of trawl cable and wire types that all have different characteristics to meet the requirements of the survey or research to be completed. Cable and wire are manufactured with different number of strands, cores, lubrication, metals and tracers. The cable and wire selected for the AFSC trawl nets must be compatible with the Program Office cable press having the capability of pressing swages up to 5/8” wire diameter, must meet certification procedures by minimal length tolerances, material stretching tolerances, and interchangeable with existing trawl inventory, and must meet the same specifications as existing cable and wire to minimize any differences in drag, turbulence, and the vertical or horizontal opening of the trawl. The required trawl cable and wire are not produced or manufactured in the United States in sufficient and reasonable available commercial quantities of a satisfactory quality. REQUIRED: 3/4" x 60 ft W/ Rein Thimble eyes both ends no tag; 3/4" x 40 ft W/ Rein Thimble eyes both ends no tag; and 5/8" 6x19 Galv Galvanized Independent Wire Rope Right Regular Lay lube dry Imported
cable.

column29: The National Institute of Standards and Technology, Materials Measurement Laboratory, Biomolecular Measurement Division (BMD) requires a Raman spectrometer capable of performing rapid in-line upstream and on-line downstream Raman measurements of mammalian cell cultures and protein biologics. The MSED conducts research on the development of polyolefin Standard Reference Materials (SRMs), which requires measurement of the polymers’ molar mass, molar mass distribution, and branching content. To achieve this, a high-temperature gel permeation chromatography (HT-GPC) instrument was previously purchased from Polymer Char, model GPC-IR. The GPC-IR instrument was purchased from Polymer Char in fiscal year (FY) 2013 to analyze, extend, and recertify polyolefin molar mass standards. At the time, the GPC-IR was a state-of-the-art detection device capable of measuring real-time concentration of methyl and alkyl CH stretching of an eluting species with a resolution within ten methyl (CH3)/ 1000 total Carbons. As NIST needs to maintain state-of-the-art capabilities for standards certifications, texting, and polymer separations research, we seek to replace our current GPC-IR with a new HT-GPC to improve the detection capability of low levels of short-chain branching, along with replacing legacy equipment, where parts and service will soon become unavailable from the vendor due to the age of the instrument. The new instrument must be compatible with an existing external high-temperature light scattering detector currently coupled to the legacy GPC-IR instrument, which is required for absolute weight average molar mass measurements and determination of polymer conformation plots. Additionally, sample preparation requirements for our GPC-IR system now include analysis of commercial and recycled polyolefins, which contain fillers, colorants, and other contaminants that can damage the GPC-IR system components. The ability to dissolve samples at high temperatures, use a hot filter to remove high concentrations of pollutants, and safely handle solvents is not a current capability on our legacy GPC-IR instrument, but the development of commercial sample preparation systems from instrument manufacturers has recently made filtration and safe solvent handling possible.

It is made completely of carbon fiber, including the ball bearing within the base plate of the rotation unit, and is designed to easily attach to the existing Varian TrueBeam Linac system already in use at the Puget Sound VAHCS Radiology Department. column29: The Alaska Fisheries Science Center(AKFSC) requires trawl materials and supplies that that will be used for survey trawl repairs and to construct trawls in preparation for surveys and research conducted in Alaskan waters. A variety of materials are used to construct and repair these trawls, including wire rope, floats, Poly web, and assorted hardware. These materials must be the same from year to year, and the specifications must remain the same over time to ensure standardization. The AKFSCRACE Division Research Fishing Gear Program is responsible for the construction and repair of all trawls used for both survey and research purposes. There are a variety of trawl types that all have different characteristics to meet the requirements of the survey for research to be completed. Rope and twine looming techniques are held to secrecy and patented, where each manufacturer produces the specific fibers, natural or synthetic, used for their product. The rope and twine fibers are twisted and braided together to create a specific tensile strength. The specific fibers used and the tensile strength applied by each manufacturer is distinct and unique. The rope and twine selected for the AKFSC trawl nets must support a certain amount of stress when stretched. The constructed trawl net receives considerable impact and force when fi shing on the sea floor, where the average catches within the trawl net is approximately 1,100 -1,200 kg. It is imperative that the rope and twine is manufactured to withstand the stress and weight of both the bottom fishing and the resultant catch. column29: The National Institute of Standards & Technology, Material Measurement Laboratory, Material Science Engineering Division , Mechanical Performance Group (MPG) is responsible for maintaining the national scale realizations used for hardness measurements traceability. Hardness testing is of prime importance to industry as it enables the estimation of a material’s ability to resist deformation, wear, and durability. During the course of hardness testing, an indenter is typically impelled into the material under test, using a prescribed amount of force for a specified length of time, and subsequently estimating the amount of deformation by measuring the distance between opposing edges of the created indentation. Traditionally, optical microscopy has been used to quantify the size of the deformation; however, it is now recognized that this method is susceptible to errors caused by operator subjectivity, material reflectivity, varying lighting conditions, and the challenge of the detection of the edge of the indentation. On the other hand, advanced surface profilometric techniques have proven to overcome many of these deficiencies, yielding higher accuracy, and improved repeatability of the measurements. The MPG requires a stylus profilometer system to be used for characterization of the surface of indentations made for hardness testing, and the geometry of hardness testing indenters.

column5: TeleFiOpto Wireless Fiber Photometry System Equipment
column29: The goal of the National Institute on Alcohol Abuse and Alcoholism (NIAAA), Section on Neural Circuits (SNC) is to understand how genetic and environmental insults affect the computation of neural signals within brain networks to cause neurodevelopmental disorders like autism, epilepsy and attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder. The SNC requires TeleFiOpto wireless fiber photometry system equipment. This is critical for their research, which aims to understand how the brain functions and how neurodevelopmental disorders disrupt neural activity. These studies often require precise manipulation and recording of brain activity to examine its impact on behavior and symptoms, and the requested TeleFiOpto system enables high-quality, wireless, real-time control and monitoring of neuronal activity with minimal noise and artifacts. Specifically, the flexibility and adaptability of this device improves the scientific rigor, innovation potential, and translational relevance of the lab's research.

The mission of the NIAAA Section on Fibrotic Disorders (SFD) lab is to understand the cellular and molecular mechanisms involved in the development and progression of fibrosis, to explore novel therapeutic targets and to develop effective pharmacotherapies for fibrotic disorders of different etiologies, including alcohol use disorders. We are particularly interested in pursuing a multi-target therapeutic approach to improve treatment efficacy by simultaneously engaging multiple pathogenic pathways in fibrotic disorders. The NIAAA SFD has been using the Beckman Coulter Inc CytoFLEX Flow Cytometer instrument for immuno phenotypic measurements in the lab. This instrument is used daily by multiple lab members at the NIAAA. Currently, this instrument measures biological samples one by one by single vial format. Due to its frequent use supporting multiple research projects, we need to add the Beckman Coulter Inc brand autosampler/plate loader unit in order to improve the efficiency of our research. This requirement is for a Cytoflex Plate Loader, which is a brand-new part for our current cytoflex flow cytometer instrument, which is produced by Beckman Coulter. They will provide a certified service engineer to properly install the new part and make a compatible configuration. Specifically, this item will allow our staff to load samples in 96 well plate format, a process which takes about 15 minutes. Currently, staff load samples into the instrument manually, a process which takes around 5 hours to load 24 samples. Therefore, this upgrade significantly improves efficiency and makes this instrument more user friendly, which will benefit our research mission.

column29: The National Institute of Standards and Technology, Material Measurement Laboratory Chemical Sciences Division (CSD) is involved in the Innovations in Measurement Science (IMS) Project seeking to deliver a testbed for heterogeneous catalysts based on emerging X-ray characterization methods as a key component of the IMS. The IMS projects are competitively chosen for their potential for high impact on a scientific, engineering, and/or technological challenge that benefits society.  They impact stakeholders at NIST, industry, commercial labs, other federal agencies, or academia. These capabilities are being commissioned at NIST’s X-ray facilities at the National Synchrotron Light Source II and involve the integration of a NIST-built detector to enable a first-of-kind measurement approach. The UV-Vis spectrometer will play a key role in the project by probing d-electron chemistry and screening samples prior to measurement at the X-ray facility.

column29: To provide fast digitization of radiofrequency signals, the Photonics and Optomechanics Group, Microsystems and Nanotechnology Division, NIST require a high-speed digitizer. The digitizer will enable direct frequency comb spectroscopy of atomic gases, molecular gases, and optomechanical sensors. To provide the necessary measurement bandwidth, the digitizer needs to have a sampling rate of ≥ 10 Gsamples/s and an analog bandwidth of ≥ 4.5 GHz when operated with a single input channel. The digitizer must have at least 12 bits of digitization resolution, include at least 2 input channels, include an input for an external reference clock at 10 MHz, and include onboard memory that is capable of storing ≥ 8 GSamples at full resolution. The digitizer must be compatible with a PCI Express form factor to allow it to be incorporated directly in a desktop computer already owned by NIST. In addition, the digitizer must include a software development kit (SDK) to allow for streaming the digitizer directly to a graphics processing unit (GPU).

column29: The National Institute of Standards and Technology, Engineering Laboratory, Intelligent Systems Division, Production Systems Group (PSG), conducts foundational research in metals-based additive manufacturing (AM) to accelerate innovation and support rapid design-to-product transformation through advances in measurement science. As part of its ongoing expansion of AM research capabilities, PSG requires a Mid-Size Rotary Riffler system for precise and reproducible sampling of metal powders. Rotary spinning riffler systems are essential for producing representative sub-samples of dry powders, which are subsequently used to evaluate powder flowability, spreadability, and other properties critical to AM processes. Powder is fed via a vibratory feeder into a rotating divider head, which evenly distributes the material into multiple collection bottles, ensuring high reproducibility across tests. The PSG seeks to procure a system capable of dividing approximately 2000 cm³ of dry powder into 10 discrete samples, each collected in separate bottles. This capability is vital for supporting interlaboratory studies, validating AM process inputs, and improving the repeatability and reproducibility of powder-based measurements. Ancillary equipment and installation at the NIST Gaithersburg, Maryland campus are included in this requirement. This acquisition directly supports NIST’s mission to address critical measurement challenges in metal AM and ensures that PSG remains a leader in the development of standardized testing methodologies for powder characterization, thereby contributing to the broader advancement of U.S. manufacturing competitiveness in the rapidly growing AM sector.

column29: The National Institute of Standards and Technology, Engineering Laboratory, Fire Research Division, Flammability Reduction Group (FRG), conducts research to reduce fire hazards in building materials by developing measurement science and supporting fire safety standards. A critical tool in this work is the Simultaneous Thermal Analyzer (STA), which performs Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA) and Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) simultaneously. This allows precise measurement of material decomposition behavior, including mass loss, heat capacity, and reaction kinetics under controlled thermal and environmental conditions. To improve performance, NIST is acquiring a platinum furnace and TG-DSC sample carrier upgrade compatible with its existing STA system. The new furnace offers a sixfold improvement in measurement reproducibility and is expected to reduce testing time by half, significantly enhancing experimental throughput and data accuracy. This upgrade is essential to advancing NIST’s work in fire safety science, supporting the development of reliable, performance-based standards for fire-safe materials, and ensuring continued leadership in the field of fire research and standardization.

column29: The National Institute of Standards and Technology, Engineering Laboratory, Materials and Structural Systems Division (MSSD) conducts advanced research on the durability and service life of infrastructure materials, including polymers, concrete, and engineered composites, in support of resilient infrastructure applications. A core component of this research involves the evaluation of mechanical properties to assess both baseline material performance and degradation resulting from environmental or loading influences. As part of this mission, the Additive Manufacturing with Cement-based Materials project aims to advance measurement science and the scientific foundation necessary for the development of performance-based standards for reinforced, 3D-printed concrete structures. This work supports the construction industry’s shift toward innovative, automated, and sustainable building technologies. To further these objectives, NIST intends to procure a volumetric cementitious mix-pump system with advanced capabilities that exceed those of the currently owned unit. The required system must enable increased precision in the mixing and delivery of cementitious materials, higher throughput rates, and greater consistency in output—features essential for scaling research in additive construction. This acquisition is critical to enabling expanded experimental capacity, improving the reproducibility of test results, and ensuring that NIST remains at the forefront of standardization efforts in the rapidly evolving field of 3D concrete printing.

The National Institute of Standards & Technology, Materials Measurement Laboratory, Facility Adsorbent Characterization & Testing (FACT) Laboratory within the Chemical Sciences Division (CSD) aims to serve the sorbent materials research community as an impartial source for testing and characterization of sorbent materials via the establishment of reference materials and testing procedures. The Laboratory seeks to contribute to gas separation efforts by developing reference materials for dynamic adsorption measurements to enable the creation of standard measurement procedures and reference data. The performance of an adsorbent is typically determined by measuring an adsorption isotherm, a plot of gas uptake as a function of equilibrium pressure at a fixed temperature. To address the challenge of reproducibility in these measurements, the FACT Lab has sponsored international, interlaboratory studies to
develop reference isotherms using NIST reference zeolitic materials. The FACT Laboratory is a state-of-the-art laboratory with support from the U.S. Department of Energy's Advanced Research Projects-Energy (ARPA-E) to address the challenges inherent to measuring gas and vapor sorption properties. A breakthrough analyzer will permit the evaluation of differential adsorption properties of porous materials under dynamic conditions. Dynamic breakthrough data are especially useful for evaluating and selecting industrial sorbents by monitoring the uptake of multiple gases, including water vapor, which often competes with the adsorption of common gases.

column29:
NIDA IRP seeks to conduct behavioral experiments while calcium signals,
indicating neuronal activities, from the mouse brain are being recorded. To
understand behavioral functions of neural activity, we need a behavioral device
that measures conditional (operant and Pavlovian) behavioral responses and
neural activities from the same subject at the same time. The Mobile Home Cage
must be able to produce behavioral responses while being head-fixed for the twophoton
calcium-imaging recording.

The proposed acquisition of Mobile Home Cage must be incorporated into our
existing two-photon microscope system. The four major components of the twophoton
microscope are: Zeiss AxioExaminer D1 microscope, Cambridge
Technology Galvo optical scanner, Spectra-Physics Mai Tai HP laser, and a large
air table. This is a large instrument system, taking up a 12 square yard space, and
valued hundreds of thousands of dollars. This instrument allows little room to
modify its physical configuration, to accommodate other instruments. Therefore,
the dimension of behavioral instrument that is incorporated into the microscopic
system is restricted. The Mobile Home Cage is the only such instrument available
on the market.

column5: Portable Molecular Diagnostic Device - Genie III
column29: Customer reached out to the scientific community and found the Genie III and obtained an informal quote. Market research found similar devices but not equipped to service the science that was required. Posted a “Name Brand or Equal” (Sol: 1232SA25Q0090) SB to SAM.gov with limited options. Original solicitation requested SB for name brand or equal, no equal products were submitted. Solicited unrestricted (Sol: 1232SA25Q0090 Amd 001) and again received no equal products. It appears that the Genie III device is the only item that meets the specification requirements. The Genie III is the only known scientific device that is handheld suitable for field work that is capable of amplifying DNA and RNA in isothermal conditions with real-time fluorescence for sensitive and rapid detection of DNA and RNA viruses. Genei III features dual-channel fluorescence that enables simultaneous detection of two different viruses in each sample, which will be useful for detecting mixed viral infections in plants. Further, Genei III is a standalone device with internal memory for storing data and with export options. Genei III supports minimum sample processing time which is a requirement to maintain integrity of the samples after extracting. It is also the only known device that completes this without thermal cycling, which is critical due to the delicate nature of the samples. The Genie III is made in United Kingdom and sold by USA company.

column29: The National Institute of Standards and Technology, Material Measurement Laboratory, Materials Measurement Science Division,  Materials Structure and Data Group (MSDG) supports work with ceramic specimens printed using a vat-photopolymerization additive manufacturing processes containing large (on the order of 50 % by volume) amounts of organic material in addition to inorganic ceramic filler. To successfully process a functional, dense part, the organic material must be pyrolyzed using a careful thermal de-binding treatment. This organic burnout process must be tailored for the organic resin chemistry and dimensions of the printed part.  If the de-binding process progresses too rapidly, the organic material in the center of the part may try to escape too quickly and create cracks or defects in the part or, in worse case scenarios, cause the part to catastrophically fail. The de-binding process often takes on the order of days to complete, so optimizing this process prior to performing it on critical parts is necessary to save time and resources. The MSDG is requesting one Benchtop Thermogravimetric Analyzer (TGA)/Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) to support their research in ceramic additive manufacturing.  With the TGA/DSC, the complex de-binding profile can be optimized by measuring TGA curves on representative sized samples while the DSC function of the equipment is useful in measuring phase changes during processing of ceramics and interactions with reactive gas environments during the high temperature sintering portion of the processing. Reactive gases (e.g. N2/H2 forming gas environments) are often used in ceramics processing to stabilize desired phases or tune the oxygen stoichiometry for particular applications. The TGA/DSC equipment also controls the gas environment at elevated temperatures is necessary for ceramics research that covers a broad range of applications.

column29:
The EMSS Materials and Devices Branch FCDD-RLS-ED at the Army Research Laboratory (ARL) needs to advance its test capability to ten (10) kV UHV to enable characterization of wide bandgap and ultra-wide bandgap semiconductor power switches being developed in-house and through collaborative device development programs. Electrical characterization is critical to validating two (2) and three (3) dimensional device models and key material and performance metrics from test structures and fully fabricated in-house devices. The outcome of this work supports the development of high voltage power-switching devices that will enable Army and DoD capabilities for high efficiency and high power density power conversion systems. 

The Keysight Technologies Inc. ten (10) kV UHV expander is the only product in the commercial marketplace that provides the minimum blocking voltage requirements with  integration into the teams existing power analyzer framework previously purchased from Keysight Technologies Inc, safe operation with interlocked test fixture with ten (10) kV and one (1) kiloampere (kA) capabilities, and dedicated protection modules for lower voltage source measurement units integrated into the existing mainframe unit.  

The research that will be conducted using the required UHV ten (10) kV Expander and critical add-ons from Keysight Technologies, Inc. will allow the Army to fully characterize and evaluate power switching devices being developed under various Department of Defense (DoD) programs to be transitioned from lab-to-lab in support of the Warfighter. If the Government does not procure this system, ARL will have to find a collaborator with this capability which will slow electrical testing and analysis and impact the speed of development and the overall program. 

Due to all of the aforementioned details and based upon the market research performed, Keysight Technologies, Inc. is the only company that can satisfy the Government’s minimum requirements as they are the only source that allows for complete integration of the 10 kV UHV expander and critical add-on components into a one system solution.

column29: The National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST),  Superconductive Electronics Group (SEG), develops and disseminates Josephson Voltage Standard (JVS) systems as a primary reference for the SI volt. The compressors are essential components of the larger JVS system and are required to pair with Sumitomo RDK-205D2 coldheads to enable operation at 4 K. column29: The Fundamental Thermodynamics Group conducts fundamental and applied research to improve the measurement of thermodynamic quantities. Develops new measurement methods for pressure, vacuum, and temperature using atomic and molecular gases, refractometry, solid-state point defects, optomechanics, integrated photonics, advanced nanofabrication technologies, and advanced AI. The group continuously improves the performance of national standards, participates in national and international intercomparisons to validate these standards, and produces assessments of their respective measurement uncertainty. The group has recently begun developing primary thermometers based on measurements of the Doppler effect in atomic gases, specifically cesium and ytterbium. Accurately extracting the thermodynamic temperature from the Doppler absorption spectrum of a gas requires a laser system with very low frequency drift and low intensity noise. NIST requires a flexible digital laser servo that can both stabilize spectroscopy lasers to frequency references, such as vapor cells or optical cavities, using lock-in techniques and reduce optical intensity noise via proportional-integral-differential control. The digital laser servo must also provide instrumentation to test and characterize the intensity and frequency stabilization performance to ensure rigorous uncertainty analysis of the Doppler thermometer. This procurement is essential for meeting the goals of an IMS project.

The National Institute of Standards and Technology, Sensor Science Division, Fundamental
Thermodynamics Group (FTG) is conducting a Photonic Thermometry project. The main
focus of the project is to develop the next-generation chip-scale photonic temperature
metrology that can replace Standard Platinum Resistance Thermometers (SPRTs), reduce
the temperature calibration chain and sensor ownership cost. One of the essential
milestones of Photonic Thermometry is to develop robust and reliable photonic chip
packaging. The fiber-coupled chip must be hermetically sealed in various-size capsules as
well as in long-stem packaging modules. To ensure the reliability of photonic chip
packaging, the Fundamental Thermodynamics group has a critical need for one helium
leak detector that is fully dedicated to Photonic Thermometry project. The helium leak
detector is required to perform routine checks for possible vacuum leaks in packaged
thermometers and for troubleshooting of failed photonic packaging.

Our laboratory performs measurements and calibrations using cryogenic optical sources and detectors, and we operate a number of high vacuum chambers and cryostats for these experiments. The ultra-high vacuum (UHV) mobile pumping systems to be purchased will be used to evacuate experimental vacuum chambers required for the development of far-infrared sources, optical systems, and detectors. The National Institute of Standards and Technology, Material Measurement Laboratory, Biosystems and Biomaterials Division (BBD)
develops and provides methods, reference materials, and reference data for characterizing biological systems. To fulfill this mission,
robust characterization of microorganisms is required. The acquisition of an instrument to accurately and rapidly sequence DNA by next
generation sequencing (NGS) methods is required. This measurement capability is critical to ongoing and future work towards RNA- and
DNA-based reference material and measurement method development within BBD.

column17: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF STANDARDS AND TECHNOLOGY
column20: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF STANDARDS AND TECHNOLOGY
column29:
The National Institute of Standards and Technology’s, MaterialMeasurement Laboratory, Materials Science and Engineering Division (MSED) has a
requirement for a stable Bipolar Power Supply that addresses the nation's need for improvement in its semiconductor supply chain. The semiconductor
supply chain is global, specialized, and interconnected. Chipmakers do business with thousands of individual suppliers that provide the highly complex
materials and tools used to produce semiconductors. To address the lack of full visibility into the semiconductors markets supply chain and R&D ecosystem
gaps, NIST will conduct the measurement science, or metrology, critical to the development of new materials, packaging, and production methods in chip
manufacturing.
The MSED is involved in the development of standard reference materials (SRMs) for magnetic moment – a critical standard for measuring magnetic
materials for next generationmemory and computing. The SRMs are certified using the NIST-built Absolute Magnetometer, a unique custom instrument.
For SI-traceability, the magnetic force acting on the SRMs will be measured as a function of the applied magnetic field gradient. To produce an accurate
and stablemagnetic force, this work requires a very stable bipolar power supply. The power supply will be integrated into NIST’s Absolute Magnetometer
and must communicate with the control computer of the Absolute Magnetometer system.

column29: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) Physical Measurement Lab (PML), Sensor Science Division (SSD), requires a laser spectrum analyzer. The purpose of this acquisition is to procure a laser spectrum analyzer, a critical instrument required for the precise measurement and verification of wavelengths emitted by the tunable laser system. Accurate wavelength determination is essential for ensuring the reliability and traceability of calibration procedures, particularly when the laser is used to calibrate sensitive optical equipment such as spectroradiometers and photodetectors. By integrating a high-resolution spectrum analyzer into the calibration setup, the facility can confidently assess the spectral accuracy of the tunable laser, thereby enhancing the overall performance and credibility of its measurement services.

Commercial spectrographs are used in a variety of applications, wherever measurements of optical radiation can be interpreted in terms of physical properties. Major commercial applications include manufacturing, quality control, food safety, quantification of pharmaceuticals, material characterization, medical imaging, forensic analysis, and environmental safety. A stable, accurate, and well-characterized spectrograph is needed to hold the luminance scale and the spectral radiance scale from the ultraviolet (UV) to the near infrared (NIR). Based on previous experience within the Sensor Science Division we have established the specifications required for this Reference Spectrograph System. NIST requires a scientific-grade integrated lens, spectrograph, and camera system with the minimum requirements listed below to hold the spectral radiance scale of lamp or laser-illuminated integrating sphere sources from the UV to the NIR, e.g. using silicon-based detector technology.

column17: ANIMAL AND PLANT HEALTH INSPECTION SERVICE
column20: ANIMAL AND PLANT HEALTH INSPECTION SERVICE
column29:
Fermentation Nutrients Ingredients (FNI) 205 is a primary yeast product manufactured by Lallemand Bio-Ingredients USA, LLC (UEI: DVU8RZLRX4J9).
The FNI 205 Yeast is a crucial ingredient needed in the production of the Mexican fruit fly artificial diet for both the fertile adult fly used for both egg collection and for the irradiated sterile fly that is used for release in the quarantine areas. FNI 205 Yeast provides the much-needed protein to assist in the development of the fly.
There is currently no alternative diet ingredient for this product and without it available, this can cause a major work decrease in production and/or stoppage due to low egg production and collection. It is mission essential because it is needed in the production of the Sterile Insect Technique - Mexican fruit fly to protect the citrus industry from wild incursions of Anastrepha ludens.
The Mexican fruit fly, Anastrepha Ludens (Loew) is a destructive fruit fly that is native to Mexico and Central America but is not endemic to the United States. The Mexican fruit fly attacks more than 45 commercial varieties of fruits and vegetables as described in the revised federal host list. The United States Department of Agriculture is conducting an eradication program using sterilized Mexican fruit flies as a means of population control. Without such program the invasive fruit fly would flourish, decimate the citrus industry, and prohibit or restrict the movement of interstate commerce.

column5: Custom low-pass and high-pass optical filters for use in the far-infrared
column29: The Observational Cosmology Lab at Goddard Space Flight Research Center (GSFC) develops technologies and instruments for astrophysics and cosmology at far-infrared, sub-millimeter, and microwave wavelengths, including ultrasensitive, low-temperature, superconducting filters. Cryogenic test facilities with the ability to illuminate detectors with known amounts of radiation across a controlled optical bandwidth are required for validation of detector designs and verification that the filters’ performance meets mission requirements. Celtic Terahertz Technology is the only known vendor capable of producing custom low-pass and high-pass optical filters for use in the far-infrared. The custom filters being procured are critical for defining the optical passband of cryogenic testbeds. Without them the GSFC team risks the loss of critical testing capabilities for the development of filters for future astrophysics missions including Origins Space Telescope.

These electronics will be integrated into a laboratory apparatus at NIST built to inspect semiconductor integrated circuits and to collect infrared photons emitted when transistors switch their state. The goal of the project is to understand the emitted light so it can be used for circuit evaluation and failure analysis. By measuring the faint, infrared emission from active transistors, we can better understand how this light can be used for circuit evaluation and failure analysis, and therefore develop the capability in service of the US semiconductor industry.

A major component of these efforts relates to NIST’s work in the bioeconomy and biomanufacturing. The Overton Brooks VA Medical Center in Shreveport, LA, requires one (1) Anaerobic Workstation with accessories. column5: Ultra-High Pressure Liquid Chromatography Quadrupole Time of Flight Mass Spectrometer
column17: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF STANDARDS AND TECHNOLOGY
column29: The National Institute of Standards and Technology, Material Management Laboratory, Materials Measurement Science Division (MMSD) has significant research efforts in the fields of analytical chemistry and mass spectrometry, targeting the forensic science, public health, and homeland security communities. The Rapid Drug Analysis and Research (RaDAR) program provides comprehensive, near real-time analysis of drug residues collected from public safety and public health entities across the U.S. The core platform used to provide these measurements is high-resolution ambient ionization mass spectrometry (AI-MS). Over the last 12 months the RaDAR program has grown significantly and has begun to couple non-targeted qualitative analysis with quantitative analyses using liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (LC-MS). It has also expanded to include an effort to provide laboratory-grade measurements to community partners at point of need via a mobile testing laboratory. The mobile testing laboratory is a 17-foot box van that has been customized with the necessary infrastructure to allow for on-site analysis of drug residues. To further enable our ability to provide high-quality data in the field, we require a high-resolution mass spectrometry system that can provide both qualitative and quantitative analyses. Therefore, a liquid chromatography quadrupole time-of-flight mass spectrometry (LC-QTOF) system is required. For the LC-QTOF system to be able to be used within the mobile laboratory, it must meet specific size requirements. Specifically, the mobile laboratory has built-in benches that are 30” in depth and a maximum of 72” in length that the LC-QTOF must be able to fit on. Given the height of the benches relative to the ceiling, the height of the system cannot exceed 50”. Given gross vehicle weight restrictions, the weight of the entire LC-QTOF system cannot exceed 600 pounds. There is no space in the mobile laboratory for a floor-based instrument. In addition to these requirements, the LC-QTOF system must be able to couple with a direct analysis in real time (DART) ambient ionization source to enable rapid qualitative analysis using existing methodologies. It must have a resolving power of at least 30,000 full width half max that is independent of scan frequency or mass range and can be used in full scan or tandem (MS/MS) data acquisition to be able to differentiate structurally similar compounds. All necessary data analysis software and a computer must also be provided.

column17: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF STANDARDS AND TECHNOLOGY
column20: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF STANDARDS AND TECHNOLOGY
column29: The Physical Measurements Laboratory (PML), Time and Frequency Division (TFD), Neutral Atom Optical Clocks Group (NAOCG) is responsible for advancing and leading the frontiers of optical timekeeping. A major component of this is the Yb optical lattice clocks which utilize magic wavelengths to leverage 1000’s of neutral Yb atoms as atomic references for clock lasers.
