This procurement is for the purchase of a laser to explore this new magic wavelength.

Hematopoietic stem cell (HSC)-targeted gene therapy has emerged as a promising one-time cure for various genetic disorders, such as sickle cell disease (SCD). Traditionally, successful HSC gene therapy trials have relied on ex vivo HSC culture for lentiviral gene addition and CRISPR gene editing, presenting limitations for widespread application. Recent advancements have shown that systemic injection of lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) enables in vivo gene editing in the liver, yet achieving tissue-specific gene delivery is essential for developing in vivo HSC gene therapies. Consequently, The Cellular and Molecular Therapeutics Laboratory is working on an HSC-targeted gene delivery system using lentiviral vectors (LVs) and LNPs to propel in vivo HSC-targeted gene therapy forward. This innovation could facilitate a single-injection cure for SCD, accessible globally, including in low-resource countries, marking a significant advancement in gene therapy research for scientists and pharmaceutical companies alike.

Our preliminary studies have demonstrated targeted LV/LNP gene delivery in cell line models, but further optimization of HSC-targeted molecules is vital to enhance targeting efficiency, progress to in vivo animal experiments, and ultimately translate findings into clinical applications. Phage display methods, commonly employed to screen targeted molecules of engineered antibodies, like the variable heavy domain of heavy chain (VHH), involve expressing an antibody library on phage surfaces. These library phages are selected using antigen-coated beads, followed by cloning and DNA sequencing. Initially, we outsourced the phage display screening, but this limited our data comprehension and hindered further optimization. Therefore, acquiring premade antibody phage libraries for in-house phage display screening is crucial, allowing for a faster process and repeated experiments without additional purchases. This approach enables customization of screening methods, improves efficiency, and reduces costs, thus accelerating the development of in vivo HSC gene therapy.

This gene therapy method extends beyond blood diseases to potentially treat other organs, including the heart and lungs. The ongoing research to develop in vivo gene therapy is a primary focus of NHLBI, as highlighted by our Scientific Director, Dr. Richard Childs. Our branch, including our laboratory and Dr. Larochelle's laboratory, collaborates with the NHLBI non-human primate program on this project. Therefore, our purchase request is critical to advancing NHLBI research.

Phage display screening can optimize antibodies and develop diverse targeted therapies, such as:

Antibody therapies via immunoreaction
Targeted drug delivery through antibody-drug conjugates (ADC)
Targeted gene delivery for in vivo gene therapy
Tissue-specific imaging
Chimeric antigen receptor (CAR)-T cell therapy
These applications underscore the transformative potential of this technology in medical research and treatment development.

The acquisition of advanced research equipment is essential to the NCCIH/PRC’s mission of conducting transformative pain research. The equipment is a thermal testing device that measures threshold and tolerance responses to stimuli of various heat and thermal temperatures and provides standardized assessment of individual somatosensory responses. The equipment will be used in all ongoing protocols to characterize sensory phenotypes in patients with various pain conditions, and changes in pain responding due to the administration of non-pharmacological and pharmacological interventions. The equipment will be used to test somatosensory function in persons with rare pain conditions, temporomandibular joint disease, response to transcranial magnetic stimulation in patients with pain and comorbid depression, and in several other collaborative projects testing the effects of Resiniferatoxin for neuropathic pain conditions. 

This equipment will enhance the Center’s ability to perform innovative pain phenotyping, deepens the understanding of pain mechanisms, especially in rare conditions, and support the development and evaluation of novel pharmacologic and nonpharmacologic therapies. It will also enable research aimed at preventing the transition from acute to chronic pain and reducing opioid dependency, all within a whole person health framework. Furthermore, it will strengthen cross-institute collaborations and promote the integration of complementary and integrative approaches to chronic pain management, ultimately advancing the scientific and clinical objectives of the institute.

The following product features/characteristics are required for one (1) QST Lab Thermal Cutaneous Stimulator (TCS II.1.b) System and associated components. The equipment shall:

•	Have a precise temperature control with a temperature ramp of 0.10C to 3000C per second to prevent the device from overheating. 
•	Include an external touch screen capable of managing the stimulation parameters and the launching of manual stimulations. 
o	The external touch screen shall include an option to connect to an external computer.
•	Be MRI, EEG, and ECG compatible to administer thermal stimulation and the ability to set the intensity of the digital filter on the stimulator. This shall allow the use of existing MRI equipment to generate increased strength of the digital filters. 
•	Be ultraportable with a maximum weight of 10 Ib, allowing the equipment to move from multiple bedsides and rooms performing testing across the Clinical Center (CC) on different floors. This is to accommodate and minimize the transport of patients who are unable to move from room to room.
•	Include a temperature/stimulation duration function that integrates four parameters: the setpoint temperature, the dwell time, the ramp and the return ramp to reach the setpoint temperature to avoid burns on the skin.
•	Include a single probe that shall be used to study thermal grill illusion and diffuse noxious inhibitory control pain.
•	Allow the user to move the stimulation from one spot to another easily without having to reposition the device.

column5: Batteries, Cell Type, Various Sizes and Types - Hearing Aids and Other Medical Devices
column29: The Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) Denver Logistics Service (DLS) and Office of Connected Care have identified cell batteries of various sizes and types for continuous reordering as an ongoing requirement. The VA's DLS is a national distribution center which procures, stocks, and distributes batteries directly to VA clinics, medical centers, and eligible Veterans directly. The DLS has been providing batteries to VA hospitals and clinics, eligible Veterans, and other Government agencies since at least 1999. The types of batteries in this requirement are needed to power a wide variety of medical and other quality of life medical devices the VA provides to eligible Veterans including rechargeable and non-rechargeable, lithium, alkaline, zinc-air, nickel-metal-hydride (NIMH), and silver oxide batteries in a wide variety of form. The types of batteries included in this requirement are readily available in the commercial market, as commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) items that are widely used for applications other than the Government’s needs. All the various types of batteries in this requirement fit the definition of commercial items as set forth at FAR 2.101. Additionally, pursuant to the other than full and open competition authority set forth in Federal Acquisition regulation (FAR) 6.302-5, Authorized Or Required By Statute, this procurement will be a sole source award pursuant to 41 U.S.C. 3304(a)(5), the VA is required to procure seventeen (17) of the twenty-five (25) battery types included in this battery requirement from the AbilityOne Procurement List (PL). Furthermore, in accordance with Veterans Affairs Acquisition Manual (VAAM) M808.002-70(a), which states: "Priorities for use of mandatory Government sources - AbilityOne procedures, states: "As a result of Public Law 116-155, certain covered products and services on the AbilityOne Procurement List must be procured in accordance with Veterans Affairs Acquisition Regulation (VAAR) 808.002(a)(1)(iv) and (a)(2)."  Pursuant to FAR 8.704(a) states: “41 U.S.C. chapter 85 requires the Government to purchase supplies or services on the Procurement List, at prices established by the Committee, from AbilityOne participating nonprofit agencies if they are available within the period required.” This procurement action is a follow-on effort to the current five (5) battery FFP-IDIQ contracts, which all expire on August 31, 2025. The period of performance for this procurement will start on September 01, 2025 and run through August 31, 2030 if all option periods are exercised. The robust market research (e.g., both past and present iterations) has repeatedly shown there are no small business manufacturers of the battery types included in this requirement. All known companies that manufacture batteries described in this requirement are large, international businesses of which only three (3) of the large business manufactures are known to produce end products from a WTO-GPA designated country. The goal of this acquisition is to award one Firm-Fixed-Price (FFP), Indefinite Delivery - Indefinite Quantity (IDIQ) contract to the designated Central Nonprofit Agency (CNA) selected by the AbilityOne Committee through their program management representatives at Source America to supply the seventeen (17) battery types of this requirement currently listed on the AbilityOne Procurement List.

Illumina has identified a list of 101 items out of 414 items from their current price list of reagents and consumables that are manufactured in Singapore. The items include: Infinium Microarray products, NovaSeq products, NextSeq products, MiSeq products, MiniSeq products, and library prep products.

The NIH must continue to purchase Illumina reagents and consumables in order to avoid the introduction of new variables into the experimental results and to avoid long delays resulting from retesting of products and sequencing systems.

All reagents and consumables are designed and produced specifically for use with the unique manufacturer’s sequencing system. The use of another manufacturer’s reagents and consumables in Illumina manufactured equipment would not guarantee the response or expected outcomes and would jeopardize the research results. Changing reagents and consumables would require the changing of sequencing systems which would negate the Government’s investment of millions of dollars of equipment.

The Illumina reagents and consumables are proprietary to Illumina and are not manufactured by another source. There are no authorized third-party resellers. In addition, the use of another manufacturer's reagents and consumables would void the warranties on the current Illumina systems owned/leased by NIH.

column5: High-Quality Fishmeal-Based Supply of Pelleted Fish Feed
column29: The Northwest Fisheries Science Center requires high-quality fishmeal-based supply of pelleted fish feed for full life-cycle that will be used for the rearing of ESA-listed Redfish Lake Sockeye Salmon Captive Broodstock program at the Manchester Research Station and Creek Hatchery. High-quality fish meal feeds have been used consistently by this program for 30-plus years, with excellent results in producing high-quality ESA-listed captive broodstock fish and eggs for supplementation. The continued use of 100% fish-meal protein fish feed will ensure consistency in fish growth management, data collection, and palatability. The high-quality fishmeal-based supply of pelleted fish feed requires component specifications and is the only known product capable of meeting the needs of the Northwest Fisheries Science Center.  All feeds must have a proven performance in culturing Pacific salmon. The vendor must be Best Aquaculture Practices (BAP), Feed Assure, Nutrace, and ISO 9001:2000 Certified. Feed must contain high-quality marine fish oils, high levels of digestible protein from premium fish meal, and balanced lipid content. Protein ingredients should be predominantly fish meal and fish oil-based. All diets must be formulated to be fed as the sole ration to cultured salmon species. All diets must be shelf-stable and not require refrigeration. The vendor must have extensive experience providing fish feed to the Pacific salmon hatchery system.   A sources sought notice was not posted to SAM.gov.  However, an unrestricted solicitation was posted to SAM.gov, Solicitation ID 1333MF25Q0021, on 03/03/2025 through a CD-570 waiver process, approved by the NOAA Small Business Office. During the solicitation, interest was limited, and Moore-Clark U.S.A., Inc. (Bio-Oregon) reached out and requested that the requirement be changed to only the base year. The solicitation was then amended to remove the options from the solicitation. No eligible quote responses were received besides the foreign manufacturer of the required products. The original solicitation was posted for 8 days, and the amendment extended the response time by another 3 days.   The Contracting Officer searched the General Services Administration (GSA), Small Business Administration (SBA), and Acquisition Gateway websites on 01/28/2025 for fish feed and animal products. The search did not produce any other sources capable of providing the required fish feed.  None of the other fish feeds were found to be comparable to meet all of the requirements by the program office. The Contracting Officer submitted a National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) Manufacturing Extension Partnership (MEP) Supplier Scouting Opportunity Synopsis form on 04/07/2025, and the results on 04/25/2025 showed "No Matches" found.  No domestic sources were identified during market research that can meet the requirements of the NOAA Fisheries Northwest Fisheries Science Center.The mission impact would be critical because a change in diet could impact fish survival and productivity and negatively affect program goals. Changes in diet could potentially affect the growth and reproduction of ESA-listed broodstocks, disrupting the program while adjustments and evaluations are made to the long-established growth curves and predictable reproductive results. The resulting impacts could affect the annual stocking goal of 1 million smolts mandated by the Recovery Plan.

column6: 423730 - Warm Air Heating and Air-Conditioning Equipment and Supplies Merchant Wholesalers
column29:
The Contracting Officer posted a request for information on SAM.gov on 28 March 2024 with a response date of 8 April.  Two responses were received, the first from UKT Air Conditioners and the units they offered did meet the Buy American Preference criteria but did not produce systems in the size and capacity required.  The units provided are made for small office spaces or bedrooms.  The second response from Blue Jacket Industries, which provided cut sheets on products such as Daiken, Mitsubishi, and Pioneer, have previously been reviewed by the Contracting Officer and found not compliant with the Buy American Preference.  

The FAA is aware of one supplier of a compliant product: Johnson Controls who is the controlling entity of Trane, York, Goodman, Coleman, and others.  Trane has partnered with Mitsubishi to provide and sell VRF units under the Trane name.  They admit through a letter that they do not meet the Buy American Preference requirements. They also state that none of their ductless mini-split units are produced or manufactured in the U.S. 

Additional market research has been conducted in the past six months through internet research and discussions with subject matter experts (SMEs) within the HVAC industry. Through these discussions and emails with SMEs, none of the ductless HVAC mini-splits in the size and capacity needed are produced or manufactured within the United States.  

After discussions with industry experts, they have indicated that the ductless mini-splits are expected to be another two to three years away to be compliant with the BAA requirements.  The leading manufacturers are not capable of producing the ductless units in the size and capacities the FAA is needing due to new production lines and the technology to be added to the current locations within the U.S.

A previous waiver was approved in June 2024 for the same type of units.  Additional market research through the internet was conducted to find possible changes to the market.  The market has not changed, and no BAA Compliant manufacturers have been found.  

Continued research was conducted on 4/29/25 on the Made in America Office website with several waivers submitted requesting the same equipment.  The most recent was posted on 2/6/25 by the Dept of the Army and another posted 1/17/25 by the U.S. Coast Guard.  Continued market research over the past year continues to provide the same result of no U.S. manufacturers of the Ductless HVAC Mini-Split systems as indicated from the MIAO with other agencies requesting the same types of units.

column29: The Production Systems Group (PSG) in the NIST Engineering Laboratory (EL) conducts research in metals-based additive manufacturing (AM) to develop and deploy advances in measurement science that will enable rapid design-to-product transformation. The PSG is expanding its measurement science facilities of additive manufacturing (AM) research. A static angle of repose measurement system (in its basic form) creates a conical pile of granular material onto a flat surface and measures the angle between the slope of the pile and the base plane (flat surface). That is called the "static angle of repose." This measurement can be done using either a protractor arrangement, a silhouette image, or a laser.  The static angle of repose is three-dimensional. Therefore, it is desirable to check the conical pile from all sides to estimate the best value of the static angle of repose. The Production Systems Group (PSG) requires the above-mentioned ‘static angle of repose measurement system’ to be installed at the PSG at NIST Gaithersburg, MD campus.

The National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke (NINDS) is part of the NIH and researches the causes, treatment, and prevention of neurological disorders
and stroke. Its mission is to seek fundamental knowledge about the brain and nervous system and use that knowledge to reduce the burden of neurological disease.
This acquisition is to purchase the Kinetix22 Scientific CMOS Camera for the Inherited Neurodegenerative Diseases Section (INDS). Kinetix22 Scientific CMOS
Camera emerges as the optimal solution, offering features tailored to advanced microscopy applications. The Kinetix22 Scientific CMOS will be seamlessly
integrated into the existing Nikon Ti epifluorescent microscope utilized by the INDS lab, thereby enhancing its imaging capabilities for studying human stem cell-derived
neurons in the context of neurodegenerative diseases. By investing in the Kinetix22 Scientific CMOS Camera, the laboratory can accelerate its research progress,
enhance data quality, and gain deeper insights into complex mechanisms underlying neurodegenerative diseases, ultimately contributing to advancements in biomedical
science. The Kinetix22 Scientific CMOS Camera will be attached to a current Nikon epifluorescent microscope and will replace an outdated camera that does not have sufficient sensitivity for modern imaging requirements. The specific experiment that relates to this camera is called an optical pooled screen,
in which we use the microscope to determine how cells respond to cellular perturbations associated with neurodegenerative diseases. This microscope was
heavily modified and there are no replacement options on the campus that can be used as alternatives.

column29: The National Institute of Standards and Technology’s (NIST), Material Measurement Laboratory (MML), Gas Sensing Metrology Group (GSMG) of the Chemical Science Division is committed to supporting the NIST gas Standard Reference Materials (SRMs) and NIST-Traceable Reference Materials (NTRMs) programs. To support the SRM and NTRM programs, GSMG maintains groups of Primary gas Standard Materials (PSMs). Many gas PSMs are currently depleted, and GSMG is in urgent need of producing replacement PSMs. GSMG requires the purchase of new, highly stable, non-reactive aluminum gas cylinders for the production of new PSMs for both non-reactive and reactive gas mixtures. The production of PSMs requires the use of stable, long lifespan gas cylinders that exceed the specifications found in industry. These gas cylinders are required to hold gas mixtures stable for several years without any change in the composition of the gas. Gas mixtures range from non-reactive to reactive and at concentrations that range from low to high. Sources of deviation from long-term stability include impurities and moisture trapped in the cylinder walls during production, reactions with the gas cylinder walls, reactions with the gas cylinder valve, and the presence of any leaks. Particularly for the low concentrations that NIST sometimes uses, even small levels of reactivity will affect the stability of the gas mixture after years of containment. With proper maintenance typical cylinders provide mechanical stability lasts for about 10 years, but this does not imply that typical cylinders maintain chemical stability of the gas mixture for 10 years. MML’s GSMG requires a cylinder that provides chemical stability of gas mixtures for a minimum of 10 years, as verified by NIST-internal stability measurements.

column29: The National Institute of Standards and Technology, Material Measurements Laboratory (MML) requires a multichannel potentiostat/galvanostat—a device that can conduct both potentiostatic (voltage control) and galvanostatic (current control) electrochemical experiments to support research into film deposition processes. This instrument will be used to perform electrochemical film deposition, a method where thin films of material are formed by electrochemical reactions, which is key for creating effective microelectronic interconnects. The research aims to understand the mechanisms behind film deposition and to optimize these processes to reliably achieve metallizations, vias (vertical interconnect accesses), and lines with the electronic properties required for high-performance circuits. The work will span a broad range of length scales, from tests on macroscale electrodes (measuring in square centimeters) down to experiments on submicron (less than one-millionth of a meter) microelectrodes, ensuring that both large-scale and highly miniaturized processes are thoroughly studied. In addition to deposition behavior, the study will examine phenomena such as adsorption (the adhesion of atoms or molecules to a surface) and charge transfer processes across various time scales, providing a comprehensive understanding of the dynamic processes involved in film formation

column6: 333514 - Special Die and Tool, Die Set, Jig, and Fixture Manufacturing
column29:
The agency requires a Sodick ALN600G 4 axis CNC Wire EDM that utilizes our already in use, proprietary SODICK® controller,
operating Sodick conversational EDM software found throughout the shop.
A 4-axis Sodick wire EDM (Electrical Discharge Machining) is used to cut complex shapes and contours in conductive materials
with high precision. The purpose of the 4-axis functionality is to allow machining in the X, Y, U, V, and rotary (A) axis, enabling
the wire to approach the workpiece from multiple angles. The additional rotational axis allows for more intricate cuts, such as
tapering or machining parts with geometry which cannot be achieved with conventional machining methods. Wire EDM is
commonly used for prototype machining, allowing for rapid production of custom, high-precision parts with complex geometries,
enabling efficient testing and iteration of designs before full production. The 4-axis wire EDM is essential for producing highprecision
components from tough materials like titanium, tungsten, Inconel, Hastelloy, hardened steel, or any other conductive
material while maintaining structural integrity without thermal distortion.
For a prototype shop, the need to quickly and efficiently program a 4 axis wire EDM is central to the operation. Our limited
space, floor weight capacity, as well as work envelope requirements and prior experience with Sodick machines,
demand a very specific machine. To look outside of the Sodick brand would level serious setbacks in software and machine
integration as well as an extremely steep, unnecessary, and expensive learning curve for the entire Model Shop. No other brand
uses the Sodick control software.
The EDM is made in Thailand and there is no domestic source for this machine and accessories.

The intramural laboratory within the NINDS Neurogenetics Branch’s IND Section focuses on research efforts for identifying intersecting mechanisms of neurodegenerative diseases, with an ultimate goal of developing targeted, disease-modifying therapies for affected patients.
Imaging capabilities is critical for studying human stem cell-derived neurons in the context of neurodegenerative diseases. Therefore, NINDS has identified a need for a microscope camera in order to accelerate its research progress, enhance data quality, and gain deeper insights into complex mechanisms underlying neurodegenerative diseases, ultimately contributing to advancements in biomedical science.
The brand-name item that meets the need of this procurement is Nikon Instruments, Inc.’s Kinetix back-illuminated sCMOS (Part #77018310), which relies on imaging capabilities to study human stem cell-derived neurons.
The significant, essential physical, functional, performance, or material element(s) that are the Government’s minimum acceptable standards for an equivalent system to meet the government’s objective are as follows:
An equivalent microscope camera shall have:
• Extreme Speed: 498 frames per second (fps) at 8-bit readout mode, facilitating rapid data acquisition for dynamic imaging studies.
• Large Field of View: 29.4 mm diagonal field of view, maximizing data capture in a single frame, crucial for efficiency in image acquisition.
• High Sensitivity: Near-perfect 96% quantum efficiency and low 0.7 e- read noise ensure clear imaging, even in low-light conditions.
• High Resolution: 6.5 ìm x 6.5 ìm pixels provide detailed images suitable for live cell imaging at 40x and 60x magnification.
• Superior Background Quality: Pattern Noise Reduction Technology and Correlated Noise Reduction Technology guarantee clean, pattern-free 
   images.
• Integration set-up with Existing Equipment: Seamless attachment to the laboratory's Nikon spinning disk microscope for enhanced imaging 
  capabilities.
The microscope camera shall be warranted, and the Contractor shall warrant its equipment against defects in material, fabrication, or workmanship for a minimum of one (1) year from the date the Government accepts delivery of the item.  
Anticipated mission impact if there is no waiver: NINDS mission would be severely impacted if the abovementioned item does not meet the minimum salient characteristics. The need for this equipment is vital for the use by its NINDS researchers and scientists to conduct its research and delays would cause a significant loss in the Institute’s investment of time and funds required to change existing protocols or restart ongoing studies if previous data from samples is deemed invalid. The laboratory’s ongoing research experiments on neurodegenerative diseases for the development of targeted, disease-modifying therapies for affected patients would be disrupted and could not continue.

The VRC Virology Laboratory comprises the Virology, Humoral Immunology, and Structural Biology Sections and related Cores. The goal of the Virology Laboratory is to conduct research to understand better the cellular and molecular regulation of viral gene expression, viral entry into the host cell, optimization of immune responses to gene-based vaccination and correlates of immune protection, assessment of active and passive approaches for HIV-1 vaccine development, and to apply structural biology techniques toward rational vaccine design for emerging and re-emerging infectious diseases. The Vaccine Research Center is developing vaccines for HIV-1 and other viruses.  Currently, SBS/SBIS is working on developing vaccines for SARS-CoV-2. Production of wild-type and mutant viral proteins and anti-viral antibodies is critical in vaccine development. Making specific immunogens as probes is critical to isolate antibodies and develop therapeutic agents.  Relevant salient characteristics of the required Anti-human IgG Fc Capture Biosensors are that they provide counting label-free detection, low limit of detection, high output screening, small sample volume for the immobilization of human IgG and other proteins containing the human Fc region for their kinetic analysis. The sensitivity, resolution, linearity, zero drift and full-scale drift, range, repeatability and reproducibility of these required biosensors allow for consistency and integrity in the lab results. These biosensors are used on the Octet BLI system that has an estimated price of $561,900.  There are no other manufacturers that offer biosensors compatible to this instrument and an equal product does not exist.   The impact if the required biosensors are not acquired is that the lab will not be able to run binding/screening, affinity, and kinetic measurements for protein samples. This will hinder the research progress relating to antibody structural studies, vaccine designs, and antibody improvements.

column17: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF STANDARDS AND TECHNOLOGY
column20: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF STANDARDS AND TECHNOLOGY
column29:
The Material Measurement laboratory, Materials Measurement Science Division, Nanomechanical Properties Group is constructing a custom Time Domain Thermoreflectance (TDTR) instrument. In TDTR, a pump and a probe laser are concentrically focused on the sample: a modulated, pulsed pump laser heats the sample; and a delayed, pulsed probe beam interrogates the thermal properties of thin film or multilayered materials as a function of the probe delay time.  To accomplish this the samples are placed on top of the desired hexapods where they are translated, rotated and tilted.  In a thermoreflectance (TR) measurement, the sample of interest is coated with an Au or Al transducer film that has a large coefficient of TR at the probe laser wavelength. The Coefficient of TR is a physical property that relates a material's change in temperature to the resulting change in optical reflectance. 
 In this multiple color system, two separate pump lasers at different wavelengths will be used to selectively measure both Au and Al transducer coated samples. The sample must be translated precisely into the focal plane of the objective (Tz axis), and the tip-tilt (Rx and Ry axis rotation angles) of the sample must also be adjusted to properly reflect the probe beam back through the objective. In addition, translation in the x and y directions is required for 2D property mapping. Hexapods are kinematic devices uniquely capable of simultaneously, precisely, and stably controlling 6 degrees of freedom: Translation in Tx, Ty, and Tx, and Rotation in Rx, Ry, and Rz. Hexapods are also dimensionally more compact than any combination of 6 individual linear and rotary motion stages. The TDTR instrument will enable NIST to uniquely measure the in situ thermal properties of thin film and multilayer materials in wide bandgap power electronics materials, packaging materials, and integrated chip components, directly supporting the "Thermoreflectance Thermal Property Measurements" CHIPS project.

column20: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF STANDARDS AND TECHNOLOGY
column29: The National Institute of Standards and Technology, Physical Measurement Laboratory, Quantum Measurement Division, Quantum Optics Group (QOG) is furthering NIST’s mission to develop campus-wide and metropolitan-scale quantum networks, using highly sensitive few-photon measurements for distribution of photonic quantum information. As part of these efforts, the QOG is developing the capability to stabilize optical phase over long fiber links with faint light, enabling the distribution of phase-sensitive quantum information carriers. This project combines QOG expertise in single-photon measurement with the expertise of the Time and Frequency Division (TFD) in ultra stable optical frequency references. Before incorporating quantum measurement, the QOG shall first replicate state-of-the-art methods of classical phase stabilization using a benchmark system designed by the TFD. A central component of this system is a low-noise single-frequency laser, to be purchased here. The QOG requires a tunable, single-frequency laser which shall be frequency-locked to an external cavity, and the combined laser-cavity system shall serve as a stable phase reference for active phase stabilization of a fiber link. The optical reference cavity shall be provided by the TFD, and the remaining commercial equipment (including the laser) is meant to replicate the TFD’s original phase stabilization apparatus as closely as possible.

column29: The National Institute of Standards and Technology, Physical Measurement Laboratory, Sensor Science Division, Remote Sensing Group (RSG) requires an automatically tunable ultraviolet to infrared laser system to be used in conjunction with the Primary Optical Watt Radiometer (POWR), which serves as the Nation’s optical power reference. POWR, an absolute cryogenic radiometer, measures the optical power from a minimally diverging light source and uses that same light source to then calibrate the responsivity of different types of detectors, which are then used as optical power standards. The detectors used are primarily Si photodiodes/traps (for the UV to near-IR) or InGaAs photodiodes (for the IR). The laser system shall be used for setting the UV to IR optical power scale/standards and must be capable of providing light that can be tuned, at least, between 190 nm to 2300 nm with continuous wavelength tuning. Additionally, a narrow spectral bandwidth (≈10 cm−1) at each chosen wavelength is required. Automatic (i.e. computer controlled/programmable) tuning of the system is essential so that time-efficient calibrations of detectors can be performed in the specified wavelength range.

column29: The Fabrication Technology Office (FTO) provides a wide range of engineering, fabrication, and technical services to support the creation of unique measurement instruments and scientific apparatus needed by NIST scientists and research staff. The FTO provides the mission critical engineering and manufacturing expertise and equipment needed to produce specialized parts and subassemblies for a variety of one-of-a-kind instruments conceived by NIST scientists and engineers. To meet mission requirements, the FTO needs to procure a handheld CMM. Currently, FTO uses a bridge type of CMM that is several decades old, which experiences calibration and operational issues. As a result, the quality and dimensional accuracy of the parts produced vary significantly, directly impacting the science and mission at the NIST. A handheld CMM is required that can calibrate with an accuracy of 5 thousandths of an inch. Currently, inspections taking place in the inspection room or at the machine are done by hand which don’t have the same accuracy as a CMM. Thus, a handheld CMM that meets the minimum specifications required by FTO, including a non-rotary encoder is required.

To NEMA 4/4X/6; Cable Length - [00] Compact -None; Process Connections - [A] ASME 150lb RF Flanges; Housing/Flange Material Sheet Steel/Carbon Steel (150 lb); Grounding Rings Without; Hazardous Area Approvals-General Purpose; calibration - standard + 316L SS tagplate (120x46mm); Lining - Teflon(PFA) – Standard. The exploration of brain regions known to contribute to reward-related behaviors requires electrodes designed to explore the exact regions under study. Disentangling the contributions of multiple regions requires the ability to recording the electrical activity of many neurons simultaneously. Simultaneous recording is especially important for reward-related circuitry, where computational models are used to guide the research. The specified purchase is for a electrodes able to reach deep structures in nonhuman primate, like the amygdala and ventral striatum. This new generation of electrodes can record from hundreds of neurons simultaneously. The specified electrodes are required for both ongoing experiments and upcoming ones. Understanding the circuitry behind reward-related behavior is an essential component of the NIMH mission to understand and develop treatments for behavioral disorders.

The National Institutes of Health (NIH), National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases (NIAID) Laboratory of Allergic Diseases (LAD) Adaptive Immunity and Immunoregulation Section (AIIS) has the overall goal to define the cellular and molecular mechanisms that regulate the balance between protective and pathogenic adaptive immune responses to allergens. Ultimately, this research aims to develop new immunotherapies to treat and prevent food and respiratory allergies without inducing profound immunosuppression. The major areas of research are to characterize the mechanisms controlling adaptive immune responses, particularly memory T and B cells and T follicular helper cells, in the context of food and respiratory allergies; investigate how infections contribute to the development of allergies and how allergies, in turn, affect immune responses to pathogens and vaccines; and develop novel immunotherapies that balance protection and immunosuppression for food and respiratory allergens.

The single-cell suspensions or thorough homogenates must be easily and reproducibly obtained using the unique C Tubes or M Tubes. Salient characteristics include: dissociate cells from various organs to include as spleen, lymph nodes, brain, kidney etc; make a single cells suspension and purification for studying the function of specific immune cells population during inflammation, autoimmune disease or after cellular activation with various stimuli; and dissociate immune cells from various tissues. column29: The U.S. Department of Commerce, National Institute of Standards and Technology, Materials Measurement Laboratory, Materials Measurement Science Division (MMSD) requires a cryogenic mill for grinding plastic materials. The mill must have cryogenic capability to freeze plastics below their glass transition temperature (Tg), making them brittle and suitable for milling into fine particles. MMSD aims to produce and recover submicron (≤ 1 μm) particles, which is achievable only through cryogenic ball milling. This method employs high-impact forces to fracture frozen materials, with liquid nitrogen often used to maintain sub-Tg temperatures and prevent heating during milling.

. The NHLBI Murine Phenotyping Core needs the smart-Kage system to conduct activity, and behavioral phenotyping in home cage environments for core projects. The current home cage activity monitoring system being used in the lab is old and outdated. Additionally,  2 out of the 8 arenas are no longer functioning. The cage’s cameras and lids are outdated and being phased out and the newer version cameras are not compatible with the software running in the current system.  This means that all 8 cages must be replaced at once so that experiments may be run simultaneously. 
The Cambridge Phenotyping Smart-Kage system is a fully automated home cage system allowing for both cognitive and behavioral phenotyping of mice.  This system uses IR top mounted cameras to monitor mouse movement and behavior which is analyzed and interpreted through CNN deep learning based technology for robust phenotypic characterization in models. The Cambridge Phenotyping smart-Kages are the only available home-cage monitoring system for fully automated comprehensive cognitive and behavioral phenotyping of individually housed mice, compatible with long-term experiments.
The Smart-Kages, incorporate multi-body point analysis of the animals, random forest classifiers to assign behavioral labels, behavioral parameters to predict underlying mouse phenotype using agglomerative hierarchical clustering, for more robust behavioral characterization and applications. No other mouse home cage monitoring systems utilizes this robust AI based technology. The closest alternative to the Cambridge Phenotyping smart-Kage system uses a surgically implanted transmitter which tracks animal location in cages by RFID antennas. Therefore this system is only capable of location tracking. In comparison, the smart-Kage utilizes IR cameras and multipoint tracking to monitor location, movement patterns, body positions, which can then be used by an AI based analysis tool using CNN and random forest classifiers to assign behavioral labels, and then behavioral parameters can predict underlying phenotypes using agglomerative (hierarchical) clustering.
The Smart-Kages are fully automated home-cage monitoring system for both cognitive and behavioral phenotyping and the home cages include within their design and components along with the AI based technology that ability to additionally test T-maze alternation with variable inter-trial intervals, novel object recognition and object in place recognition tests, place preference learning, associative learning, light/dark preference, locomotion, running wheels, quiescence, water intake for robust behavioral and cognitive testing of mice in their home cage environment. The Cambridge smart-Kage is the only system available that allows cognitive and activity testing utilizing long term 24/7 IR video based monitoring at 2Hz, multipoint body/position tracking and AI-based software for monitoring and controlling experimental pipeline and data analysis. Cognitive tasks include T-maze-like alternation (“smart T-maze”), novel object recognition (“smart NOR”), and object-in-place recognition (“smart OPR”) tasks carried out continuously and simultaneously in the smart-Kage without any interference from the experimenter and the system monitors an animal’s position, water consumption, quiescence, and locomotion. This is the only smart cage system utilizing AI related technology, the videos are analyzed using CNN and random forest classifiers assigned to behavioral labels, and then behavioral parameters can predict underlying phenotypes using agglomerative (hierarchical) clustering.

agreements with other companies for sale to commercial clients in small quantities, while
Kaymont retains the exclusive right for sale to the government, including NWS. Kaymont has
provided Totex GP26 balloons for use at NWS Upper Air sites for over fifteen years. Only Kaymont is
able to meet the high demand of the NWS Upper Air Program of approximately 6,500 balloons per
month within a 75-day lead-time with a balloon that meets the NWS Specification. Using an
alternative balloon will reduce burst heights and/or cause damage during ascent, negatively
impacting the reliability and accuracy of radiosonde measurements.

The Alaska Fisheries Science Center (AFSC) Auke Bay Lab requires three (3) each of flow sensors and signal converters to incorporate into their ultraviolet (UV) disinfection system that will treat combined waste sea water from laboratory aquariums, experiment tanks and the building’s heating system. The waste sea water is discharged to coastal waters via a marine outfall in Favorite Channel. The discharge of wastewater from this outfall is regulated by the Alaska Department of Environmental Conservation (ADEC), where ADEC has issued an Alaska Pollutant Discharge Elimination System (APDES) permit for the outfall (permit number AK0053236). The permit establishes allowable limits for wastewater parameters including flow, Fecal coliform (FC) bacteria, Enterococci bacteria (EB), pH, Dissolved Oxygen (DO), temperature, and ammonia nitrogen. The UV disinfection system is required in order to meet the Fecal coliform and Enterococci bacteria limits stipulated in the permit. This purchase is to buy flow sensors and converters for the new UV disinfection system in accordance with the system design approved by ADEC.

To NEMA 4/4X/6; Cable Length - [00] Compact - None; Process Connections - [A] ASME 150lb RF Flanges; Housing/Flange Material Sheet Steel/Carbon Steel (150 lb); Grounding Rings Without; Hazardous Area Approvals-General Purpose; calibration - standard + 316L SS tagplate (120x46mm); Lining - Teflon (PFA) – Standard. The purchase also includes Electromagnetic Flow/Signal Converter for Flow Sensor (3 each) with the following technical specifications: 4-wire electromagnetic signal converter compatible with above 6" Electromagnetic Flow Sensor; continuous measurement of volume flow and flow velocity; minimum accuracy and long-term stability: ±0.15% of measured value ± 1 mm/s; LC Display, backlit white, standard English, Metric, British and US selectable units; maximum measuring error: ±0.15% of the measured value ±1 mm/s; repeatability: ±0.06%; compact; Power Supply - [A] 100-230 VAC; Hazardous Area Approvals-General Purpose; cable connection - [46] 1/2" NPT; custody transfer- without; process diagnostics-standard; converter housing-Die-Cast Aluminum (Polyurethane Coated); outputs -communication- IO: RS485 Modbus; first IO Module-without IO module; second IO Module-without IO module; reference method-virtual reference; tag plate-without.

column5: Loligo ApS Swim Tunnel Respirometry System
column29: National Marine Fisheries Service - Alaska Fisheries Science Center - needs to procure two (2) Loligo Swim Tunnel respirometers and components for aquatic respirometry research to provide real-time monitoring of juvenile fish metabolic processes in response to sea water condition changes. There are no known domestic manufacturers of swim tunnel respirometers. This requirement supports AFSC’s mission requirement to evaluate the effects of climate conditions on the fishery resources of Alaska. Ocean acidification results from increased concentrations of CO2 in the ocean waters resulting in a change in pH that can have negative impacts on the ion-exchange system and respiratory capacities of marine organisms. These impacts may make the organisms more vulnerable to extremes in other aspects of habitat conditions such as high temperatures or low oxygen levels. column5: Thermo Scientific Gallery Aqua Master with ECM (Filter Photometer)
column29:
Filter photometer to analyze water samples, soil extracts, and plant tissue extracts for a wide range of dissolved constituents in the Soil and Water Quality Lab at the USDA-ARS Cropping Systems and Water Quality Research Unit (CSWQRU) in Columbia, MO. This instrument must be identical to the existing instrument is only provided by Thermo Electron North America for efficiency and to provide long-term data continuity.
Specifications include:
Office of the Director . Midwest Area Administrative Office Acquisitions & Personal Property/Fleet Team
1815 North University Street . Peoria, IL 61604 USDA is an Equal Opportunity Provider and Employer
2
• Single channel filter photometer
• Absorbance resolution of 0.001 A or better
• Minimum absorbance range: 0.0-3.0 A
• Minimum spectral range: 300 – 850 nm
• Minimum 10 filter positions
• Concurrent pH, conductivity, and photometric analysis
• Automated system for hands-free analysis
• Operates with 120 V or 240 V power supply
The USDA-ARS CSWQRU conducts long-term water quality research at the plot, field, and watershed scale. Laboratory instrumentation is required to analyze water samples in accordance with research objectives and following standard approved analytical methods. Our current throughput capacity for analysis of water samples [aqueous nutrients, pH, and electrical conductivity (EC)], does not match the rate of sample submission. This causes the laboratory to fail to comply with standard methodological quality assurance and quality control (QA/QC) requirements. Therefore, we require a second identical instrument to handle sample throughput, maintain QA/QC compliance, and maintain continuity in analytical methods across the dataset. Our current instrument used for analysis of water samples is a ThermoFisher Scientific Gallery Aqua Master with ECM. Adding a new data stream from a different manufacturer would require a different instrument parser and data format for the Laboratory Information Management System, require an additional method tag in the final data repository, require a new layer of method tracking for sample reanalysis, and negatively affect the integrity and utility of the dataset for our long-term research program. For consistency across data streams and seamless harmonization of data for statistical analysis and interpretation, a second ThermoFisher Scientific Gallery Aqua Master with ECM is required. This is not only for efficiency, but also for data integrity in our long-term research.

column29:
The US Department of Commerce (DOC), NIST, Intelligent Systems Division (ISD) develops, advances, and deploys measurement science and standards to speed the development, adoption, and integration of leading-edge intelligent technologies to advance U.S. manufacturing performance. One of the focus areas of ISD is to develop measurement science for sensing and perception system performance characterization to reduce the risk related to the adoption of new technologies and to advance the agility, safety, and productivity of collaborative industrial and mobile robots. This requirement is part of the Manufacturing Science for Manufacturing Robotics (MSMR) program. Evaluating 3D sensors such as the ones used in robotic bin-picking systems is one area that MSMR is exploring as thousands of such systems are being deployed in the US and around the world. Developing performance metrics and evaluation procedures requires access to the technologies to establish ground truth. Laser tracker technology was invented at NIST in the 1980s and is the gold standard for short—and long-range high-accuracy measurements. The required laser tracker system is needed by the NIST ISD to obtain ground-truth information while evaluating 3D sensors, part inspection, and robotic arms.

The NIST Engineering Laboratory (EL) and Physical Measurement Laboratory (PML) collaborate on work related to the use of 3D imaging systems as well as standard development for these systems on a regular basis. EL’s current work includes a need to track robot arms and mobile robots in real-time with very high precision. A laser tracker with the ability to measure the 6 degree-of-freedom position and orientation of a robot is currently the most precise way of doing this. This laser tracker capability is also an area for which PML is seeking to help develop standards (since very few standards exist). EL’s expertise in robotics combined with PML’s expertise in laser tracker standards development require that EL and PML be able to use compatible instruments. PML already owns a Leica AT-960-LR laser tracker, as well as a Leica T-MAC and Leica T-Probe. EL does not currently own a compatible laser tracker and requires a Leica AT-960-LR laser tracker to use with the PML-owned T-MAC and T-Probe to support this collaboration in an effort to fulfill its mission.

The required laser tracker system must be capable of dimensional measurement capabilities with sub-milli-meter accuracy at the long-range (≈50 m) and micron-level accuracy at the short range (≈ 2 m), as well as be compatible with 6DOF accessories to perform high-speed 3D data acquisition.

Due to the aforementioned compatibility requirements and the fact that the required system is the only system capable of performing the dimensional measurement capabilities necessary, NIST has determined that only the Leica AT960-LR Laser Tracker System will satisfy the agency’s needs for continued mission requirements.

The GML requires radiosondes to be used during launches of balloonborne
ozonesonde and water vapor/aerosol instruments. The InterMet IMET-54-AS is a radiosonde
interfaced with the ozonesonde, water vapor, and aerosol instruments. The instrument measures air
temperature with a small glass bead thermistor. Its small size minimizes effects caused by long and
short-wave radiation and ensures fast response times. The humidity sensor is a thin-film capacitive
polymer that responds directly to relative humidity. Radiosonde height is provided by the integrated

swinging of the radiosonde beneath the balloon. Packet transmission uses error correction
techniques to ensure reliable, accurate data reception for all expected link conditions.

Statement of Need
The LMS is in need of an automated stereotaxic instrument. This instrument is used to do viral microinjections into mouse brains or do any brain surgeries, such as those needed for in vivo calcium imaging experiments. The instrument automatically measures the mouse brain orientation, creates the necessary windows for the injections, and injects into the brain at a steady and controlled rate. The instrument is needed for cell and brain region specific viral injections to overexpress a receptor, called GPR110, being studied by the lab. Due to the number of mice being used for this experiment, the automated stereotaxic machine will help reduce time, increase reliability, and reduce error during these injections. RWD Life Science Inc., a small business, located in San Jose, California, USA and incorporated in Delaware, USA, manufactures the needed 71001-S model of Automated Stereotaxic Instrument in China. To our knowledge the RWD Life Science Inc. is the only supplier of the RWD 71001-S Automated Stereotaxic Instrument and Automated Injection in the market.

The automated food pathogens detection instrument offers quality control testing solutions for fast and accurate microbial identification. With technology that includes an extensive and robust identification database, rapid results, and minimal training time, it will streamline laboratory workflow for increased productivity.

column5: TARGETED DESORPTION ELECTROSPRAY IONIZATION X-SPOT MASS SPECTROMETRY IMAGING (DESI XS MSI) INSTRUMENT
column29: The acquisition of a targeted DESI XS MSI is crucial for the establishment of the Emerging Chemicals of Concern Program Area within CFSAN’s Office of Applied Research and Safety Assessment (ORSA), Division of Virulence Assessment (DVA). This advanced analytical tool allows for detailed imaging and analysis of chemical compounds directly on surfaces, particularly in complex samples such as plant tissues. The DESI XS MSI is a sophisticated analytical technique used to perform high-resolution imaging and analysis of chemical compounds directly on surfaces. This method allows for the detection and visualization of molecules in complex samples, such as plant tissues. When applied to assessing the presence of chemicals like Perfluoroalkyl Substances (PFAS), DESI XS MSI enables sensitive and specific detection of these compounds as they move within plant tissues from the soil or water sources during crop growth. DESI XS MSI functions by generating a charged aerosol of microdroplets that are sprayed onto a surface to desorb and ionize molecules for subsequent analysis by mass spectrometry. Notably, this technique requires minimal sample preparation, enabling the rapid detection and mapping of various compounds including chemicals, drugs, metabolites, and lipids in diverse sample types.

column29: CDC NIOSH Field Studies Branch requires a PExA instrument to determine whether additive manufacturing (AM) workers breathe in enough chemicals to induce measurable changes in lung health. PExA is a non-invasive method for sampling particles in exhaled air from lower airways and analyzing for protein, lipid, and cytokine markers of airway health using multiplex immunoassay, spectrometry, or chromatography. PExA differs from other types of exhaled breath collection instruments in that PExA provides a more concentrated exhaled breath sample. PExA is the only instrument available to collect a concentrated sample of exhaled particles from the lower airways. Specifically, PExA will be used to evaluate cross-shift/week changes in biomarker levels associated with adverse respiratory health. PExA is a non-invasive, reproducible and easy method for sampling non-volatile material from the distal airways. The method is based on the collection of endogenous particles in exhaled air (PEx). Individuals exhale into the sampling device, which collects PEx onto a substrate inside an impactor. After extraction from the substrate, the particles are analyzed using standard methods such as ELISA, MS, LC and HPLC. The PExA is the exclusive product identified to meet all the requirements of this project, and is commercially available only from PExA AB. The requested delivery is on or before 30 September 2024.

National Institute of Health, Clinical Center (NIH-CC)’s Translational Biobehavioral & Health
Disparities Branch (TBHD) is involved in the study of psychological and neural factors involved
in cooking behaviors in order to develop adaptive and potentially therapeutic dietary behavior
interventions. As part of the research, the research team will conduct feasibility testing of mobile
body and brain imaging and measurement while professional cooks are engaged in a cooking
task. This project is an initial step for the Section on Dietary Behaviors and Biopsychosocial
Health laboratory in determining biobehavioral factors involved in dietary behaviors.
The purpose and objective of this order is to acquire mobile brain and body measurement system
for the study. Services are required of a company to provide the training, equipment and
maintenance services for the mobile brain and body measurement equipment. In order to carry
out this study, additional ANT Neuro branded EEGs and its accompanying accessories and
software are required.

Our Program office can only use this brand of EEG systems to continue research/work that was
done with this brand, and because EEG systems from different vendors are not compatible with
each other. ANT Neuro, North America, Inc. has been an established mobile brain and body
imaging company since 1997, and ANT North America Inc. is the only US distributor of their
ANT EEG system. EEG systems from different vendors are not compatible with each other.
Program is currently using ANT products software and systems, and need the compatibility that
comes with purchasing additional ANT EEGs will. More specifically, Program has conducted a
demonstration of the planned pilot study using the ANT Neuro mobile equipment. In addition,
Program has contacted a comparable company, MBrain Train, and were told that they do not
offer a saline wet mount option with the required 64 channels for our pilot study. Program also
contacted another company called Brain Vision that company’s product could also not meet
Program’s needs.

Program’s initial mobile EEG was purchased from ANT Neuro, North America. Program need to
purchase the exact same equipment again for multiple reasons – upcoming research protocol may
include a two-participant design whereby two subjects will wear the EEG cap at the same time,
requiring purchase of the exact same second EEG for the second subject. For upcoming
protocols with one person design, the second EEG will be used as a back-up should the 1st EEG
break. To help ensure consistent reliable data and decrease potential variations other EEG
machines could cause in relation to data output, the exact same equipment needs to be used in
both scenarios posed above. Therefore, purchasing a new EEG system from a different vendor
would not be compatible with the existing systems and research programs used by Program.
Only purchasing additional ANT EEGs would allow compatibility and continuation of research.

column29: The National Institute of Standards and Technology, Materials Measurement Laboratory, Materials Measurement Division, Microscopy and Microanalysis Research Group (MMRG) requires a 660nm single frequency (SF), continuous wave (CW) laser system. This equipment is necessary for building a microscope that will be built as a part of a CHIPS project for the mapping of the mechanical properties of packaging material for microelectronics. Variations in the local degree of cure in these packaging materials can lead to variations in mechanical properties. Understanding these local variations in properties is important to understand how to limit warpage, a major issue in next-generation microelectronics packaging. The microscope that will be built as a part of this project will perform Brillouin Light Scattering spectroscopy and low-frequency Raman spectroscopy. These two techniques can be used to quantify local variations in mechanical properties, providing insight into the relaxation processes (and thus mechanical toughness), and the bulk modulus, shear modulus, and Poisson’s ratio.

column7: AJ12 - General Science and Technology R&D Services; General science and technology; Applied Research
column17: CENTERS FOR DISEASE CONTROL AND PREVENTION
column20: CENTERS FOR DISEASE CONTROL AND PREVENTION
column29:
Detailed description of the product or service being acquired. The Bruker X-Ray Fluorescence Spectrometer (S2 Puma) can detect elements as light as carbon. The competitor (Malvern) is limited to elements heavier than Fluorine. Limiting to elements lighter than fluorine prevents identification of many minerals of interest in mine dust. This S2 Puma is operable in air, helium purge gas, or vacuum.
Elements lighter than Na must be measured in vacuum. Operation in vacuum will enhance the sensitivity for all light elements. Samples can be measured prepared as loose powders, liquids, pressed pellets, or fused beads. Included in this offer are a 20 position X-Y changer, high performance XP EDX detector, touchscreen control, standardless analysis software, general purpose XRF measurement and analysis software, installation, and two days of on-site training.
Explanation of why the agency needs the product or service. The Respiratory Health Division, Field Studies Branch conducts field industrial surveys for the Spokane Mining Research Division for inhalation hazards, such as toxic gases, in support of health hazard evaluations and National Occupational Research Agenda projects. No energy dispersive X-Ray Fluorescence Spectrometer other than the Bruker S2 Puma can give NIOSH researchers the flexibility to detect elements as light as Carbon and as Heavy as Americium. This will enable accurate quantification of minerals within mineralogically complex mine dusts and thereby enable the development of a monitor that can accurately quantify respirable crystalline silica in real-time. The development of a real-time silica monitor works toward the Mining Program’s Strategic Intermediate Goal 1.1: Workplace solutions are adopted to reduce miner overexposure to hazardous airborne dust and diesel contaminants.

(1) Market Research consisted of:
 Searched the following term through the GSA Federal Supply Schedule: “X-Ray Fluorescence Spectrometer” “S2 Puma System” resulted in no contractor for this item.
 Market research activities included searching Mandatory Sources:
(1) the Federal Prison Industries (UNICOR), no results;
(2) Procurement List maintained by the Committee for Purchase from People Who Are Blind or Severely Disabled (AbilityOne), no results; and
(3) GSA Advantage Federal Supply Schedule, no results.
 A review of sam.gov indicates there are currently no applicable FASCSA orders for CDC and there are currently no internal orders.
 A notice of intent to sole source was posted on SAM.gov on 31 July 2024, and no inquiries were received for this posting.
 The Bruker X-Ray Fluorescence Spectrometer (S2 Puma) is manufactured in Malaysian or Germany. Because it is the only product suitable for the Spokane Mining Research Division’s needs, there are no domestically manufactured alternatives. The Bruker X-Ray Fluorescence Spectrometer (S2 Puma) is a commercially available off-the-shelf (COTS) item.

Bruker AXS LLC is the sole source manufacturer of the X-Ray Fluorescence Spectrometer (S2 Puma), which can detect elements as light as carbon. As such, they are the sole authorized provider of preventive maintenance services for this instrument and the exclusive source for material or service contracts to these instruments. Bruker is not a Small Business under NAICS 541713.

column5: Liquid Chromatograph-Gas Chromatograph w/Flame Ionization Detector
column29:
acquisition of a liquid chromatograph (LC) coupled to a gas chromatograph (GC) for the analysis of mineral oil hydrocarbons (MOH) and sterols in fats and oils.

The requested equipment will replace an existing aged gas chromatograph system that is not capable of performing LC-GC analysis of MOH. The salient characteristics are summarized in the Specific Tasks.
In brief, the requested LC-GC equipment shall:
1. Be capable of isolating by LC the MOSH and MOAH contained in an oil or fat sample, automatically transfer them to the head of two GC columns, provide parallel quantification of MOSH and MOAH using FID detection.
2. Be delivered ready to perform MOSH and MOAH analyses according to method DIN 16995/DGF C-VI 22 in a single chromatographic analysis.
3. Be furnished with a kit that permits the setup of the system for sterols analysis without the need for optimizations or purchase of other components.
4. Include an HPLC with ultra-violet (UV) detection, an interface transferring analytes from LC to GC, a GC, a robotic autosampler, an extension kit for the epoxidation of olefin prior to LC separation, an extension kit for the aluminum oxide purification post LC of MOSH, a controller unit, a software to collect data from the two parallel GC separations, a software to process the MOSH and MOAH GC-FID chromatograms, and a fraction collector.
5. Be managed by a single controller unit capable of operating the entire system, which shall be furnished with pre-loaded methods for MOSH/MOAH and sterol analyses.
6. Be capable of performing automated epoxidation of olefins prior to the LC separation.
7. Be capable of performing purification of the MOSH fraction from biogenic olefins by elution through an aluminum oxide column.
8. Be capable of performing saponification of lipids and isolation of unsaponifiable matter using the robotic autosampler.
9. Be capable of collecting fractions for analysis with other techniques as GCxGC-MS.

column29:
The exploration of brain regions known to contribute to reward-related behaviors requires different electrode designs depending on the exact region to be studied. The specified purchase (Plexon V-probes) is for electrodes that have been designed and tested for reaching deep structures in nonhuman primate, like the amygdala, but still suitable for superficial cortical recordings in nonhuman primates. Further, the specific versions of the Plexon V-probes in this request have either a rotated connector or a pig-tail tether for the connector. They allow more probes to be used in very tight spaces. The specified electrodes are required for ongoing studies of reward-related circuitry, an essential component of the NIMH mission to understand and develop treatments for behavioral disorders. 

Plexon Inc 32-contact electrode with 90 degree rotated connector (92078-010), 32-contact electrode with 51 mm pig-tail tether (92078-012),
5.	Salient characteristics 
•	Passive electrodes for deep recordings in nonhuman primates
•	32 contact linear electrode arrays
•	100% Plexon V-probe compatible
•	Sufficiently robust to tolerate 20 to 40 or more penetrations
•	Validated for our application
•	120 mm overall length to reach deep structures
•	Omnetics connector wired to match Ripple Nano head stages
•	Omnetics connector at 90 degrees (type 1)
•	Omnetics connector on 51 mm pig-tail tether (type 2) 

Dr. Bruno Averbeck’s Section on Learning and Decision Making (SLDM) of the Intramural
Research Program (IRP) of the National Institute of Mental Health (NIMH) records a large
numbers of neurons simultaneously from specific brain locations in nonhuman primates during
behavior. This is a highly specialized activity (called electrophysiology) that requires months,
often more than a year, of preparation and customization of equipment. Much of this preparation
centers around the electrodes used for recording in the brain. The technology of the electrodes is
intimately associated with the specialized electronic and mechanical apparatus used in the
experiment. The Plexon V-probes are the only electrodes used in the present, ongoing
experiments. The need is for additional electrodes of the exact same characteristics so as to not
disrupt these experiments. The experience shows that changes in electrode types can lead to
disruptions, which can have a major impact on the research. Changes in electrode type impacts
the timing of preparation, activities associated with precise positioning of the electrode, quality
of signals, and delays during recording. These adverse events would not be acceptable. Thus,
these specific electrodes are essential to fulfil the requirements of this research.

This equipment is necessary to determine the performance of these optoelectronic materials as quantum sensors. The measurements require spatial resolution that is not achievable without coupling them to an optical microscope. Temperature control is essential to this research. In many cases, the sensor performance improves dramatically at reduced temperatures due to a decrease in phonon noise. While helium cooling would be prohibitively expensive for these sensors to be used commercially, liquid nitrogen is acceptably priced. Additional research will require elevated temperatures. This includes optical measurements of color centers in wide band gap semiconductors for power electronics operating at temperatures up to 600 °C.  Fine control of temperature is required to develop optical thermometry at high temperatures. The need for optical measurements of electronic devices necessitates that the optical cryostat and heating stage has four built-in connections for electrical measurements. The sample holder must also be small enough to be easily incorporated into our existing spectroscopic microscopes. The new temperature-control capability afforded by this equipment will support the development and advancement of optical, electrical, and magnetic spectroscopic measurement methods for nanoscale characterization of materials and will apply this knowledge to nanoscale devices. This new characterization capability is an essential component of the NDCD’s ongoing efforts to develop color centers and excitonic materials as a platform for future quantum sensing technologies.

The NIST Physical Measurement Lab, Sensor Science Division, Thermodynamic Metrology Group

supports calibration of all types of contact thermometers up to 2370 K. The Standard Platinum
Resistance Thermometer Calibration Laboratory (SPRTCL) provides critical national and
international stakeholders with International System of Units (SI) traceability to the kelvin.
Government, military, pharmaceutical, aerospace, and high‐level industrial calibration laboratories
depend on the low uncertainties and direct SI traceability provided by the SPRTCL. As part of the
realization of the ITS‐90 temperature scale, the zinc (Zn) and aluminum (Al) fixed points require
high‐temperature heat pipe furnaces. In order for NIST to offer services for the calibration of

metrology furnaces to maintain NIST‐specific Zn and Al cells at their respective

column5: Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP) – manufactured Monoclonal Antibody
column29:
UB-421 is a monoclonal antibody which is being used to treat individuals living with HIV (PLWH) carrying multidrug resistant virus (MDR). NIH, NIAID, LIR is currently treating two patients harboring MDR HIV at the NIH Clinical Center(Institutional Review Board #IRB001672 and IRB001927) under an expanded access program approved by the Food and Drug Administration (Investigational New Drug #169181 and 166203).  United Biopharma, located in Taiwan, is the patent holder for UB-421,the only company that possesses the cell line that needs to be used to produce UB-421 and as a result the only known source to produce this drug. These Study participants have no viable HIV treatment options because they developed resistant virus to many, if not all, the FDA-approved drugs. The LIR initiated the treatment program in March 2023 to prolong the lives of these two patients using an optimized drug regimen that included UB-421. To continue the lifesaving treatment for these two patients, the LIR must request United Biopharma, a foreign corporation, to manufacture a lot of UB-421 (100 liters) and perform the FDA-mandated stability tests of the new lot. If the drug UB-421 is not acquired and the use is discontinued, the HIV viral load will rebound on these two patients and their life expectancy will dramatically be shortened. Additionally, the LIR has an ongoing clinical trial entitled “A trial of anti-CD4 antibody UB-421 in combination with optimized background antiretroviral therapy in patients with multidrug-resistant HIV-1infection”. This study was approved by the NIH Institutional Review Board and the FDA (Clinicaltrials.gov ID NCT05582694, https://clinicaltrials.gov /study/NCT05582694?term=UB-421&rank=5). The purpose of this clinical trial is to evaluate the efficacy of UB-421 and to seek FDA approval to treat PLWH harboring MDR HIV who do not have any viable treatment option with UB-421.
To continue this ongoing clinical trial, it is necessary to obtain clinical grade per the FDA’s mandate. As explained above, only United Biopharma can manufacture UB-421 because United Biopharma holds the patent on this antibody and United Biopharma is the only company that possesses the cell line that needs to be used to produce UB-421.

The primary goal of the Section on Growth and Obesity is to elucidate the genetic underpinnings of the metabolic and behavioral endophenotypes that contribute to the
development of obesity in children. Using an integrated program of basic and clinical translational research, our ongoing studies of patients with monogenic and polygenic
obesity and volunteer children of all weight strata aim to advance our understanding of energy balance regulation during childhood. Using their unique longitudinal cohorts of
children who have undergone intensive metabolic and behavioral phenotyping, they examine genetic and phenotypic factors predictive of progression to adult obesity in
children who are in the “pre-obese” state, allowing characterization of phenotypes unconfounded by the impact of obesity itself. Once identified as linked to obesity, genetic
variants that impair gene function are studied intensively. These approaches are expected to improve their ability to predict which children are at greatest risk for obesity and its
comorbid conditions and to lead to more targeted, etiology-based prevention and treatment strategies for pediatric obesity.

column29: This is a sole source requirement for Bio-Rad’s OPUS DX instruments required to detect influenza and SARS-CoV-2 from clinical specimens. The instrument is FDA-approved (IVD-cleared) rRT-PCR that can be used in conjunction with the CDC FDA-approved and FDA-authorized in vitro diagnostics for detection of Influenza and SARS-CoV-2 viruses from clinical samples. This instrument uses a distinct method for collecting the data in each well that is unique to BioRad instruments. Furthermore, Bio-Rad’s OPUS DX uses a 96-well block with a scanning method for data collection. It can multiplex up to 5 targets, allowing the CDC to adapt FDA-approved and authorized IVD and EUA diagnostics for influenza and SARS-CoV-2 to run on this instrument. BioRad will manufacture supplies and provide services to the BioRad OPUS Dx. The government is specifically purchasing the BioRad OPUS rRT-PCR system to evaluate the performance of our FDA-cleared and FDA-approved in vitro diagnostic assays for influenza and SARS-CoV-2 on this instrument.

column5: Brand name or Equal: JuliStage real time live cell imaging system
column29:
The National Institutes of Health (NIH) is an agency of the Department of Health and Human Services and the Federal focal point for health research. Its mission is to seek fundamental knowledge about the nature and behavior of living systems and apply that knowledge to enhance health, lengthen life, and reduce illness and disability. 

The NTDU routinely images live cells to determine viability and response to treatment with a variety of drugs. Such imaging experiments are part of the core workflow of the NTDU and often are used as assays in compound screens to identify drugs that may have potential as neurotherapeutics. 

The JuliStage can be placed in an incubator with the appropriate conditions to allow growth of cultured cells. The software purchased with the JuliStage allows for the timed monitoring of cells for prolonged periods of times. As the cells that the NTDU works with are engineered to expressed blue, green, and red fluorescent proteins detectors for each of these three are needed to monitor cells. 

The instrument that is being purchase has detectors for each of these fluorescent proteins and as such will be compatible with the experimental needs of the NTDU. It is anticipated that the JuliStage will receive heavy usage for determining the effects of a variety of treatments on mammalian cells used at the NTDU and also as secondary confirmation of drug screen hits that are identified at the Unit.

The NINDS CNS focuses on circuit and cellular properties of dopaminergic neurons located
in the midbrain and is interested in the physiology of a subset of dopamine neuron located in
the substantia nigra pars compacta (SNc) that undergo selective cell death in Parkinson’s
patients. Specifically, the laboratory’s staff are interested in understanding how synaptic and
intrinsic conductance differs among subpopulations dopamine neurons with the hope that this
may provide insight to why SNc dopamine neurons are particularly vulnerable in Parkinson’s
disease.
To identify novel physiological and genetic markers in single SNc dopamine neurons which
may be useful in defining subpopulations of these neurons in a meaningful way by
conducting microscopy to analyze mouse brain sections, the brand-name item that meets
NINDS need is Evident Scientific, Inc.’s Automated VS200 Slide Scanner System.

the Government’s minimum acceptable standards for an equivalent system to meet the
government’s objective are as follows:
