The Unit on Computational Decision Neuroscience (UCDN) at NIMH conducts research on state- 
dependent effects on decision-making. One of the central areas of focus of this laboratory’s 
research program is the study of the mechanisms behind the effect that negative emotional valence 
states and pain have on decision-making behavior in individuals with and without mental disorders. 
This research requires very precise control of stimulus features. Pain must be manipulated in a 
precise and reliable way that creates emotions that are both genuine and safe. The UCDN requires a 
device that possesses the precise level of control needed to generate such manipulations and 
analysis.

The UCDN at NIMH is a new lab focusing on characterizing factors that affect decision-making in 
mental disorders such as mood disorders, substance use, and chronic pain. It is part of the Emotion 
and Development Branch (EDB). A substantial amount of CDNU research will involve eliciting negative 
valence states in healthy volunteers as well as in clinical populations and characterizing how 
these states may affect decision-making. These negative valence states include negative emotion and 
pain. UCDN is designing several tasks for this purpose which are conducted on healthy and clinical 
populations of different age groups. Precise thermal pain stimulation will be central to many of 
our behavioral studies as will the ability to combine these paradigms with simultaneous neural and 
physiological recordings. To this end, UCDN requires additional Thermosensory Stimulator
Thermodes (“Thermodes”) compatible with our current Medoc TSA2 thermal stimulation device.

The UCDN requires additional Thermodes for use with its Government-owned Medoc TSA2 device to 
generate thermal pain and evaluate how decision-making is affected. UCDN requires four (4) unique 
thermode parts for generating thermal pain stimulation to examine decision-making in healthy 
volunteers and clinical populations in Building 10. The goal of this research is to characterize 
behavioral, neural, and computational mechanisms that lead to changes in valuation after pain 
induction. A key requirement for the research is the pain stimulation rate must be fast enough to 
allow recording of evoked neural responses while using neurophysiological or neuroimaging 
techniques.

column17: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF STANDARDS AND TECHNOLOGY

column29: This acquisition is for the Laser Cooling and Trapping Group, in the Quantum Measurement Division of the Physical Measurements Laboratory and is for a frequency stabilized laser system serving the capture and cooling of Yb+ ions. The capture of ions is an essential routine step in the operation of a noise‐enabled quantum computer based on trapped ions, currently under construction at the Joint Quantum Institute. The research goals of this apparatus range from practical tests of noise‐aware quantum error correction and quantum error modeling to fundamental studies of open quantum systems. The frequency of these lasers must be tuned to address specific electronic transitions of neutral Yb and Yb+ ions. The frequency of both lasers must be stabilized to a suitable linewidth to address the bandwidth of these electronic transitions. Further, the frequency must be independently reported by an interferometer‐based wavemeter which may be calibrated to an absolute frequency reference.

column5: Molecular Devices, LLC Digidata 1550B1 Low Noise Data Acquisition system
column29: Research in the National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke (NINDS), Neurovascular Research Unit focuses on different neurovascular coupling mechanisms in control of the blood flow in the brain. This Unit also examines functional deficits in molecular mechanisms in the small vessel diseases of the brain and Vascular Cognitive Impairment & Dementia (VCID) including Stroke and Alzheimer’s disease. We rely on functional imaging approaches (in-vivo and ex-vivo) and electrophysiology to answer scientific questions. To study the signaling properties of cells that comprise the neurovascular system, this laboratory uses patch clamp electrophysiology, which allows its researchers to perform precise measurements of ion channel activities in different vascular cells. To record the activity associated with individual ion channels, there is a need for a high-precision digitizer system for collecting data through the acquisition software pClamp11, which will allow the laboratory to accurately measure sub millivolt (very small) changes in the membrane potential of cells associates with individual ion channel activity. The brand-name item the Unit identified that meets the need of this procurement is Molecular Devices, LLC Digidata 1550B1 Low Noise Data Acquisition system (Part Numbers DIGIDATA 1550B1 and PCLAMP 11 STANDARD), which relies on imaging capabilities to study human stem cell-derived neurons.

The National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke (NINDS), Laboratory of Circuits, Synapses, and Molecular Signaling (LCSMS) studies fundamental issues of synaptic transmission, neuronal excitability, neuromodulation, and neural circuit activity using animal models (mouse and monkey). These issues are related to human neurological disorders, such as Alzheimer’s and other neurodegenerative disorders, and are explored in brain areas including (but not limited to) the amygdala, basal forebrain
cholinergic areas, the ventral hippocampus, and the prefrontal cortex. A major line of research employs patch-clamp recording systems to interrogate cellular properties in the brain. Therefore, NINDS has identified a need for Scientifica LLC brand-name patch-clamp recording manipulator system to further its ongoing research, in which single cell responses can be recorded from a glass electrode held by the manipulators of the system. This will enable the laboratory to continue accelerating its critical research studies
related to human neurological disorders for enhancing data quality and gain deeper insights into complex mechanisms underlying neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer’s, ultimately contributing to advancements in biomedical science.

The National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases (NIAID),Vaccine Research Center (VRC) Virology Laboratory comprises the Virology, Humoral Immunology, and Structural Biology Sections and related Cores. The goal of the Virology Laboratory is to conduct research to understand better the cellular and molecular regulation of viral gene expression, viral entry into the host cell, optimization of immune responses to gene-based vaccination and correlates of immune protection, assessment of active and passive approaches for HIV-1 vaccine development, and to apply structural biology techniques toward rational vaccine design for emerging and re-emerging infectious diseases. The VRC is developing vaccines for HIV-1 and other viruses. Currently, SBS/SBIS is working on developing vaccines forSARS-CoV-2. Production of wild-type and mutant viral proteins and anti-viral antibodies is critical in vaccine development. Making specific immunogens as probes is critical to isolate antibodies and develop therapeutic agents in the Covid19 treatment. The requested items are consumables for the Mabtech™ Reader Systems already in use with the VRC. This FluoroSpotPlus kit is intended for the enumeration of cells secreting monkey IFN-γ using the FluoroSpotassay for use in research. This kit is based on a matched pair of mAbs enabling specific detection of IFN-γ from non-human primates (NHP). The mAbs react with human IFN-γ and cross-react with IFN-γ from NHP. Interferon-γ (IFN-γ) is the only type II interferon. This pro inflammatory cytokine is secreted by activated T cells and NK cells. It activates macrophages and endothelial cells and regulates immune responses by affecting APCs, T cells, and B cells. Production of IFN-γ by helper T cells and cytotoxic T cells is a hallmark of the Th1-type phenotype. Thus, high-level production of IFN-γ is typically associated with effective host defense against intracellular pathogens. Interferon-γ (IFN-γ) is the only type II interferon. column29: National Marine Fisheries Service - Alaska Fisheries Science Center - needs to procure two (2) Sonardyne water and pressure-proof wideband USBL transponders for offshore (continental shelf) fish and crab stock assessments. The AFSC RACE habitat research group (HRG) conducts research at the continental-shelf scale to address the Congressional EFH mandates, the HAIP priority for habitat-based enhanced stock assessments, as well as strategically important ocean-mapping under Presidential directive, using innovative methods and the contributions of world-class partners. Current projects include: (1) Evaluating sonars as a quantitative tool for explaining the basin-scale distribution and abundance of ground fish (2) Seabed mapping to develop a habitat-based catchability function for the Bristol Bay red king crab (Paralithodes camtschaticus) stock assessment (3) and Seabed mapping to quantify untrawlable habitats & reduce bias in Gulf of Alaska stock assessments.

The TNC’s responsibility is to aid other labs, primarily at NINDS but also throughout the NIH, in translating their basic science discoveries into potential therapeutics.  One technology the TNC is using to better understand therapeutic targets in single-cell (sc)-RNAseq.  The group has piloted Parse Biosciences scRNAseq protocol and found that it works well.  The protocol will be used at larger scale and in order to improve precision, accuracy, and efficiency it needs to be automated. NIST requires one (1) Entangled Photon Source (EPS) that emits entangled photon pairs in the telecom O-band. The EPS will assist NIST in achieving the goal of developing a measurement and testbed infrastructure to test and validate the performance of optical quantum components on the NIST Gaithersburg campus. NIST currently has existing entangled photon sources for the telecom C-band around 1550 nm. In order to test and verify entanglement distribution also in the important telecom O-band, NIST requires the purchase of such a source with photons being created in the O-band. Acquisition of the subject required EPS will allow for NIST to implement the detection and quantum state metrology required to detail the polarization performance of the links within the testbed fiber infrastructure. The required 1320 nm EPS shall meet the following minimum technical specifications: 
• Photon generation centered at 1320 ± 2 nm. 
• The bi-photon bandwidth shall be greater than 30 nm and less than 100 nm. 
• Photon pair rate >500,000 pairs/s at the source. 
• Polarization-entanglement visibility >97%. 
• Photon-heralding probability >30%. 
• Photon Output Fibers must be single mode fibers.

column29:
This procurement is for the igus® LCA Room Gantry system – a cartesian robotic arm system with drylin® technology.

The Program Office has a requirement to build a robotic arm that simulates a worker performing industrial grinding to evaluate the dust particles released. The program aims to study if the addition of novel carbon-based nanomaterials as an additive in concrete contributes to further exacerbation of pulmonary hazards and toxicity in construction workers handling these novel composites. 

The igus® LCA Room Gantry is the only type of robotic arm that has drylin® technology. Drylin® technology uses a base polymer with fiber filaments and solid lubricants instead of stainless-steel roller bearings. The base polymer with fiber filaments provides wear resistance and the use of solid lubricants reduces friction amongst the system. The elimination of the use of oil and grease in drylin® technology eliminates the occurrence of dust and particles adhering to collected concrete particles being released into the working environment from the robotic arm, allowing for a clean evaluation of the overall dust that is released. The use of drylin® technology also eliminates odor from being generated in the working environment.

Drylin® technology is required for the Program's current research study because this type of technology doesn’t require the use of lubricants that can contaminate the collected concrete particles that will be studied. This technology prevents aerosol contamination from lubricants to mix with particulate generated from occupational material handling (an object of interest for the current research study). Without the use of drylin® technology, there exists the potential that particles from the robotic arm and its lubricants will mix with the concrete particulate generated during a grinding motion, which would make it impossible to distinguish the hazard component particulate of the overall dust generated. This would negatively impact the Program's study to evaluate if the addition of novel nanomaterials can further exacerbate the toxicity in construction workers that work with these types of materials.

The NIST facility for carrying out protection-level beta dosimetry
calibrations has been operational for several decades with a major
upgrade of instrumentation and sources in 1998 (BSS2 system). This is
the date when the radioactive sources (Sr-90 and Kr-85) currently used

measurement results in the form of longer integration times and
increased measurement uncertainties. New Kr-85 and Sr-90
radioactive sources will be acquired for the BSS2 protection-level beta
calibration facility to improve efficiency and uncertainties of calibration
measurements.

column5: Opentrons Flex Workstation (96 Channel) w/ Installation and Service Plan
column29:
The National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases (NIAID) Viral Epidemiology and Immunity Unit are conducting a Phase I clinical trial wherein healthy adults with no (naïve), one (primary homotypic or heterotypic), or more than one (multitypic) previous natural dengue virus (DENV) infection(s) will be immunized with the DENV3 monovalent vaccine (rDEN3d30/31-7164) developed by LID/NIH/CRL. The aim is to characterize how pre-vaccine host immunity influences the responses to monovalent DENV3 vaccination and determines protective immunity. It is hypothesized that broad protection following secondary dengue exposure arises from a two-part process: 1) primary infection produces low-affinity cross-reactive antibodies and MBCs that can enhance disease while 2) secondary infection activates these MBCs to undergo affinity maturation and develop potent cross-reactive, protective MBCs and antibodies that neutralize even previously unexposed serotypes. Here, it is proposed to safely model primary and sequential DENV infections with a monovalent dengue vaccine.  Numerous components of the trial require high-throughput immunological assays that are highly labor-intensive.  Currently there is no liquid handler in the lab to aid in high-throughput serosurveys, a core component of our lab work.  The Opentrons liquid handler will be a workhorse for the trial as well as other ongoing projects in the lab, including work to characterize antigenic diversity among dengue viruses as part of a climate change grant that was recently funded.

The Viral Epidemiology and Immunity Unit is interested in procuring the Opentrons Flex Prep Workstation, 96 Channel to enable high-throughput serological assays needed in for the lab to generate large datasets of serological titers for use in clinical trials, large observational cohort studies, and techniques like antigenic cartography.   The Opentron Flex Workstation will enable the lab to conduct numerous complex workflows including neutralization assays and ELISAs, enabling much higher throughput than what they are currently able to do with just standard multichannels alone.

The National Institutes of Health (NIH), National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases (NIAID), conducts and supports basic and applied research to better understand, treat, and ultimately prevent infectious, immunologic, and allergic diseases. Within the LID, the Viral Epidemiology and Immunity Unit (VEIU) uses a multidisciplinary approach to investigate immunological protection against and susceptibility to emerging and re-emerging viral diseases with the goal of informing how vaccines can be effectively and safely licensed and introduced through vaccination programs.  The VEIU has a current requirement for Cytokine 30-plex Human Panel multiplex ELISA-based assay (Luminex) to measure the cytokines and chemokines in order to 1) comprehensively describe the immune biomarkers and kinetics in response to vaccination 2) elucidate the key immunological factors responsible for establishing an enhancing, proviral immune environment in place of a protective, antiviral one, thus enabling the identification of novel biomarkers of protection and risk 3) calibrate our mathematical model of innate and adaptive responses to contrast the mechanisms initiating and sustaining adaptive immune responses during primary infection with the immune pathways that are pre-existing and recalled/reactivated during secondary and subsequent infections.

2

All seven items are required for a fully functioning Gas ChromoGraphy- ElectroAntennograpic Detection (GC-EAD) system.
All equipment pieces must communicate and operate on the same software system. 
The desired equipment is extremely unique, in that it is highly specialized and custom-designed to objectively measure the activation of individual neurons within the insect brain. The neuronal activation can be experimentally determined because the equipment is capable of measuring changes in electrical gradients across cellular membranes at an ultrafine scale. There do exist other types of electrophysiological equipment that have similar function and capabilities in general, however these other pieces of equipment are designed to measure electrical gradients on samples or specimens that are not of comparable scales as the insect brain. For example, other relatively comparable equipment options are capable of measuring these electrical gradients in intact frog eggs (which are >10,000 times the size of insect brain cells). As such, while similar in function, this other equipment is not technologically capable of conducting these types of studies on insects because of the physical characteristics (i.e., physical size) of the electrophysiological equipment. Briefly put, the salient characteristics that make this equipment the only option are 1) the highly sophisticated technical capabilities of the equipment, and 2) the micro-scale of the equipment, which allows it to be used to conduct electrophysiological studies on micro-scales, and 3) the fact that it is purpose-built to facilitate the measurement of olfactory-sensory neuron activation in the insect brain. No other equipment in the world has these characteristics, and no other available equipment can be used to perform these functions.

column29:
The Occupational Allergy Team
has been tasked to assess the immunological and toxicological responses to fungi
through an interagency agreement with the National Institute for Environmental
Health Sciences (NIEHS) as part of a National Toxicology Program research
activity. These studies involve murine exposures to fungi using a unique
acoustical generation system that mimics the aerosolization and inhalation of dry
fungal spores and processing murine tissues, including lungs, brains, and lymph
nodes, for downstream analysis of immune responses. One critical analysis is the
assessment of cellular immune responses in the lung and lymph node tissues via
flow cytometry. Following murine necropsy, these tissues must be immediately
processed and dissociated to create single cell suspensions that are then stained and analyzed via flow cytometry. To successfully perform the dissociation in
real-time as mice are being necropsied, researchers in the Occupational Allergy
Team require the gentleMACS Octo Dissociator with Heaters unit to allow for
automated and reproducible dissociation. This gentleMACS Octo Dissociator with
Heaters unit is an isolation system that is required to allow consistent and
reproducible processing of tissue samples. The gentleMACS Octo Dissociator with
heaters offers full automation for tissue dissociation and is enabling the dissociation
up to eight samples at a time, without cross contamination and meeting the labs
requirements by providing the required performance in standardization of sample
processing from tissue to single cell suspension with high yield and viability. It also
integrates connectivity features for traceability, which significantly increases the
efficiency and quality of sample preparation. This gentleMACS Octo Dissociator
with Heaters is also required to dissociate cells from various organs such as spleen,
lymph nodes, brain, kidney etc. and make a single cells suspension and purification
for studying the function of specific immune cells population during inflammation,
autoimmune disease or after cellular activation with various stimuli. The unique
required specifications for this instrument are tubes that bear a unique rotor/stator
system for the gentle and efficient tissue dissociation or homogenization and the
gentleMACS Dissociators are the only known devices that in combination with the
gentleMACS Tubes can be used to dissociate or homogenize various tissues in a
closed system, and process multiple samples at once without cross contamination.
The team has utilized the gentleMACS Octo Dissociator with Heaters in previous
studies that is owned by another researcher at NIOSH located in a separate building.
As the need for this piece of equipment has increased, repeatedly transporting
samples out of the lab has introduced the potential for sample contamination as well
as reduction in cell viability during transport. The only source and instrument known
to meet these specifications is the gentleMACS Octo Dissociator with Heating Unit,
which is manufactured by Miltenyi Biotec in Germany.

column29: As part of the National Institute of Standards and Technology's (NIST) research into CHIPS, the Material Measurements Laboratory (MML), Material Measurements Science Division, Nanomechanical Properties Group (NPG) requires three lock-in amplifiers to meet CHIPS metrology project deliverables. NIST is constructing a custom Time Domain Thermoreflectance (TDTR) instrument. In TDTR, a modulated ultrafast laser “pump” pulse heats the sample and a delayed pulsed “probe” beam interrogates the thermal properties of thin film or multilayered materials as a function of the probe delay time. Lock-in amplifiers are used to detect the thermal response signal of the probe beam, which acquires the same modulation frequency of the modulated pump beam.

The Laboratory of Neuropsychology investigates higher brain functions such as learning, memory, and reward-related decision making. These studies require electrodes to explore deep structures in the nonhuman primate brain to evaluate the relationship between these structures and primate behavior.  

Background Information and Objective:  The exploration of brain regions known to contribute to reward-related behaviors requires different electrode designs depending on the exact region to be studied. Collecting data on large numbers of neurons provides critical data for computational modeling. As technology advances, the ability to collect large numbers improves. The specified purchase (Neuropixels probes) is for electrodes that have been designed and tested for collecting large numbers of simultaneously recorded neuronal data from deep structures in nonhuman primate. The specified electrodes are required for continuing studies of reward-related circuitry, an essential component of the NIMH mission to understand and develop treatments for behavioral disorders.

The Vaccine Immunology Program (VIP) has assembled functional groups that engage in basic and translational B cell immunobiology research (including high throughput automated immunoglobulin and transcriptome analyses) in addition to standardized end-point analyses for clinical trials. The goal was to bring these functional groups under a single program in order to facilitate the coordinated development and deployment of state-of-the-art technologies and analyses, which can be utilized effectively for vaccine
discovery, early development and testing of clinical products. Next generation sequencing (NGS) has aided in the rapid characterization of antibodies derived
from single cell analysis of vaccine trial participants. There are multiple components that are required for the efficient generation of amplified cDNA for sequencing single cell sorted plates: (1) disrupt cellular membranes for the release of RNA; (2) for the initiation of unbiased cDNA synthesis, allowing for amplification and library construction from any RNA transcript; (3) to prevent the degradation of the cellular RNA; and DNA Polymerase, a high-fidelity PCR enzyme with hot start capabilities that is especially well suited for use with specific SMARTer kits for transcriptome analysis (NGS). These componentshave been used within the VRC for several years with use increasing with the development of RAT-PIg, a high throughput antibody screening assay that relies on the SMARTSeq/NGS platform for the generation of matched sequencing data. These Smartseq reagent kits from Takara are specifically needed to perform highthroughput antibody sequencing within our lab and are not available from US sources. The protocols in the lab have been optimized for use with these specific reagents, and these protocols are used in multiple labs throughout the VRC, as these reagents are required for upcoming experiments within the lab.

The Genetic Engineering and Viral Vector Core (GEVVC) provides support to the National Institute on Drug Abuse (NIDA) Intramural Research Program (IRP) by developing and producing genetic tools capable of modulating and monitoring the molecules, cells, and circuits in the mammalian nervous system. These tools are employed by researchers within the NIDA-IRP to study brain function under physiological and pathological conditions. GEVVC's mission to produce viral vectors requires the capacity to prepare plasmid reagents at large scales while maintaining plasmid integrity and purity. In GEVVC’s current configuration, a full time contractor dedicates two full days per week preparing a maximum of four plasmids for the coming week’s virus production queue. The acquisition of an automated plasmid preparation robot would expand the core’s capacity to 12+ plasmids per week while reducing the required hands-on technician time provided by the contractor.

The mission of the NIDA IRP is to conduct state-of-the-art research on basic mechanisms that underlie drug abuse and addiction, and to develop new methods for the
treatment of drug abuse and addiction. Research is supported at the molecular, genetic, cellular, animal, and clinical levels and is conceptually integrated, highly innovative, and
focused on major problems in the field. The long-term goal of the research is to better understand the biological and behavioral factors contributing to initiation, maintenance,
and elimination of drug abuse and addiction (and associated diseases), and to translate this knowledge into improved strategies for preventing, treating, and reducing the negative consequences for the individual and for society caused by drug abuse and addiction.

The National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases (NIAID)’s Laboratory of Molecular Immunology (LMI) conducts basic, translational, and clinical studies related to innate and adaptive immune system function in health and disease. LMI scientists have made major contributions to our understanding of immunoregulation by chemokines and their G protein-coupled receptors, HIV pathogenesis, the NFkB family of transcription factors, mucosal immunology in the gut, reovirus and rotavirus infection in the gut, and mouse models of inflammatory bowel disease. They explore the basic properties of neutrophils, macrophages, naïve and memory T cells, and dendritic cells, as well as genetic risk factors for complex immune-mediated diseases. In LMI, current studies focus on the molecular pathogenesis of infectious and immunologic/inflammatory diseases, including West Nile virus infection, Listeria infection, Trypanosoma cruzi, Toxoplasma gondii, fungal infection, sepsis, atherosclerosis, psoriasis, inflammatory bowel disease, primary immunodeficiency disease, and cancer, working toward the goal of identifying novel therapeutic targets and strategies.

Agility to move from one application to another application with a smooth transition so the system does not have to stop and restart causing information to be lost. Quick change filter technology is critical when there are 40 filters in this equipment that can be changed quickly and easily with a filter slide which allows for the equipment to run uninterrupted.  The ergonomic design of the system provides full front access for all key operations. (e.g., plate loading, filter change and connecting reagents).  JET injector technology for highest accuracy, speed and cell-friendliness, Broad wavelength range selection from UV through the visible range, BRET/BRET2 and NanoBRET™. The SSD develops radiometers for quantifying the power of light in different regions of the electromagnetic spectrum, including the infrared and terahertz ranges. For sensing light in these regions outside of the visible spectrum, specialized cameras and specialized optics are required for capturing images. In addition to the camera, a terahertz lens and terahertz optical filter is necessary to make the camera system fully functional. The camera must be capable of capturing images in the terahertz frequency range, which can be used for viewing beams of terahertz radiation in our experiments. The SSD develops instruments and optical systems in the frequency range from 3 THz to 20 THz and requires a camera that can be used for characterization and alignment of the terahertz beams used in this research. In addition to the requirement for responsivity in the frequency range from 3 THz to 20 THz, the camera must have sufficient sensitivity to image the low power signals often used in SSD experiments.

100% COMPATIBLE USE WITH NANOASSEMBLR IGNITE microfluidic cartridges must be equipped with mixing technology for use on both the NanoAssembler™ Ignite™ and NanoAssembler™ Ignite+™ systems for nanoparticle formulation for the process development of lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) .Non-turbulent flow conditions for particle formation ensure reproducible results for a wide range of nanoparticle types. Automated in-line dilution versions allow modeling of the processes that will be required for future GMP production of nanoparticles.•	A single mixer reduces risk during scale-up while maintaining particle quality and reproducibility. These cartridges are specific to an instrument that require these specific cartridges listed to be from this manufactured by the requested manufacturer.  These proprietary cartridges are equipped with NxGen™ mixing technology for use on both the NanoAssembler™ Ignite™ and NanoAssembler™ Ignite+™ systems for nanoparticle formulation. The NxGen™ technology consists of a unique mixing architecture that includes toroidal structures within the flow path to ensure steady and consistent mixing conditions. This helps in the effective transfer of critical process parameters (CPPs) while scaling up by using our NanoAssemblr™ family of formulation systems, all of which are equipped with the same advanced NxGen™ technology. This minimizes process redevelopment and mitigates risk. The NanoAssemblr™ Blaze™ cartridges and tubing kits are single-use, reducing the risk of cross-contamination during process development of LNP formulations. In-line dilution of cartridges automates a key process, while the tubing kit enables external vessel connections.

In-line dilution: Automated, in-line dilution simplifies LNP workflows, saving time while ensuring particle stability. Scalable: Efficient technology transfer of key process parameters using NxGen™ technology during scale-up from preclinical development to clinical and commercial production, helping to ensure consistent critical quality attributes (CQAs). Flexible: NxGen™ 400 and NxGen™ 500 cartridges enable a range of flow rates from 4 – 115 mL/min using the same NxGen™ mixing architecture but in different dimensions, enabling flexibility for formulation-specific process parameters. Closed-system environment: The single-use tubing kit enables connection to external vessels and a closed-system environment on the NanoAssemblr™ Blaze+™ system, allowing optimization of manufacturing processes used during clinical development and minimizing risk.

The GLERL requires a Mini CO2 Digital Logger that will document the impacts or potential shifts in phytoplankton species in the Great Lakes. The M220 Mini CO2 Digital Logger uses infrared detection to measure the partial pressure of CO2 gas dissolved in liquids with a range from 0-1000 ppm up to 0-100%. The instrument is a compact, lightweight, plug and play sensor; and measures total dissolved gas pressure (TDGP). The housing is rated to 6000 dbar of hydrostatic pressure, and is resistant to most corrosive liquids. 

The M220 Mini CO2 Digital Logger includes the following components/specifications and is the only known product capable of meeting the needs of the GLERL. The GLERL is a multidisciplinary environmental research laboratory that provides scientific understanding to inform the use and management of Great Lakes and coastal marine environments. The GLERL uses sensors to collect and monitor the CO2 and acidification levels of the Great Lakes and provide data for HAB analysis. As atmospheric CO2 increases, the availability of dissolved inorganic carbon (DIC) is shifting within aquatic systems causing the pH to drop. The GLERL requires the M22 Mini CO2 Digital Logger in order to document the impacts or potential shifts in phytoplankton species composition and abundance.

The CO-RE Pipette Tips, manufactured by Hamilton Supplies in Switzerland. The Vaccine Immunology Program (VIP), at the Vaccine Research Center (VRC), supports the development of novel methodologies and optimization of assays, along with immunological basic research for the attainment of biologics and vaccines in the fight against infectious diseases such as Influenza, Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV), Ebola, Corona-virus (COVID-19) and other infectious diseases. The Pandemic Response Repository Microbial/Immune Surveillance and Epidemiology (PREMISE) and COVID-19 response needs high throughput, multiplexed serological and cellular assays as part of one of its main objectives that will be supported by the VIP laboratory. The VIP laboratory (GCLP compliant), is fundamental in supporting intramural and extramural clinical trials through method development and clinical sample testing with the generation of data for the advancement of treatments and vaccines for the above-named diseases. Currently, VIP has two Beckman automated liquid handlers and a new Hamilton Vantage workstation to perform high-throughput testing. This request would be for additional workstation pipette tips for performing experiments on the workstation. Salient Characteristics-
Essential for precise pipetting, Hamilton's 1000 μL CO-RE® II tips feature a fine, thin tip that facilitates dosing of micro volumes. The CO-RE tips possess a lock-and-key fit on each Hamilton pipette channel for high-precision results. The tight fit of the CO-RE tips allow for extreme accuracy and precision. A loose fit tip will have a flawed result and all the research would be compromised. The compatibility of the pipettes with the Liquid Handling Workstation allows for the Workstations to operate its Liquid Level Detection and Total Aspiration and Dispense Monitoring.  These CO-RE tips are essential for precise pipetting. Small variances in using other manufacturer pipette tips can make a big difference and compromise the workflow integrity and void the brand-new Vantage Liquid Handling Workstation’s warranty. The VIP requires pipette tip consumables for use on the Hamilton liquid handling workstation to fulfill the testing burden of the VRC. This workstation is new and still under warranty.  Therefore, any other manufacture’s consumables on this equipment will void the existing warranty. These requested items are consumables for the Hamilton Vantage Liquid Handling Workstation purchased from Hamilton Company 3 months ago.
This Vantage Automated Liquid Handling Workstation uses air displacement pipetting to achieve superior measurement accuracy. Additionally, Hamilton  pipette channels and tips are designed to fit precisely together using a patented lock-and-key design called CO-RE(Compressed O-Ring Expansion). The
required tips creates a tight seal with precise tip alignment to eliminate tip distortion and ensure the highest accuracy during liquid handling steps. The
anti-droplet control and liquid level detection technologies further safeguard samples and results integrity. These CO-RE Pipette Tips are a
consumable solely manufactured by Hamilton for the Vantage Automated Liquid Handling Workstation.

This request is to acquire two dissecting microscopes with custom configuration from a small business (W.E.L. Instruments) that can aid in research continuation with the National Institute of Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH) collaborators. Working with microvessels and tissue where further experimental investigations must be performed needs precision and agility. NIOSH collaborators who utilize the same configuration have provided the training to dissect and test various animal tissues including cardiovascular investigations involving hanging vascular microvessels using these microscope configurations. The selected microscopes have a 6.3-50x magnification, along with an additional front optic that can increase the working distance from 92 mm to 127 mm and has a magnification of 3.9-31.5x. Further, both microscopes will be set up on ball bearing boom stands to enable flexibility and mobility of the microscopes to different individuals’ height and working distance needs. These microscope specifications will enable NIOSH to be able to successfully collect tissue from fetal rodents or hang microvessels as small as 25 μm in internal diameter to assess vascular reactivity. These are unique requirements, and this specific small business (W.E.L. Instruments) has experience customizing the Zeiss microscopes (Original Equipment Manufacturer) to unique microvessel research needs.

These Stereo microscopes will be utilized by the NIOSH lab to evaluate the following:
1 - Cardiovascular and Gestational effects of Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances (PFAS).
2 - Pulmonary tumorigenicity potential of carbon nanotubes.
3 - Emerging Metal Exposures and Associated Immune and Carcinogenic Effects in the Lung.
4 - Combustion Source Mixtures: Characteristics and Investigation of Health Effects.
Some of these projects are ongoing and others are starting in October 2024. Therefore, if these specific stereo microscopes are not acquired, then NIOSH will not be able to perform some components of the proposed studies and other components will be delayed as the investigator needs to retrain/learn on alternate equipment.
The stereo microscopes are being configured, assembled, and sold by W.E.L. Instruments, a small business located in Pennsylvania, USA. (Qty 2) OS1010S-25BB Ball bearing boom stand and (Qty 2) OSK32MM 32mm tilt mount are manufactured in USA. column5: MOUSE CARDIAC ARRAY COIL FOR 7 TESLA MRI SCANNER
column29:
The National Institute on Aging (NIA) requires a mouse heart array coil for cardiac MRI at 7 Tesla. This coil will support cardiovascular studies of transgenic and wild type mice as a function of aging using NIA’s Biospec 70/30 MRI scanner.

On both clinical and preclinical MRI scanners, most imaging is performed using a combination of a body coil, which surrounds the subject and transmits radio waves to excite nuclear spins, and a small surface coil or array, which receives the resulting radio-frequency signal from the area of interest, e.g. the heart. On NIA’s Bruker Biospec 70/30 MRI scanner, the receive-only coil is an array with a size and shape tailored to fit the anatomy of interest. We have such array coils for mouse head and body imaging but not for cardiac imaging. Matching the size, shape and location of the mouse heart is important for receiving signal from the entire heart while maximizing signal-to-noise ratio. Given the small size of the mouse heart, its rapid contraction cycle and the limited time available to scan live animals under anesthesia, having a receive-only coil optimized for mouse cardiac imaging is key to generating reliable results for quantitative analysis. Since studies of cardiac aging in mice often hinge on detecting subtle differences in chamber volumes, wall thicknesses and muscle composition, we feel that purchasing a dedicated mouse cardiac coil is justified by our research needs.

The Laboratory of Infectious Diseases (LID) has a long history of vaccine development and identification of new agents of viral diseases. LID is noted for undertaking high-risk, high-re ward programs that require extraordinary time and resource commitments, such as programs to develop vaccines for viral hepatitis, severe childhood respiratory diseases, viral gastroenteritis, flaviviruses, and herpes viruses. Within the LID is the Caliciviruses Section focus on the role of human caliciviruses inhuman disease, with a focus on the development of prevention and control strategies for the noroviruses. The Caliciviruses Section requires a IntestiCult Organoid Growth Medium (Human), which is a cell culture medium for establishment and maintenance of human intestinal organoids. The IntestiCult Organoid Growth Medium (OGM) is a complete growth medium optimized for human intestinal organoids. It includes all necessary components for organoid culture, such as growth factors and supplements, eliminating the need for additional additives. This medium provides a robust environment for stem cell development, ensuring consistent and reproducible results, which are essential for reliable experimental outcomes. With this complete medium formulation and optimized protocol the lab can derive organoids from human intestinal crypts. Organoid growth across donor samples, including those that are other wise difficult to grow, is enabled by an enriched stem cell population. Organoids grown in IntestiCult Organoid Growth Medium (Human). incorporate a functional lumenen closed by a polarized intestinal epithelial cell layer and, for versatile modeling applications, can be further differentiated in 3D or in 2D as submerged monolayers or air-liquid interface (ALI) cultures using IntestiCult Organoid Differentiation Medium(Human). The mission need the item(s) are intended to satisfy include obtaining reference laboratory-quality results from the IntestiCult Organoid Growth Medium(Human). By using the application the lab can study the development and function of intestinal epithelium, modeling intestinal diseases, and screening molecules for both efficacy and toxicity in an intestinal model. Intestinal organoid cultures will also be used for investigation of adult stem cell properties and for regenerative therapy approaches. This is necessary in the lab’s experimentation.

column29:
In accordance with the mission of the National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health
(NIOSH) and the Nanotechnology Research Center (NTRC), to protect worker health and
safety, evaluating toxicity of workplace respiratory exposure to particulates is of great
concern. In addition, efforts are being made globally to reduce the number of animals used in
researching these exposures. The air-liquid interface is a newly emerging culture system for
toxicity evaluations that offers the most physiologically relevant culture system to mimic the
lung response observed in humans and in animal research. The custom package from SCIREQ
offers an all-in-one system for aerosol generation and exposure at the air-liquid interface in
cultures.

The items in this SCIREQ Exposure System are being procured through a single contract
with Scireq Scientific Respiratory Equipment (SCIREQ), Inc., located in Montreal, Canada,
under 75D30124P18937 for the amount of $142,370.00.

This procurement is for supplies that constitute an exposure system for toxicology studies to
characterize the risks associated with occupational exposures in direct relationship to the
mission of NIOSH. This exposure system allows the generation of wet and dry aerosols for
traditional and air-liquid interface culture systems. Accuracy of culture systems in predicting
in vivo outcomes has become a global priority to reduce the use of animals in research. The
system is unique in several features. The particle generator/delivery method does not use
electrostatic charging of the materials for exposure. This allows the investigator to perform a
test without altering the materials for the exposure, making that exposure as realistic to the
workplace as possible. It consists of a trademarked generation system, InExpose®, and
related supplies and equipment. It also comes with an ExpoCube®, that can be transferred
from the culture incubator to the exposure system without alteration which is beneficial to the
live cell culture. The software component optimizes particle concentrations and fluid
dynamic modeling for precision exposures.

This is the only system that does not alter a dry powder particulate exposure material during
delivery to a culture system. Without the waiver for this system, it will not be possible to
accurately predict pulmonary toxicity of dry powder exposures in culture.

column5: INCUBATORS - Kuhner Shaker LS-Z Shaker + Kuhner Kelvin Incubator
column29:
The National Institutes of Health (NIH), National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases (NIAID) conducts and supports basic and applied research to better understand, treat, and ultimately prevent infectious, immunologic, and allergic diseases.  Within the NIAID, the Laboratory of Immunogenetics (LIG) focuses on the cellular and molecular mechanisms that underlie the signaling functions of immune cells. This work encompasses a wide spectrum of experimental approaches from the structural determination of immune receptors to live-cell image analysis of the behavior of chemotactic receptors.

Within the LIG, the Antibody Biology Unit (ABU) aims to study antibody responses to infectious pathogens at the monoclonal level. Our two major goals are to study the antibody responses to vaccination and infection at high resolution, and to investigate the use of monoclonal antibodies for prevention of infection or as tools for vaccine design.   

The LIG/ABU is seeking two stackable floor mount incubator shakers with carbon dioxide and humidity control are vital pieces of equipment required for non-yeast cell cultures and one additional benchtop shaking incubator that is essential to provide the optimal environment to culture yeast cells and accommodate the expanded yeast-based experimental volume being performed. Both cell culture and yeast-specific cell culture is necessary to identify and isolate monoclonal antibodies against COVID-19.

column6: 314994 - Rope, Cordage, Twine, Tire Cord, and Tire Fabric Mills
column29:
The Alaska Fisheries Science Center(AKFSC) requires trawl materials and supplies that that will be used for survey trawl repairs and to construct trawls in preparation for surveys and research conducted in Alaskan waters. A variety of materials are used to construct and repair these trawls, including wire rope, floats, Poly web, and assorted hardware. These materials must be the same from year to year, and the specifications must remain the same over time to ensure standardization. The AKFSCRACE Division Research Fishing Gear Program is responsible for the construction and repair of all trawls used for both survey and research purposes. There are a variety of trawl types that all have different characteristics to meet the requirements of the survey or research to be completed. Rope and twine looming techniques are held to secrecy and patented, where each manufacturer produces the specific fibers, natural or synthetic, used for their product. The rope and twine fibers are twisted and braided together to create a specific tensile strength. The specific fibers used and the tensile strength applied by each manufacturer is distinct and unique. The rope and twine selected for the AKFSC trawl nets must support a certain amount of stress when stretched. The constructed trawl net receives considerable impact and force when fishing on the sea floor, where the average catches within the trawl net is approximately 1,100 -1,200 kg. It is imperative that the rope and twine is manufactured to withstand the stress and weight of both the bottom fishing and the resultant catch. 
The breakdown of material and country of origin are bulleted as follows:
 • Web Poly: 5mm dbl 5", 4 bails 350 lbs each100.5 md, 1400 LB: Peru 
• Twine: 60 round braid, 250 lbs in 5lb rolls, 250 LB: Peru
 • Twine: 69 round braid, 150 lbs in 5 lb rolls, 150 LB: Spain 
• Twine: 21 green twisted, green twisted in 200 lbs in 5 lb rolls, 200 LB: Spain 
• Poly Twine: 5mm green, 250 lbs in 5 lb rolls,250 LB: Peru 
• Poly Pro Mono: 1/2" 15 rolls, 15 EA: India 
• Poly ProMono: 3/8", 20 rolls, 20 EA: India 
• Poly Pro Mono: 1/4", 10 rolls, 10EA: India 
The total estimated value of the procurement is$70,595.00. The estimated aggregate value of the foreign made trawl material products is $16,782.50.

column7: 7644 - DIGITAL MAPS, CHARTS AND GEODETIC PRODUCTS
column29: The National Geodetic Survey (NGS) requires a precise and portable relative terrestrial gravimeter for executing relative gravity surveys in support of gravity field models associated with the National Spatial Reference System (NSRS), which the Office of NGS defines and maintains. The NSRS is a consistent coordinated system that defines latitude, longitude, height, scale, gravity, and orientation throughout the United States. In addition to a network of Continuously Operating Reference Stations (CORS) supporting three-dimensional positioning activities, the NSRS includes a network of permanently marked points; a consistent, accurate, and up-to-date national shoreline; and a set of accurate models describing dynamic, geophysical processes that affect spatial measurements. For this purpose, a portable gravimeter must achieve gravity measurements that are as accurate or are more accurate than our portable absolute gravimeter (Micro-g LaCoste A10 Absolute Gravimeter) that is capable of measuring gravity accurately to 10-7 m/s2 or better (Equivalent to 10 microGal or µGal).

GSA PBS Region 4 awarded a Contract to Brasfield & Gorrie, LLC (Contractor) (47PE0321C0003) for the bridging design build services for the construction of the new federal courthouse  in Huntsville, Alabama. The contract was awarded on August 10, 2021 Given the nature of the bridging design build delivery method, the specific requirements had not yet been designed at the time the contractor submitted its proposal. Therefore, the Contractor did not seek a pre-award exception to the Buy American statute for any of the inverter-driven ductless mini-split systems needed for the project. The Contractor is requesting a post award waiver for the procurement of four (4) ductless mini-split system HVAC units, in accordance with FAR Clause 52.225-9 “Buy American-Construction Materials.” The specific product requested is the Daikin Ductless Mini-Split System HVAC units. These ductless mini-split system HVAC units consist of individual and multiple-unit usage components which include:
· Four (4) outside compressors
· Five (5) selector boxes
· Twenty-Three (23) indoor units (15 wall units, 3 cassette units, 5 ducted) and a
· The control system for this product involves manufacture in multiple countries for individual parts to the system. The individual parts of Daikin systems are manufactured in the United States, Japan, Thailand, China, France, and Belgium. A price preference is not contemplated for this procurement in accordance with FAR Provision 52.225-12 “Notice of Buy American Requirement- Construction Materials”.
The ductless mini-split system HVAC units are not mined, produced, or manufactured in the United States of America (USA) as a whole system. The Contractor’s market research provided to GSA includes surveying its own supplier/market base, internet research, and historical information on the “nonavailability” posted in the Federal Register from other agencies (such as FTA). The HVAC mini-split air conditioning systems are required. The facility cannot use a standard split system unit, as an alternative to the ductless split system, because a standard system is incapable of treating ventilation air. Furthermore, standard split systems will not work for confined spaces such as server rooms and security/AV/telecom closets because standard split systems require space outdoors for the unit that houses the condenser and compressor in addition to indoor spaces that house the evaporator coil, air handler, and HVAC ductwork Standard split systems cannot be installed in confined locations that need temperature control to ensure continuity of operations for multiple tenants throughout the building. There is no domestic manufacturer that would satisfy the project needs for ductless mini-split system HVAC units. Based on the historic cost information currently available, the contracting officer has adequate information to ensure that the cost to the Government for this acquisition will be fair and reasonable. The attached BAA Waiver email request and submittals provided by the Contractor detail the equipment to be purchased. The total value of the four (4) Daikin ductless mini-split system HVAC units is $112,000.00.

A Milli-Q E7000 system offers a unique combination of optimized water purification and
monitoring technologies. This Milli-Q E7000 system is requested to replace an inoperable
reverse osmosis water purification system in the Toxicology and Molecular Biology Branch
(TMBB) within the Health Effects Laboratory Division (HELD), located in Morgantown,
West Virginia. The system needs to produce Ultra-Pure quality water that is suitable for
molecular biology-based research experiments. The requested system has been verified by
other groups within the Division to produce water compatible for these assays. The specific
items being procured are Millipore EQ 7000 water purification systems and consumables.
The purchase of this Milli-Q E7000 system is needed for multiple applications in the
Molecular Neurotoxicology Laboratory that require Ultra-Pure Reverse Osmosis (RO) water.
Specifically, the Molecular Neurotoxicology Laboratory uses highly sensitive biological
assays that can be sensitive to elemental contaminants and the presence of these
contaminants in the purified water used to prepare reagents has a cascade effect on the
measured accuracy and precision for these analytical measurements. The Milli-Q E7000
system has the lowest elemental concentrations of elements measured in Molecular
Neurotoxicology Laboratory (e.g., Pb, Hg, As, Ni) and elements that interfere with
analytical measurement of those same elements (e.g., Ca, Al, K). Due to these factors and
consistency with other systems in HELD, Milli-Q E7000 is the preferred system. The system
needs to produce Ultra-Pure quality water that is sufficient for molecular biology
experiments, be compatible with reverse osmosis water as a source, does not introduce
unnecessary IT security issues, and is reasonable to maintain high functionality. The system
must be capable of producing Ultra-Pure (Type I) water from reverse osmosis source water
that is at a purity/quality level suitable for molecular biology laboratory experiments and
assays. The Milli-Q E7000 requested is the only system that provides Ultra-Pure, molecular
biology assay suitable water from a reverse osmosis source, requires replacement of only 2
components for required maintenance, and has confirmed functionality from peer-research
teams.

SMARTScribe™ Reverse Transcriptase, 400 reactions/kit, manufactured by Takara Bio USA, Inc. NAICS CODE: 325414 - Biological Product (Except Diagnostic Manufacturing)  Salient Characteristics- A modified Moloney Murine Leukemia Virus Reverse Transcriptase that synthesizes full-length cDNA from rare or long transcripts.  SMARTScribe RT preparations are exceptionally pure, with all contaminating nucleases removed. These specific reverse transcriptase have the ability to synthesize a complementary DNA because the proprietary reagents used in these kits allow for three enzymatic activities: (1) RNA-dependent  DNA polymerase, (2) RNase H, and (3) DNA-dependent DNA polymerase. First, RNA-dependent DNA polymerase synthesizes a DNA strand complementary to the RNA template. Then RNase H  removes the RNA strand from the RNA–DNA hybrid double helix.

The VRC Virology Laboratory comprises the Virology, Humoral Immunology, and Structural Biology Sections and related Cores. The goal of the Virology Laboratory is to conduct research to understand better the cellular and molecular regulation of viral gene expression, viral entry into the host cell, optimization of immune responses to gene-based vaccination and correlates of immune protection, assessment of active and passive approaches for HIV-1 vaccine development, and to apply structural biology techniques toward rational vaccine design for emerging and re-emerging infectious diseases. The Vaccine Research Center is developing vaccines for HIV-1 and other viruses.  Currently, SBS/SBIS is working on developing vaccines for SARS-CoV-2. Production of wild-type and mutant viral proteins and anti-viral antibodies is critical in vaccine development. Making specific immunogens as probes is critical to isolate antibodies and develop therapeutic agents in the Covid19 treatment. The overarching goal of the project is to evaluate the immune response to HIV  vaccines that employ novel germline-targeting immunogens for raising broadly neutralizing antibodies. This is achieved through single-cell next-generation sequencing of RNA isolated from probe-specific sorted B cells. Critical components of this workflow include reverse transcription of the isolated RNA
and subsequent targeted amplification for downstream sequence analysis. Cutting-edge techniques and technologies are utilized in this project, including Illumina’s state-of-the-art NextSeq 1000 and NextSeq 2000 sequencing instruments. Only reagents and consumables specified for these platforms should be applied for optimal results, and Illumina’s sequencing protocols specify use of Takara Bio’s reagents. Takara Bio’s SMARTScribe™ Reverse Transcriptase kit contains a high-performance enzyme that performs
unbiased cDNA synthesis, allowing for amplification and library construction from any RNA transcript. It generates long, full-length cDNA while preserving the relative transcript proportions of the original RNA sample, particularly crucial when transcribing RNA from a single cell. Takara Bio’s SeqAmp™ DNA Polymerase is a high-fidelity PCR enzyme that is specially formulated for next-Generation sequencing and transcriptome analysis. The specificity of this enzyme is especially critical for analysis of the highly variable sequence regions of B cells, which is what is being analyzed in this project. These reagents are formulated and optimized by Takara Bio specifically for use with Illumina’s sequencing protocols and are only available through Takara Bio.

column6: 334515 - Instrument Manufacturing for Measuring and Testing Electricity and Electrical Signals
column29: The objective is to expand the capability of the MSFC's Materials and Processes Laboratory to conduct Resonance Frequency  and Damping Analysis. The goal is to conduct room temperature measurements and test a novel method for ultra-high temperature measurements. The objective will be met through the Procurement of the IMCE RFDA system and supporting equipment. IMCE provides equipment with a unique capability to measure the resonance and damping characteristics of a material specimen from which Young’s Modulus, Shear Modulus, and Poisson’s Ratio can be derived. The system has sensitive equipment for measuring acoustic signals (10Hz -100kHz). The vendor also provides custom analysis tools for processing the acoustic response at high resolution and displaying the data. The vendor additionally provides hardware for property measurements at high temperature with custom data processing tools for automatic and continuous measurements with real time displays. The tool is critical to funded efforts to develop a measurement capability for Young’s Modulus, Shear Modulus, and Poisson’s Ratio at ultra-high temperatures. This data supports material performance predictions at ultra-high temperatures above 2000C. Failure to procure this tool and develop the technique limits model and performance predictions leading to great risk of material systems at ultra-high temperatures such as those in a nuclear thermal propulsion system.

The EPA’s research program is seeking an electrical impactor particle size spectrometer for real-time aerosol and particle measurements that provides particle concentration, particle size and size distribution monitoring. This instrument shall have a wide measurement range of 6 nm – 10 µm with the same instrument and single measurement method/technique for all particle sizes. An important needed additional feature is because the particles are size classified in a cascade impactor with 14 size classes, they can be collected and analyzed for chemical or biological composition after the real-time measurement. Additionally, this instrument shall be able to be used for particle charge size distribution measurements, as well as for traditional gravimetric impactor measurements. It shall also be lightweight, portable, with internal data logging capabilities. This specialized research instrument shall operate in this broad particle size range for measurement of fine particles/aerosols in the lab and field using only one measurement technique in a single portable instrument.  The algorithm software provided with the instrument shall allow for calculations for expected size distributions and up to 500 size bins. In addition, the 14-stage impactor shall allow for chemical and/or biological analysis and classification of aerosols sampled after real-time air concentration and size measurements. Another crucial component of the instrument is the ability to conduct particle charge distribution measurements, which can specifically support studies related electrostatic sprayers and air treatment technologies that produce ions and other charged particles to interact with contaminants.

The Food and Drug Administration (FDA), National Center for Toxicological Research (NCTR), Division of Genetic and Molecular Toxicology (DGMT) requires Biopredic International NoSpin HepaRG cells, or equal, to conduct research described in NCTR protocols S000904 and E07737.01.
The NoSpin HepaRG cells will be used to assess the cytotoxicity and genotoxicity of a total of over 30 small-molecule nitrosamines and nitrosamine drug substance-related impurities (NDSRIs). Nitrosamines have been known to be carcinogenic in rodent models. Recently, nitrosamines have drawn increased attention by the FDA due to the identification of unacceptable levels of nitrosamines as impurities in several common medicines used for the treatment of hypertension, heartburn, and diabetes. HepaRG cells are human-derived, metabolically competent cells. The DGMT lab has successfully adapted HepaRG cells cultured in both 2D and 3D formats for performing three genotoxicity assays: the comet assay, micronucleus assay, and an error-corrected Next Generation Sequencing mutagenesis assay. Data generated from this cell model are considered relevant for evaluating the health risks caused by human exposure to various potential toxicants and for supporting regulatory decision-making. Specifically, these cells will be used in protocols S000904 and E07737.01 to evaluate the human cancer risks associated with nitrosamine drug impurities of concern to the FDA.

The CHIPS project requires an instrument to perform calibrated modulation distortion measurements on devices. To enable these measurements, upgrades are required for an existing instrument, a large signal network analyzer, including a source for vector modulated signals that can supply 500 MHz bandwidth signals over a frequency range 1 MHz - 44 GHz. The modulated source is required to be controlled by the large signal network analyzer in order to deliver the synchronized measurements that are needed. The project will use this instrument to evaluate the response of magnetic devices at high powers as required for CHIPS magnetic material evaluation over the frequency range 1 MHz - 44 GHz, in order to facilitate the incorporation of magnetic components with semiconductor devices.

The National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases {NIAID) conducts and supports basic and applied research to better understand, treat, and ultimately prevent infectious, immunologic, and allergic diseases. For more than 60 years, NIAID research has led to new therapies, vaccines, diagnostic tests, and other technologies that have improved the health of millions of people in the United States (U.S.) and around the world. NIAID has a unique mandate, which requires the Institute to respond to emerging public health threats. To facilitate its efforts, the NIAID has created the Integrated Research Facility {IRF) as a part of the National lnteragency Biodefense Campus located at Ft. Detrick, MD. The NIAID IRF is a specialized biocontainment facility with the appropriate infrastructure to support basic research and animal studies with various infectious disease agents including those requiring the highest level of biocontainment, (BSL-4), to evaluate therapeutics, vaccines and diagnostics as well as natural history and pathogenesis associated with these agents. The primary focus of the IRF is to manage, coordinate and facilitate the conduct of research with emerging infectious diseases and biodefense pathogens. The mission outcome is to develop improved medical outcomes for patients and develop medical countermeasures and control strategies for human health. The IRF has a procurement for a ProCyte Dx Hematology Analyzer is an automated hematology analyzer for animal blood that evaluates and provides the results of 27 parameters for each blood sample in approximately two minutes. The Synaptic Transmission Section combines physiological and cell-biological approaches to understand the dynamic modulations of the cell membrane. It investigates the role of various proteins during vesicle exocytosis and endocytosis in living cells. Some of the driving questions include how protein localization and clustering at the nanometer scale can lead to emergent alterations at a larger scale, such as the changes in the plasma membrane during vesicle docking, curvature generation, endocytosis, and so on. For this purpose, there is a need to carry out multi-color imaging using techniques that allow the detection of single protein molecules of multiple species, such as the MINFLUX nanoscopy. These precise, quantitative, yet lengthy experiments require an automated perfusion system that allows seamless buffer exchange at a steady rate over a long duration, which
otherwise introduces localization errors and produces incorrect experimental output.

column5: Multi-Sample UV-Vis Spectrophotometer for DNA Quantification and Quality Assessment
column29:
The solicitation # 12505B24Q0059 (RFQ1674522) was posted on 01/22/2024 to GSA eBuy with a quote submission deadline of 11:59 PM CT on 02/05/2024. A single quote was received for a product not listed on the vendor's FSS and  FAR 8.402(f) did not apply. As such, the solicitation had to be moved to SAM.gov. The solicitation # 12505B24Q0073 was posted on 02/12/2024 to SAM.gov as a total small business set aside with a quote submission deadline of 11:59 PM CT on 02/26/2024. A total of six quotes were received. Two quotes were for American made products and four were for foreign made products. The American made products were reviewed and found not to be technically acceptable. 

The Ultrospec 7500 quoted by Aantilia LLC was found not technically acceptable due to its failure to meet the following technical requirements stipulated in the SOW: 
•	The 8-cuvette holder does not provide multi-sample analysis, but rather automated single sample analysis. This is absolutely unacceptable.
•	The 8-cell cuvette holder does not meet the “Easy, rapid sample loading and clearing (maximum time between readings: 30 s).” Simultaneous readings of 8 samples should occur every 30 s.
•	< 2 uL samples are only achievable by purchasing additional equipment/consumables.
•	The instrument does not automatically compensate for path length.

The Agilent Life Sciences EPOCH-SN and TAKE3-SN quoted by Technical Communities, Inc. (TestMart) was found not technically acceptable due to its failure to meet the following technical requirements stipulated in the SOW: 
•	Software does not include adequate contaminant assessment and correction: “Software: … Include accurate, full-spectrum contaminant assessment and correction”
o	According to the company’s application note for purity assessment (Microvolume Purity Assessment of Nucleic Acids Using A260/A280 Ratio and Spectral Scanning (agilent.com)): “Spectral analysis of samples can be compared to spectra of known standards for comparison by a trained eye.” This is not acceptable, as the software itself does not handle the assessment and correction.
•	Concentration range for dsDNA is not provided/assured by the provider. “Concentration range: 2 – 10,000 ng/μL dsDNA”
o	The company’s application note for dsDNA microvolume quantification (Low Volume Nucleic Acid Quantification using a Multi-Volume Spectrophotometer System (agilent.com)) does not clearly demonstrate the accuracy of low-concentration (< 1000 ng/uL) dsDNA quantification.
•	Also, this instrument requires a laptop, which is not included in the quote. “Hardware: Instrument must be stand-alone or laptop must be included.”

The Laboratory of Viral Diseases (LVD) explore fundamental aspects of cell and molecular biology, viral pathogenesis, and viral immunology within the context of a diverse group of medically important viruses that includes alphaviruses, coronaviruses, enteroviruses, flaviviruses, herpesviruses, human/simian immunodeficiency viruses, influenza viruses, papillomaviruses, and poxviruses. The goals of these research programs are to create knowledge that increases a basic biological understanding of these pathogens and their interactions with their hosts and to generate new strategies for preventing and treating viral diseases. One of the research programs under the LVD is the Single Virion Biology and Biophysics Unit (SVBBU). Viral outbreaks and pandemics arise when animal viruses adapt so that they can recognize and use human cells as their host. Viral persistence in human circulation is subsequently driven by rapid acquisition of mutations in viral proteins that allow evasion of the adaptive human immune response. The SVBBU is working on working to define how the structural organization of virus particles might enable entry into cells not displaying fitting receptors. Viral ability to establish new infections under suboptimal conditions is probed as a possible mechanism of adaptation to new hosts with the potential to lead to new pandemics. Complementing our research on virus cell entry is our effort to define the mechanisms of virus particle assembly and regulation of the assembly outcomes (i.e., structural features of virus particles such as shape, size, or viral protein incorporation). We have developed novel quantitative and high-throughput approaches for studying virus particle assembly,
and detailed functional characterizations are underway. The SVBBU, requires an advanced cell and tissue mechanical conditioning platform. The Curi Bio
cytostretcher is a simple yet powerful device that will let us physically stretch cells in a controlled and sterile environment. This will allow us to directly determine the effects of cell tension on virion shape and the effects this has on the virulence of influenza A. IMPACT – If this waiver is not granted the SVBBU will not be able to physically stretch cells in a controlled and sterile environment. Not being able to do so will prevent the lab to determine the effect of cell tension on virion shape and the effects this has on the virulence of influenza A. Curi Bio Cytostretcher ( Description Item: Cytostretcher, External Control Unit, Cooling Unit, Cooling Plate,

The National Center for Advancing Translational Sciences (NCATS) requires a Cellix Venaflux Starter 1.0 (quantity 1), Vena8 Fluoro+ Biochip Pack 10 (quantity 5), and a Biochip-Connect Biochip Single Outlet Cable, Pack 10 (quantity 1) (brand name or equal) to facilitate the measurement of thrombosis/clotting in a human vascular cell model in a quantitative and reproduceable manner suitable for higher throughput.

The National Center for Advancing Translational Sciences (NCATS) is a part of the National Institutes of Health (NIH). NCATS is in need of an instrument that can measure changes in clotting upon exposure to various toxicants in a human endothelial cell model. This method must be quantitative, reproducible, cost effective and able to interface with the existing infrastructure. NCATS, as part of its commitment to the Tox21 Consortium, is responsible for finding a model human cellular system that can measure the effects of various toxicants on the cardiovasculature. The Gerhold Laboratory has found that a number of these toxicants may affect coagulation and thrombosis transcriptionally,  and would like to confirm that these results are physiologically relevant in a model system that can measure changes in coagulation and thrombosis. The system will require a pump, microscope adapter that will interface with an Echo Revolve microscope as well as relevant software to control the system and collect data.

The Cellix Venaflux Starter System provides a microscope adapter along with flow pumps with 8 microcapillary flow chambers that will allow a model human cell line 
 differentially treated) to be perfused through the chambers and allow measurement of flow/coagulation to software that will quantitate differences in response to different treatments concurrently across the 8 chambers. The system will interface with an existing Microscope and will allow data acquisition to software unique to the instrument.

This action is for procurement of NAN Electrode Drive (Electrode Positioning System). The objective of this purchase is to enhance our comprehension of the neural mechanisms underlying visual perception. To achieve this, our research studies awake non-human primates with concurrent electrophysiological recordings and electrical stimulation in the targeted brain region during a visual perception task to address gaps in the existing literature. This research necessitates the simultaneous recording from four electrodes within the specified brain region. These electrodes must be designed to reach ventral brain regions without veering from their intended path during their descent through the brain. Furthermore, they must offer flexibility in their placement within a recording chamber, allowing for arbitrary reconfiguration as needed. The UNCB needs a specialized electrode driver that provides precision, consistency, and accuracy the NAN electrode drive system offers.

Salient characteristics for this requirement include; One (1) NAN Electrode Drive System Set-up for chamber recordings: four channel controller with software included, Adapter custom fitted for chamber (24.16 mm for base), four channel base custom fitted for chamber, NAN electrode tower, grid for 23-gauge tubing (1mm hole spacing), coarse movement system, neuropixel motorized and manual arm, neuropixel holder, guide tube clamp for neuropixel, 8 guide tubes for neuropixel (7mm x 5mm). Additionally, it must have:
- Modular system with the capacity to record up to 16 channels
- Reliable and stable recordings over extended time periods
- Effective noise reduction even during electrode movement
- Flexibility and consistency in electrode positioning within the XY plane
- High-resolution capabilities of up to one micron
- Precise electrode placement facilitated by direct motor-electrode coupling
- Adjustable electrode depth manipulation within a user-defined operational range
- Controlled speed range, ranging from one to 200 microns per second
- Effortless electrode replacement, requiring less than one minute per tower
- Advanced controller with a user-friendly GUI, enabling communication with a 19-inch rack-mount control box through a standard interface
- Lightweight design, with each tower weighing 25g and a 4-channel base weighing 35g
