column5: Quorum Cryostage Power Supply for PP3010
column29:
The RTB/RML requires a replacement Quorum cryostage power supply and installation to replace a nonfunctioning Quorum cryostage power supply. RML’s Electron Microscopy Unit’s Quorum cryostage power supply is currently broken and needs to be replaced. The cryostage allows for preparation of cryo lamella, visualization of cryo samples by Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), and volume Focused Ion Beam Scanning/Electron Microscopy (FIB/SEM) analysis of frozen samples. In the quorum’s current down-state the lab is unable to work with samples that require these important techniques. 

 RTB/RML requires the same cryostage power supply to replace the broken unit power unit for their Quorum cryostage, which is a PP3010 cryo sem preparation system. This system is the latest in cryo-SEM technology and combines the highest quality results with unparalleled ease of use. The PP3010 is a highly automated, column-mounted, gas-cooled cryo sem preparation and cryo transfer system suitable for most makes and models of W-SEM, FE-SEM and FIB/SEM. Cryo preparation techniques for cryo SEM and FIB/SEM are essential for the observation of biological, wet or beam sensitive specimens. Using cryo sem electron microscopy removes the need for conventional preparation methods, such as chemical fixation and critical point drying, and allows observation of specimens in their natural hydrated state. There are no domestically produced alternatives for this power system. 
Anticipated Mission Impact if there is no waiver: If this waiver is not granted, the RML Electron Microscopy Unit will not be able to operate this equipment (Quorum PP3010 Cryostage – Cryo-SEM Preparation System) to observe biological, wet or beam sensitive specimens that are required in many of the lab’s experimental processes. Cryo-scanning electron microscopy (cryo-SEM), enables observation of bulk biological materials in hydrated conditions by conversion of liquid water to solid by cryofixation, has been widely used for ultrastructural studies of biological materials. HHSAR PGI 325 Appendix AA Template for Individual or Class Determination and Findings (D&F) For Domestic Nonavailability Exceptions to the Buy American Act.

Based on the market research information above, the sources sought results, and the requirement of the Quorum Power Supply System for the Quorum PP3010 equipment to function, and market research findings, the feasibility of foregoing the requirement or providing a United States manufactured substitute has been considered, and no substitutes were found. Authority is requested to acquire the above-described Quorum PP3010 Power Supply System, manufactured in the United Kingdom by Quorum Technologies for the total cost of $21,950.00. Once the approved waiver is granted, this requirement will be published on the Government Point of Entry (SAM.gov) as a Request for Quotation (RFQ), with a brand name restriction, for a period of fifteen (15) days to provide opportunity to distributors in the marketplace.

Based on the findings detailed above, [Quorum PP3010 Power Supply System manufactured by Quorum Technologies] is not mined, produced, or manufactured in the United States in sufficient and reasonably available commercial quantities and of a satisfactory quality. Therefore, it is hereby determined, consistent with [FAR 25.103(b)(2)], that these items are non-available.

column5: Hexapod Component of Extrapolation Chamber for Measuring Absorbed Dose from Alpha-emitting Sources
column17: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF STANDARDS AND TECHNOLOGY
column20: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF STANDARDS AND TECHNOLOGY
column29: The National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) Radioactivity and Dosimetry Groups have initiated a collaborative project with University of Wisconsin (UWI) to characterize radionuclides for application in targeted alpha therapy to treat various types of cancer. The Radioactivity Group will develop methods used to fabricate alpha-particle-emitting radioactive sources and measure the absolute activity of these sources. The Dosimetry Group will measure the absorbed dose due to the sources, which will complement the activity measurements, fully characterizing the source and providing valuable information relevant to clinical treatment planning. In order to perform absorbed dose measurements, a specialized instrument called an extrapolation ionization chamber is required. The UWI has built an extrapolation chamber specifically designed to measure the absorbed dose from alpha-emitting sources, which is the first of its kind as commercially available extrapolation chambers only measure beta-emitting sources. It is critical for the instrument at NIST to be a copy of the one at UWI in order to allow for transfer of the NIST measurements to the UWI as a secondary standard and to enable measurement comparisons between the two institutions. To fabricate the same instrument for NIST, specific Hexapod components, available only from one source, are required as an integral part of the extrapolation chamber. Any difference in physical dimensions and mounting platform geometry of different Hexapod components would not allow the radioactive source/shield assembly to be an exact copy of that of the UWI extrapolation chamber. Measurements of absorbed dose from alpha-particle-emitting radionuclide sources are extremely sensitive to the source-detector geometry due to the short range of the alphas. If this geometry is not the same between the NIST and UWI extrapolation chambers, there will be unacceptable differences in the measurement results, therefore making it impossible to transfer the NIST absorbed-dose measurements to the UWI and invalidating any measurement comparisons between the two institutions. Without the ability to transfer NIST standard measurements, the alpha sources cannot be fully characterized for eventual use in a therapy clinic, and the goal of this collaborative project cannot be achieved. Having such an instrument at NIST is essential to the success of the project, and the required Hexapod components are only available from one source.

column5: FC-470/589/635, fiber-coupled, 3 wavelength integrated laser system
column29:
As part of its service to the NIH community, the NIF must provide specialized equipment for shared use to a broad spectrum of researchers with various levels of expertise from Neuroscience fields. 
This laser is of high importance in our research as it will enable us to stimulate multiple sites across the brain of mammals and to test the causal link across multiple brain networks. Similarly, our approach can be readily adopted in a variety of fMRI studies that require (1) sequential cell-type-specific stimulation on multiple sites for mapping functional cortico-cortical or subcortical networks, (2) temporally controlled stimulation on multiple sites for investigating interregional functional coupling balances, (3) an in situ subject-specific stimulation design defined by anatomical or functional imaging, and (4) simultaneous functional mapping of cortical areas through optical imaging and cortical-subcortical regions by fMRI. 

Multi-channel (FC-MS-CH) laser system. Type: 3 combined wavelengths FC-470/589/635, Components: Fiber core diameter: 200 um, Output power: 1W, Multimode Fiber, Integrated cooling method, RS232.

Salient Characteristics: 
1.	Powerful laser system for optogenetics ( ~ 1W).
2.	Wavelengths suitable to our needs. Other companies such as Thorlabs and Optomechanis offer multiple wavelengths that are not compatible to our needs.
3.	This system can accommodate the 3 wavelengths we need for our research: 470 nm, 589 nm, and 635 nm. 
4.	The laser can individually control the laser output of each channel or combine multiple channels at the same time.
5.	Built-in driver for easy operation. Light can be delivered using TTL modulation or CW, or any analog input.
6.	TTL modulation can be adjusted to different frequencies 1 Hz,-30kHz, perfectly suitable to optogenetically stimulate neurons. 
7.	Comes with SMA connectors, which are compatible with our multimode fibers.
8.	Operates at low temperature 10 ~35 compared to other laser systems as it is integrated with cooling method.
9.	Dimensions of this system is around 330 x 291 x 156 (mm).
10.	The products offer by this company are certified by FDA.

column5: Coil Cooler for Cool Coils and High Performance Option/Refrigeration Unit for Coil Cooler
column29:
A coil cooling unit is needed to support the Study entitled, “Role of GABAergic transmission in auditory processing in Autism Spectrum Disorder”. This coil cooling unit is an essential component for a complete TMS system. A coil cooling unit is fundamental for use of the liquid cooled coil, which is also being requested in order to carry out our study, which has been reviewed and approved by the NIMH committee for the Scientific Review of Protocols (CSRP) and by the NIH IRB. As part of the study, TMS pulses must be applied repeatedly, which may result in coil overheating. The coil cooling unit keeps the coils from overheating and maintains the temperature of the coils between 18 and 20°C at all time. The existing system does not have a coil cooling unit as it has not previously been used for repetitive stimulation protocols. The coil cooling unit requested here will allow for the study to utilize existing equipment currently in the Noninvasive Neuromodulation Unit. 


The blinded, cooled coil will allow for the targeted stimulation of language processing networks within the brain in order to transiently modulate the putative brain mechanism, GABAergic transmission. In doing so, we aim to define the causal link between the putative mechanism and the neurological and behavioral phenotype of ASD.

The liquid coil cooling system and a refrigeration cooling unit keeps the coils from overheating and maintains the temperature of the coils between 18 and 20°C at all time.

column29: The product that needs to be acquired is a highly accurate interface shear force sensor. The shear force sensor is a device that can measure and record shear forces between one or two contact areas. This is a new requirement needed for successful completion of several research projects for the biomechanics team (CDC/NIOSH/HELD/PERB). The shear force sensor provides impactful data that can reduce musculoskeletal injuries and traumatic injuries in many occupations and is vital to several of our research projects. The interface shear force sensor system to be purchased has two important unique requirements: 1) it needs to measure shear force with great accuracy; 2) needs to be thin and flexible so it can be placed in between surfaces. If we are unable to acquire a highly accurate shear force sensor it will impact the mission and goals of both our team and CDC/NIOSH. We do not currently have a shear force sensor and the sensor is vital for determining the development of chronic and acute injuries in many occupations and thus reducing these injuries in the future. This sensor is important for successful completion of our National Occupational Research Agenda (NORA) projects regarding the use of exoskeletons in industry and reducing injuries in healthcare workers. Without the shear force sensor, it will limit the accuracy and completeness of our projects, which aim to reduce injury and improve both short term and long-term worker health directly in line with the mission of HHS, CDC, and NIOSH. Japan is currently the only country that has a vendor (Nissha) that produces the shear force sensor (Japan is part of WTO).

The items being acquired are differentiated pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) engineered by Bit.Bio that have tremendous potential in disease modeling, toxicology assessments, drug testing, and regenerative therapies. The Bit.Bio cells are unique in that they are derived from human iPSCs using a patented procedure developed by Bit.Bio and have been
shown to be highly homogeneous and differentiated cells that are far more uniform and cell type specific than we have obtained using “standard” differentiation protocols
with other iPSCs or ESCs. As a direct result of this, only the cells produced by Bit.Bio are adequate for our research needs. Bit.Bio can provide all of the cell types
(glutamatergic neurons, GABAnergic neurons, sensory neurons, microglia, oligodendroglia and liver cells) from a source/origin using the same method of generating the cells. critical given the complex nature of the organizational unit that represents the brain. For the specific experiments we plan to conduct our work requires well-defined neurotransmitter specific neurons and microglia that are important for assessing toxicologic endpoints and environmental impacts on induction of disease states and studying molecular mechanisms. Bit.Bio is unique in that they can provide proprietary differentiation protocols that produce a pure, or highly purified cell population of specific cell types highly required for our studies. Additionally, their proprietary differentiation protocols will allow cell differentiation to resemble cells in healthy human tissues. Many domestic companies have not been able to achieve differentiation of iPSC-derived liver cells with these cells showing characteristics of fetal liver cells and do not have sufficient differentiation to allow us to use them for our specific studies whereby we need well-defined and differentiated hepatocytes. The Bit.Bio hepatocytes will help to screen and detect environmental chemicals that may target the liver resulting in fibrosis and other disease outcomes. Currently Bit.Bio is the only vendor that can meet our aforementioned requirements is located in Cambridge, United Kingdom.

column5: Four (4) spools of commercial trawl fishing wire rope
column29:
Since 1997, the NOAA Fisheries Northwest Fisheries Science Center (NWFSC) Fisheries Resource Analysis and Monitoring Division (FRAM) has conducted bottom trawl surveys off the coast of the Continental United States from the Canadian Border to the Mexican Border in depths ranging from 30 to 700 fathoms (180 to 4200 feet). These annual surveys provide stock assessment managers with critical data required for the management of biologically and economically important fish stocks located in these waters. FRAM contracts four to five commercial fishing vessels per year to perform these stock assessment survey and FRAM supplies its charter fishing vessels with trawl warp cable to deploy its bottom trawl sampling net. The research samples that are recovered are imperative to the calculation of catch per unit effort (CPUE) which in turn, is used directly by West Coast Fisheries Management. FRAM’s current trawl warps have been used for three seasons and are no longer suitable for use.  
The requirement is to provide four (4) spools of 7500 feet (each) (30,000 feet total) of steel core galvanized 6x19 trawl wire rope for use during the West Coast Groundfish Bottom Trawl Survey. This will allow the FRAM Division to accomplish its mission of performing its annual, west coast wide fish and ecosystem survey. The trawl wire rope is used in trawl applications; specifically, in this application it will be used for deploying a trawl net sampling system from a chartered commercial fishing vessel. The data will be used to enumerate demersal and benthic animals during the West Coast Groundfish Bottom Trawl Survey (WCGBTS). The relative abundance of up to 40 harvestable fish species collected during the surveys are used as a main index for managing relevant fish species.

NIST has a requirement for a contractor to provide one state-of-art micro-focused XRF spectrometer system with all available modern features, the ability to conduct high volume survey measurements, and be equipped with two silicon drift detectors, each of which is capable of counting with high linearity into the 100,000 counts per second with high quality data reduction and 400,000 counts per second survey measurements allowing for fast point-to-point area maps.

This requirement will support the research requirements for the NIST EL’s Materials and Structural Systems Division (MSSD) studies into the durability and service life of infrastructure materials (polymer, concrete, engineered composites) for resilience infrastructure applications.
The following is a description of the research requirements that this instrument shall support:

The requested instrument is required to accomplish milestones related to several ongoing project areas as NIST EL MSSD is seeking to expand its XRF capabilities.  The micro-focused XRF spectrometer instrument X-rays are focused into a small spot approximately 15 µm to 50 µm in diameter and is capable of analyzing X-rays fluoresced from a sample in an X-ray chamber.  Each micro-focused XRF has five basic components: 1) an X-ray tube (or multiple tubes), 2) a poly-capillary optic, 3) an X-ray detector (or multiple detectors), 4) a sample stage (X, Y, and Z translation), and 5) a magnifying optical camera (coupling visible images with composition maps).  The capability to create large area compositional maps is a vital to the mission to many researchers within MSSD, who routinely analyze complex, heterogeneous materials; a task which could be enhanced significantly through the use of a dedicated micro-focused XRF instrument. MSSD is also actively creating new measurement methods and analysis techniques that require an instrument both capable of high-volume survey measurements, and state of the art measurement and analysis development.  The required instrumentation consists of tools with simultaneous excitation energies allowing complex data reduction unavailable with single source excitation, and systems with multiple detectors which allow for distinguishing between fluorescent spectra (which provide elemental analysis) and diffraction spectra (which could provide crystal orientation, if this information can be de-convoluted).  

The requested micro-focused XRF spectrometer has been identified through market research as sole instrument capable of meeting the detailed specifications to achieve MSSD’s research requirements.

The independent estimated government cost of the requirement is $576,885.00, with an estimated 120-day delivery timeframe after contractor receipt of award.

column5: Pertussis FHA and Pertussis Fimbriae Antibodies
column17: CENTERS FOR DISEASE CONTROL AND PREVENTION

column29:
The MPIR Immunobiology laboratory is working on assay development for the detection of Pertussis antigen. In order to create an antigen detection assay, monoclonal recombinant antibodies need to be obtained. These two monoclonal recombinant antibodies will be generated through means of HuCAL technology for the purpose of using these antibodies to perform in-vitro research studies. The request is for the purchase of Pertussis FHA and Pertussis Fimbriae antibodies needed to capture and detect the disease causing pertussis antigen. 

Specifically, the MPIR laboratory requires up to 20 Fab antibodies in a quantity of 100-250 µg each.  Each antibody must be evaluated for Koff and evaluated in sandwich pair ELISA.  The results of the top 5 sandwich pairs will be provided to the MPIR Laboratory.  With the quantities provided the MPIR Laboratory will confirm the results and begin evaluation of the optimal pairs in the MPIR Laboratory, laboratory developed test.  An optional 25 mg of the best pair will be needed for the assay development. The antibody generation should be approximately 8-12 weeks which is significantly faster than the use of animal models to generate antibodies.  An additional 4-8 weeks is needed to for the evaluation Koff and sandwich pair ELISA evaluation.  In total the project should take up to 20 weeks and an optional antibody production should be 8-10 weeks.

The Laboratory of Infectious Diseases (LID) within the National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases (NIAID) focuses on viral vaccine development, host immune response to viruses, and viral molecular biology and genetics. Major long-term programs are dedicated to understanding and preventing viral hepatitis, severe childhood respiratory diseases, and viral gastroenteritis. Newer programs focus on developing vaccines for pandemic influenza, West Nile
virus, human metapneumovirus, and SARS-CoV—from basic research to clinical trials. The Attune NXT AFC BRV- laser and the Cytkick Autosampler are required for use in measuring immune responses in animals and patients infected with Herpes viruses and to measure immune responses to vaccine candidates currently under development and in clinical trials. The MVS lab requires a system that fits the current space available, has 3 lasers able to handle 11 fluorochromes r(13 parameters), can handle 96-well plates and tubes in an automated fashion, can automatically shut down after running high throughput samples, and can be expanded in the future to automatically handle up to twenty 96-well plates. This system has been tested and found to give similar results to the system currently being used and therefore we have confidence in using future data in comparison with past data.

column29:
The Physical Measurement Laboratory at NIST develops advanced sensors to measure acceleration. As part of this work a shaker (also called a vibrational exciter system) is required to test photonic accelerometers. The shaker will operate vertically and include an internal reference accelerometer. The system will include the shaker, the amplifier to run it, the internal reference accelerometer, and the signal conditioning electronics to read it, as well as all cables and accessories required to operate the system with an accelerometer mounted.
Minimum technical specifications for the shaker
1.	Vertical operation
2.	Maximum payload of at least 1000 grams
3.	Frequency range of at least 5 Hz to 15 kHz for frequency response measurements.
4.	Able to supply an acceleration with a 150 gram load of at least 100 m/s2 over the frequency range of 30 Hz to 10 kHz continuously for 5 minutes.
5.	First resonance > 50 kHz
6.	Transverse motion less than 3% between 5 kHz and 15 kHz.
7.	Shaker Dimensions: requires less than 1 square foot table space and less than 6” height, shaker weight less than 20 kg
8.	A calibration certificate traceable to NIST for operation between 5Hz and 20 kHz with no load.

Minimum technical specifications for the reference accelerometer
1.	Mounted internally to shaker
2.	Sensitivity >= 1 mV/(m/s2)
3.	Freq range (3dB) greater than 3 Hz to 30 kHz
4.	Resonant frequency greater than 50 kHz
5.	Deviation from linearity less than 1%

column29: The National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), Physical Measurement Laboratory, Nanoscale Device Characterization Division (NDCD) requires a requires a narrow-band laser with broadly tunable wavelength in the visible and near-IR spectral ranges and with tunable repetition rate. The laser will be used in combination with an atomic force microscopy tool to obtain light absorption spectra and thermal property mapping with nanoscale resolution, with a technique named Photothermal induced resonance (PTIR). To enable such measurements the laser source must be able to: 1) Emit light pulses with a wavelength tunable between 570 nm and 2400 nm without gaps with power > 50 mW; 2) Within each laser stage, tune the wavelength with 1 nm steps at rate of 30 nm/s or faster; 3) Emit narrow linewidth pulses (i.e., <4 nm between 570 nm and 1000 nm); 4) Emit light pulses with a repetition rate tunable between 100 kHz to 200 kHz or wider range; 5) Sweep the repetition rate between 100 kHz to 200 kHz or wider range with a 0.4 kHz resolution in less than 60 s; and 6) Emit light pulses with pulse length between 1 ns and 10 ns.

column5: Wafer bonder and lithography aligner system
column17: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF STANDARDS AND TECHNOLOGY
column20: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF STANDARDS AND TECHNOLOGY
column29: Wafer bonder is a unique fabrication tool that bring two wafers adhere to each other without the application of any macroscopic gluing layer or external mechanical force. It produces a mechanically stable interface and hermetically sealed encapsulation between two wafers. Bonding alignment is the precedent step before the actual wafer bonding. It places two substrates in particular positions relative to each other in order to combine the functions of both substrates. The bonding alignment requires a compatible optical wafer aligner. Lithography wafer aligner does wafer exposure which transfers patterns of photomasks onto resist-coated wafers, and also perform wafer alignment for both lithography and wafer bonding purposes. It is an essential exposure tool for micro-fabrication besides the function of supporting wafer bonding alignment. Its main components include a UV light source, beam shaping optical lens, wafer positioning system with top and bottom sides of microscopes for the upper and lower wafers. For the bonding purpose, it is also equipped with a clamping mechanism for the wafer pair in order to transport aligned wafer pair into wafer bonder without introducing an alignment shift. Wafer Bonder: Technical Specifications The system shall be able to transfer and perform bonding process on substrates with various shapes and sizes ranged from 10 mm to at least 150 mm.  The system shall come with all accessories tools required for transferring and bonding substrates with various shapes and sizes ranged from 10 mm to at least 150 mm.  The system shall come with proper bonding plates for the following types of bonds: thermocompression, eutectic, adhesion, anodic and fusion bonds. Both types of bonding plates shall be included.  The system shall be able to process 75 mm wafer, 100 mm wafer and 150 mm substrates with thickness 6 mm or larger. The system shall have two independently controlled heating system for the top and bottom bonding plates.  The heating systems shall be able to perform controlled heating in the range of 40 ◦C to 550 ◦C  The heating systems shall be able to achieve temperature uniformity better than ± 2% in the range of 80 ◦C to 500 ◦C.  The heating systems shall have the capability of running a controlled ramping rate from 1 ◦C/min to 30 ◦C/min.  The process chamber shall have 2 or more gas ports available.  The pumping system for process chamber shall be able to achieve overpressure at least 2 bar and vacuum 5.0 x 10E-5 mbar or lower. The system shall be capable of providing 20,000 N force with a repeatability ± 2% or better.  The system shall be equipped with a power supply providing at least 2000 V and 60 mA for anodic bonding purpose. The system shall be provided with at least one recommended recipe for each of the following types of bonding: adhesion, fusion, eutectic and anodic.  The system shall be provided with a computer and MS based operating system to perform tool control and data logging.  Tool control software shall be GUI based and support different levels of user access.

column29:
The U.S. Department of Commerce, National Institute of Standards and Technology, Physical Measurement Laboratory, Nanoscale Device Characterization Division (NDCD) requires an electrical static chuck (ESC) based deep reactive ion etching (DRIE) system to support nanofabrication in the Center for Nanoscale Science and Technology (CNST) user facility.

1. SYSTEM CONFIGURATION
a)	The system shall use the Bosch process according to industry standards in the semiconductor industry. Cryogenic etching of silicon is not acceptable.
b)	The system shall have a single-wafer vacuum load-lock to transport wafer in and out of the process chamber such that the process chamber does not need to be vented between process runs. 
c)	The system shall be able to operate according to one recipe that can control all the modules simultaneously.
d)	The vacuum system shall be configured with the roughing pumps located remotely in the CNST NanoFab subfab area beneath the process level approximately 15 meters (approximately 50 feet) away. 
e)	The system shall include all computer systems, power supplies, and all ancillary equipment required for the system to operate.

2.   PROCESS CHAMBER AND VACUUM SYSTEM
a)	The chamber shall be equipped with chamber wall heater capable of heating the chamber walls to at least of 65 degrees Celsius.
b)	The camber shall be constructed of material that is resistant to the processing chemicals.
c)	The vacuum system shall be equipped with a throttle valve and controller for independent control of process gas flow and chamber pressure.
a.	The process chamber pressure control shall be able to maintain the process pressure set point to plus or minus 2% during the etch process.
b.	The process chamber operating pressure range shall be settable within the following pressure range: 1.0 millitorr to 250 millitorr or higher.

3.	PROCESS GAS PANEL	
a.	The system shall have a minimum of 8 gas channels, including but not limited to: Ar, He, N2, O2, SF6 and C4F8. 
b.	The process gas panel shall be equipped with digital mass flow controllers (MFCs) that control gas flow with a precision of plus or minus 5% and an accuracy of plus or minus 5%.
c.	Each of the channels shall be independently controlled.
d.	The gas shut-off valves shall be able to open or close in 1 second or less.
ii.	The system shall be able to handle non-conductive substrates. 
iii.	The system shall be capable of plasma cleaning the substrate chuck and clamping mechanism without requiring a substrate to be loaded. 
g.	The system shall include a Wafer Edge Protection (WEP) system 

4.	CONTROL SYSTEM AND SOFTWARE	 
a.	The software shall control all aspects of the tool. This software must also provide users a Graphical User Interface (GUI) that: shows users the state of the tool, allows users to change the state of the tool, allows users to create and store recipes, and allows users to run recipes to automatically run process on the tool. 
b.	The control system shall be capable of setting process parameter limits accessible only to administrator level access.

column29:
Research in the Cellular Neurophysiology Section focuses on circuit and cellular properties of neurons located in the midbrain dopamine system and basal ganglia. As a major part of our research, we are interested in the physiology of dopamine neurons located in the substantia nigra pars compacta (SNc) that undergo selective cell death in Parkinson’s patients. Specifically, we are interested in understanding how synaptic and intrinsic conductances expressed on these neurons (particulary calcium permeable conductances, ie voltage gated calcium channels and glutamate activated receptors) differ among subpopulations dopamine neurons. The hope that this information may provide insight to why SNc dopamine neurons are particularly vulnerable in Parkinson’s disease.  Our goal is to identify novel physiological and genetic markers in single SNc dopamine neurons which may be useful in defining subpopulations of these neurons in a meaningful way.  In order to study the action potential firing properties and physiology of dopamine system
neurons, our lab uses electrophysiological approaches. Using this approach allows us to have direct access to the activity of neurons and to have very precise control over the experimental parameters. In order to record the electrical activity of these neurons, we will need to use high-precision amplifiers that allow us to accurately measure submillivolt changes in the membrane potential of cells. In addition, this activity will be recorded on computers using digitizers. NINDS end users searched across the internet for companies that supply electrophysiology equipment.

The Nanoscale Device Characterization Division (NDCD) has the capability for phosphorus doping of Si (100) surfaces with atomic scale control using ultra high vacuum (UHV) scanning tunneling microscopes (STMs) in the atom-based laboratory. There is a concerted effort within the projects, “Atomically Precise Devices” and “Towards Atomic Perfection: Artificial Dangling Bond Molecules for High Precision Single Atom Doping of Silicon”, to establish the capability to fabricate atomic scale dopant devices with single atom precision.  Utilizing hydrogen mask lithography with the help of an STM, a single dopant atom can be selectively placed in a silicon 3D lattice. This is the highest precision achievable in semiconductor electronics. The NDCD is implementing the atomic-precision fabrication of silicon encapsulated devices.    The encapsulation process requires high quality, controlled epitaxial overgrowth of silicon in UHV.  This is best achieved using a silicon sublimation source which can be bolted to the UHV chamber used for sample preparation.  one (1) silicon sublimation source that meets the following specifications:

1.	The source must be equipped with an intrinsic silicon filament.
2.	The source must have preheating mechanisms to enable intrinsic silicon filament sublimation.
3.	The source shall have high purity single crystal silicon shields that cover all metallic parts that face the target substrate.
4.	The source shall be capable of operating at deposition/growth rates lower than 2 Angstroms per minute.
5.	The source shall be equipped with a water-cooled shroud to dissipate heat and protect the UHV system from silicon coating.
6.	The source shall be equipped with a shutter.
7.	The source must be UHV compatible (<5 x 10-11 Torr materials).
8.	The source shall be able to withstand at least 200 degrees Celsius during vacuum processing.
9.	The combined source and water-cooled shroud shall be equipped with a 4.5” outer diameter CF flange to be mounted on a UHV system.
10.	The source shall include a thermocouple to measure the temperature of the sublimation cell and calibrate sublimation flux.

The National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke (NINDS) Proteomics Core Facility (PCF) provides protein identification, protein post-translational modification mapping, and protein quantitation services for NIH-wide investigators. The facility is also utilized for collaborations involving various complicated protein quantitation strategies.

Over the past several years, identifying protein binding partners via labeled or label free quantitation workflows has been the most requested services by PCF users. Most samples contain detergent which is not compatible with the downstream mass spectrometry analysis. Traditionally, protein precipitation is used to remove the detergent from the protein samples. The method has relatively poor sample cleanup and low protein yield. The recently developed S-trap method involves more than 20 manual steps. It is so time consuming that it becomes the main reason for longer turnaround time.

The required instrument automates cleanup of protein by moving magnetic beads, not liquids, which enables the removal of detergent with consistent protein extraction and high protein yield. Not only can this instrument process a much greater number of samples simultaneously than is possible manually, it will also reduce errors and increase reproducibility, which is crucial for the success of labeled or label free quantitation services used by majority of the PCF users.

column29: The item being procured is a Workstation CHRONECT Symbiosis Plus 250 bar Ultra-High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (UHPLC) system with online SPE capability. This workstation includes multiple pumps, autosampler, injector, SPE analysis compartment and software, and is connected to a mass spectrometer. The workstation is used to remove contamination and isolate chemicals of interest such as per-and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), for the accurate and precise analysis of biospecimens like serum and urine. For example, for urine analysis, the 250-bar system is sufficient because in general urine is available in larger volumes than blood. Also, because of salts and other chemicals present in urine, the 250-bar system is typically best as the smaller diameter systems tend to clog with the particulates in the urine. The Workstation CHRONECT Symbiosis Plus 250 system is the only system fully compatible with Division of Laboratory Systems’ (DLS) current systems and methods and provides an on-line solid phase extraction that can exchange the Solid Phase Extraction (SPE) cartridges in a way that is completely synchronized with the operation of mass spectrometer data collection. The exchange of cartridges is needed to ensure validity of the analytical results because of the complexity of complex biological matrices. The new Symbiosis on-line SPE system with supported software suits the needs of DLS method of systems considered, this is the only workstation that can replace an outdated, non-serviceable make and model instrument that is failing and is currently used in the Organic Analytical Toxicology (OAT) Branch of CDC. Spark Holland dba. iChrom is the only manufacturer and distributor of this specific workstation. This workstation supports methods under the Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments of 1988 (CLIA) that are extensively validated and published in peer-reviewed manuscripts using the requested workstation configuration. Any change to the workstation configuration would require extensive time and financial effort to re-validate methodology, thus resulting in significant delays in analysis and reporting of results. For policymakers, health scientists, and the public, access to timely and accurate human data from the approved CLIA methods is critical to track and understand the potential effect on diseases such as cancer and identify and evaluate ways to minimize the health impact of chemicals like PFAS and PAHs.

The Speciation and Elemental labs measure elemental concentrations in biological samples with a goal of measuring concentrations as low as possible. The presence of elemental contaminants in the purified water used to prepare reagents has a cascade effect on the measured accuracy and precision for these analytical measurements. The Millipore system has the lowest elemental concentrations of elements measured in our lab (e.g. Pb, Hg, As, Ni) and elements that interfere with analytical measurement of those same elements (e.g., Ca, Al, K). The Group is developing devices that measure optical properties of living mammalian cells in flow at high speed. NIAID requires the IJM Nanoscaler system and accompanying components made by the company Knauer in Germany. 

National Institute of Allergy & Infectious Diseases (NIAID)’s Vaccine Research Center (VRC) (collectively “Program”) is engaged in research to facilitate the development of vaccines against HIV/AIDS and other infectious pathogens/diseases. Program is researching mRNA technology as a current and attractive alternative for the development of Human vaccines. The target protein gene sequence is encoded in a template DNA plasmid containing a T7 RNA polymerase promoter required for RNA transcription. The mRNA transcripts are produced from a linear DNA template during an in vitro transcription (IVT) reaction. The resulting mRNA is subsequently purified, formulated in lipid nanoparticles, and delivered into cells to be translated into the protein of interest. Program requires the Knauer IJM Nanoscaler to further its research. The IJM Nanscaler is designed for lipid nanoparticle R&D – allowing scientists to screen for the optimal process parameters for the formulation of API-containing lipid nanoparticles.

This acquisition has unique requirements. Knauer is the only manufacturer of this custom IJM Nanoscaler system that is required by the Program to implement its available mRNA production technology for clinical development. The Knauer IJM Nanoscaler is a production scale instrumentation and fulfills the missing piece in Program’s ability to scale both process development and the clinical manufacturing needed to support NIH programs. The volume scale required for Program’s work is met by the Knauer IJM Nanoscaler at 15mL to 1L. This instrument and software is CFR 21 compliant. Knauer IJM Nanoscaler provides instrumentation and software solutions to enable scaling from benchtop to clinical manufacturing with minimal development between scales, thus creating significant time and overall cost savings for Program. The requested instrument will serve for development work at the VRC and GMP scale production at the Vaccine Clinical Materials Program (VCMP), and will provide the highest chance of success to meet the VRC’s mission.
The Knauer IJM Nanoscaler also meets the following technical requirements that is required by Program:
1. Microfluidic Channels are Required
2. Impingement Jet Mixing Technology is Required and Non-Substitutable
3. Operational Pressure Capacity at ~100 bar is Required
4. Operational Volumetric Range encompassing 15 mL to > 1 L Production Runs is Required
5. Temperature Control of Post Mixing Chamber Flow Path is Required
6. Encapsulation Efficiency > 90% is Required
7. Linear Scale Up from Development to Production Scale is Required
8. Identical Software Supporting Development and Production Platforms is Required
9. 21 CFR Compliance is Required
10. IQOQ Availability is Required
11. Mobile Platform With a Minimal Footprint is Required
12. Stainless Steel Construction is Required
13. In Line Absorbance Detection is Required

To our knowledge, only this IJM Nanoscaler platform from Knauer can meet the requirements of Program, and this platform is made in a foreign country of Germany.

column5: EasySep Negative Human CD4 Kits from StemCell Technologies
column29:
Part#19052; Description: EasySep Negative Human CD4 Kit; Qty of 160
This item is made by StemCell Technologies in Canada. 

National Institute of Allergy & Infectious Diseases (NIAID)’s Laboratory of Immunoregulation (LIR) (collectively “Program”) focuses its research efforts on the elucidation of cellular and molecular mechanisms of the regulation of the human immune response in health and disease. A major component of these efforts is focused on the study of the mechanisms of viral persistence in human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) infected individuals receiving antiretroviral therapy (ART). Program requires certain types of reagents for use in their long-standing research projects in the area of HIV/AIDS Pathogenesis. Specifically, they need EasySep Negative Human CD4 enrichment kits to isolate CD4+ T cells from HIV-infected individuals. This item provides a mechanism for negative selection of desired cell populations, a process designed to maximize yields and purities while minimizing loss of cell viability. The robotic feature of the isolation process also ensures reproducibility from one sample isolation to another, an important feature in both longitudinal and cross-sectional analyses. The requested reagents will be used to isolate T cells from the following ongoing clinical trials at NIAID NCT000389 and NCT05612178) and other related studies (Role of Viral Reservoirs in Pathogenesis of HIV Disease (Z01-AI000851-05) to understand how the host immune system controls HIV viral replication without antiretroviral drugs.

This acquisition has unique technical and compatibility requirements. These EasySep Negative Human CD4 Kits are suitable for Program’s requirements and will work with RoboSep instruments manufactured by StemCell Technologies currently in use by Program labs. This system is the world’s only fully automated magnetic cell separation system and have the following capabilities needed by Program: 1. Fully–automated Cell Separation System, 2. Multiple Simultaneous Separations, 3. Column-Free, 4. Customizable products, 5. Sterile Operating System, 6. Antibodies and magnetic beads, and 7. No Maintenance operation. This StemCell Technologies instrument needs to use the kits manufactured by StemCells.
Furthermore, Program lab has been using these same kits to process patient specimens in on-going clinical trails and research. Any changes in supplies will be disruptive to the research project for Role of Viral Reservoirs in Pathogenesis of HIV Disease (Z01-AI000851-05). The reagent requested is to be used in the most critical step of Program’s analyses where we need to isolate pure T-cell populations in a highly reproducible way. StemCell’s EasySep Negative Human CD4 cocktail of reagents used to isolate these pure populations are designed specifically for Program’s patient populations, and any variation in these cocktails would introduce a variable that would impede Program’s ability to compare both cross-sectional and longitudinal assays that Program performs with the cells isolated using these cocktails. Reproducibility and consistency is critical to pursue Program’s research goals and bring projects to completion.

The only source and items known to meet Program's requirement are these EasySep items made by StemCell Technologies in Canada.

The National Institute of Allergy & Infectious Diseases (NIAID)’s Laboratory of Immunogenetics Autoimmunity (LIA), Functional Genomics Section (AFGS) (collectively “Program”) is working to identify genetic and environmental modifiers of Autoimmune Diseases. To this end, Program needs to study the function of specific immune cells population during inflammation, autoimmune disease or after cellular activation with various stimuli. To complement this equipment, Myltenyi Biotec provides optimized protocols that are specifically designed to purify immune cells from various tissues of the mouse. The MACS MiniSampler S is an accessory for the autoMACS NEO Separator and is intended to be used only in combination with this instrument for automated magnetic cell separation. Purification and characterization of different immune cell populations from mouse tissue is an essential experimental step in Program’s research protocols. The autoMACS® NEO machine, together with associated accessory, reagents and optimized protocols, cover all of Program’s needs with the required specifications. This machine will be used for immune cell separation by many Investigators in Program’s Lab at 5625FL/4N03A for the research project entitled: AI000912 Genetic and Environmental Modifiers of Autoimmune Disease. 
This acquisition has unique technical and compatibility requirements. The need for this specific machine is to continue research that was initiated with an autoMACS Pro cell separator from Miltenyi Biotech that will be replaced with this new autoMACS® NEO Separator Instrument plus a MACS MiniSampler S from the same manufacturer, Miltenyi Biotec. Program needs to continue identical experimental protocols and use the same reagents so Program can have consistent data to combine with data already collected. AutoMACS equipment was originally selected because it is consistently precise, reliable and efficient in cell purification protocols. column29: This requirement came in with 124 foreign manufactured line items for upgrade parts for a Single objective light sheet (SOLS) microscope. Through Market Research and the solicitation, 87 of those line items were changed to a domestic manufacture.  No domestic sources could be identified for the remaining 37 line items.  The total estimated value of this acquisition is $38,638.35 (total for all line items, both foreign and domestic).  $23,732.68 is the estimated total of the 87 line items that are manufactured in the United States.  $11,170.93 is the  estimated total of line items manufactured in the United Kingdom.  $1,412.91 is the estimated total of line items manufactured in China.  $973.16 is the estimated total of line items manufactured in Germany.  $827.35 is the estimated total of the line items manufactured in Sweden. $521.00 is the estimated total of line items that are manufactured in Taiwan. $14,905.35 is the estimated total of all foreign manufactured line items. 87 out of 124 line items were successfully changed to domestic manufactured items. This waiver request is being filed for the 37 line items that are foreign manufactured items, totaling $14,905.35.  Most of these parts are not spare or replacement parts. Most of these parts are new parts that will be installed as an upgrade to the Single objective light sheet (SOLS) microscope.  The SOLS is a significant advance in light sheet (LS) technology and is at the very cutting edge for 3D live cell imaging applications. Live cells illuminated with a LS instead of traditional modalities, experience significantly less to no phototoxic and photobleaching effects, which, in turn, provides a tremendous advantage to researchers who need to image longer with high image capture frequency or extremely fast (in the millisecond regime). At the heart of the SOLS is the optic that allows the system to be assembled around a high NA (1.35), 100x objective lens on a standard inverted microscope platform, thus meeting the requirement for the resolution to study intracellular dynamics and the ability to use standard live cell imaging chambers in a familiar format for novice users. The SOLS microscope will be used in collaboration LMVR and many other laboratories across NIAID and for compatibility, it is critical that the microscope components follow the exact specifications for all parts established by the designers for success. The requested brand name or equal High-Speed Camera and Cooling System are components required for a new single objective light sheet (SOLS) microscope components are manufactured by Thor Labs in the listed countries are commercially available supply and is valued at $38,638.61.

column5: High Resolution Platform for In-Vitro Electrophysiological Imaging with High-Density Microelectrode Array
column29:
The National Institute on Aging (NIA) requires a high-resolution platform for in-vitro electrophysiological imaging with high-density microelectrode array that can record electrical currents from cell cultures including 3D organoids simultaneously from 4096 electrodes providing an unparalleled advantage in terms of recording accuracy and totality. The salient characteristics of this platform are as follows:
•	Record electrical currents simultaneously from 4096 electrodes 
•	Have a small footprint and can be placed on any lab bench and works without additional equipment such as Faraday cages or anti-vibration tables 
•	Complex electrophysiological traces that can then be converted into microscopy-like, easy-to-understand, fast-to-process functional images. 
•	The platform shall use a metal oxide semiconductor (CMOS)-based HD-MEA (high-density microelectrode array) as a reusable consumable
•	Capable of being upgraded with a 3D module, allowing to read out Accura HD-MEAs that feature 4096 3D electrodes. The Accura 3D HD-MEAs shall offer distinct advantages for recording from 3D models such as brain slices or brain organoids. The 4096 micro-needles penetrate the tissue and allow recordings from the inner cell layers, strongly improving signal quantity as well as quality. During drug applications, the 3D electrodes shall anchor the tissue on the chip, preventing displacement and enabling the same cells to be followed throughout the entire experiment. Additionally, 3D HDMEAs feature microfluidic channels that allow free nutrient and oxygen flow from below, increasing cell viability and ensuring efficient compound delivery.

•	The required system shall also come with compatible software that offers a variety of analysis tools from detection of single-cell spikes as well as local field potentials up to sophisticated algorithms and AI-powered tools.

column5: Heska Element HT5+ Veterinary Hematology Analyzer
column29: The Element HT5+ Veterinary Hematology Analyzer, manufactured by Heska in China. The Laboratory of Malaria Immunology and Vaccinology (LMIV) conducts basic research on malaria pathogenesis and immunology, with an emphasis on studies in humans who are naturally or experimentally infected with malaria parasites. Specialists in each step of the development process, from antigen selection to clinical trials, contribute their expertise as the candidate moves along the development pathway. This allows multiple vaccine candidates to move from concept to clinical trials efficiently and rapidly. This Element HT5+ Veterinary Hematology Analyzer is a blood chemistry analyzer and is being requested by the Laboratory of Malaria Immunology and Vaccinology (LMIV) as we need an additional blood chemistry analyzer to analyze blood collected from animal studies looking at various malaria vaccine candidates in pre-clinical trials. This analyzer will provide important information about the condition of the animal in real time which will be an improvement over waiting for a contract company to read and provide the results. This is a new machine model not yet released but is near market. The technology is based on the old HT5 system that has been in production for many years and used by our collaborators in the Vaccine Research. Center (VRC). The ability to study anemia is important to our work as many people in Africa suffer from anemia with malaria parasites in their blood. The ability to accurately measure reticulocytes is a critical component of our research and this machine has a 95% accuracy rate for that measurement. This blood chemistry analyzer will replace one that is beginning to fail. LMIV has been contracting out blood chemistry assays with Antech Diagnostics (a local veterinary diagnostic company) who can perform Complete Blood Counts (CBC) and other requested animal blood work at a fee for service, and we’ve spent thousands of dollars with Antech having them perform the blood chemistry analysis, because one blood chemistry analyzer in the lab can’t keep up with the current workload.  Procuring a second blood chemistry analyzer will save time and government funds and eliminate the need for an outside source to perform blood chemistry assays for us, which will also allow us to make quicker and more informed decisions regarding our preclinical studies. Since we’re currently using an older version of the requested blood chemistry analyzer, same as Antech Diagnostics uses, and our collaborators in the NIAID/VRC currently owns one and uses the HT5 which is the previous generation model, we need to maintain continuity protocols and use the same instrument. The Element HT5+ instrument can be integrated with our current protocols for studying reticulocytes accurately, which is a critical component to our mission related work using Non-Human Primate’s (NHP) in preclinical studies. Other major required parameters include total red blood cell count, hemoglobin, hematocrit, Mean cell volume, mean corpuscular Hemoglobin, white blood cell distribution in a scatter plot, white blood cell distribution in a  histogram, RBC size distribution as a histogram and platelet size distribution as a histogram.

The VEIU Mosquito-borne flaviviruses infect hundreds of millions of people globally each year and cause a spectrum of life threatening
diseases, including hemorrhagic fevers, encephalitis, and severe congenital abnormalities. There are still no licensed, broadly protective vaccines against two of the most important flaviviruses: dengue virus and Zika virus. Dengue viruses 1-4 are challenging vaccine targets because sub protective vaccines can increase risk of Dengue Hemorrhagic Fever/Dengue Shock Syndrome, the disease dengue vaccines are designed to prevent. The only licensed dengue vaccine to date significantly increases risk of severe dengue disease in those without a prior dengue virus exposure. Zika viruses emerged across the Americas in 2014-2017, causing major pandemics and congenital Zika syndrome, making development of a Zika vaccine a high priority.

column29:
Tri-color multichannel fiber photometry system. This requirement aims to
procure a tricolor multichannel fiber photometry system to enable researchers
to take advantage of optogenetics, to explore detailed insight into the activity
and behavior of neuronal populations. This technique stimulates neurons with
light and measures fluorescence signals corresponding to calcium dynamics
by detecting changes in the fluorescence intensity from a genetically encoded
calcium indicator (GECI). Calcium dynamics related to activities and patterns
in neural circuits can be measured in real-time. The technique can help
develop treatments for neurological diseases and brain-related traumatic
injuries. Its use of light offers many advantages over other neuromodulation
methods, including a temporal resolution on the order of milliseconds, closely
matching natural neuronal activity, and the stimulation of multiple neuron sets
independently through different wavelengths. In fiber photometry, data is
collected by analyzing the change in fluorescence (ÄF) relative to an initial
baseline fluorescence (F) and observing a change in signal that corresponds to
a calcium transient (ÄF/F). These indicators are typically based on
fluorophores like GFP, RFP, tdTomato, mCherry, etc., of which GCaMP is
the most common example. GCaMP contains Green Fluorescent Protein
(GFP). As such, GCaMP exhibits the same fluorescence patterns as
standalone GFP and provides key insight into calcium dynamics because
GCaMP only fluoresces when bound to a calcium ion. In neurons, calcium
ions regulate several important processes, including neurotransmitter release
and membrane excitability. Therefore, GCaMP fluorescence is tied directly to
neuronal response patterns, as depicted by the graph to the right. After
searching the market, we consider the RWD tricolor multichannel fiber
photometry the fit product for long-term scientific projects.

The Laboratory of Molecular Biology (LMB), CCR, NCI, conducts research to understand basic biochemical processes such as gene transcription, post-translational regulation, molecular chaperone systems, and bacterial cell behavior, with aims toward developing treatments for cancer and antibiotic-resistant bacteria. As part of this research, LMB utilizes a tabletop bath sonicator for DNA and RNA shearing, chromatin shearing, tissue and cell disruption, protein, DNA, and RNA extraction, protein aggregation, and lipid vesicle formation. The LMB’s current bath sonicator has ceased working, and is unable to be repaired, and as such, needs to be replaced. Since their device is beyond repair the LMB submitted an Acquisition Request Packet (APR) for the procurement of a new tabletop bath sonicator, for which the estimated value is $25,850.00.   

To continue the LMB’s research without significant delay, and in order to prepare comparable samples, they require a new tabletop bath sonicator that meets or exceeds their previous equipment’s specifications. Without such a device, the LMB’s research will be significantly delayed, and require them to develop another method of sample processing and preparation. Based on market research failing to identify a suitable domestic product, or small businesses capable of providing a required equivalent product, a Combined Synopsis/Solicitation #75N91023Q00121 was posted to SAM.gov on 07/28/2023 thru 08/04/2023, utilizing full and open competition. Additionally, due to the estimated value of this procurement no Trade Agreements were determined as being applicable, and that the Buy American Statue applied. This requirement is a sole source procurement to provide Vaisala Model Number TSS928 thunderstorm sensors to support the US Navy’s operational requirements for
their Automated Surface Observing Systems (ASOS). National Weather Service (NWS) is the Primary Inventory Control Activity for the US Navy ASOS sites, which means that NWS is responsible for establishing and controlling stockage objectives and maintaining item accountability on behalf of the US Navy for these items. The Vaisala Model Number TSS928 thunderstorm sensor is a self-contained standalone device that detects the presence of all forms of lightning, including intra-cloud, cloud to cloud and cloud to ground. Due to their proprietary nature, Vaisala’s TSS928 thunderstorm sensor is the only thunderstorm sensor that will interface with the existing ASOS power supplies and hardware for the Navy’s ASOS units. The strategy to satisfy the thunderstorm sensors requirement is to purchase twenty thunderstorm sensors with a delivery period of 180 days after receipt of purchase order. This purchase allows the NWS to accomplish its mission of upper-air observations and for providing US Navy ASOS sites with required material support. NWS ASOS Programs currently use Vaisala Model Number TSS924 thunderstorm sensor which has been in use for an excess of fifteen years. The TSS924 thunderstorm sensor contains proprietary algorithms and data strings that are currently embedded in the NWS ASOS system. The TSS924 thunderstorm sensor meets specific Air Traffic Organization requirements for ASOS and has been approved by the Federal Aviation Administration. The ASOS network has Vaisala Model Number TSS924 thunderstorm sensors installed at 25% of ASOS locations. The US Navy needs to complete their ASOS unit installation in several overseas locations and requested the
continued use of the Vaisala Model Number TSS924 thunderstorm sensor. However, the Vaisala Model Number TSS924 thunderstorm sensor was discontinued in 2018. Vaisala’s current production Thunderstorm sensor is the Model Number TSS928 and which replaced the previous model in the ASOS network. The sensor TSS928 meteorological performance matches the performance of the TSS924, since the sensor modules are the same. The TSS928 sensor has the form, fit and function with limited testing.

column5: Miltenyi gentleMACS Octo Dissociator with Heating Unit
column29: The gentleMACS Octo Dissociator with Heating Units, is manufactured by Miltenyi Biotec in Germany, will be used to support the mission of the RNA Viruses Section (RVS), Laboratory of Infectious Disease (LID), of the National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases (NIAID). The RVS is requesting the above referenced gentleMACS Octo Dissociator with Heaters, manufactured by Miltenyi for use in our long-standing clinical and research projects. The RVS has been generating seed viruses for live viral vector vaccines for intranasal immunization against SARS-CoV-2. The requested equipment is a fully automated tissue dissociation/homogenization device capable of executing up to 8 samples at one time with multiple programs allow various types of tissues. The instrument is essential for viral isolation/titration and isolation of lymphoid cells from tissue samples obtained from on-going pre-clinical animal studies. We possess one unit of this equipment, however there is a high demand with increased pre-clinical studies for COVID vaccines and, therefore, another unit is needed. The RVS focuses their studies of molecular biology, immunobiology, and pathogenesis of the human respiratory pathogens respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), human parainfluenza virus (HPIV) serotypes 1, 2, and 3, and human metapneumovirus (HMPV). Studies involve infection in vitro of epithelial cells, macrophages, and other cell types and in vivo infection of experimental animals to elucidate the viral replicative cycle, interactions between viral and host components, the host response to infection, and mechanisms of pathogenesis. Development of live-attenuated RSV, HPIV3, and HMPV vaccine candidates Vaccine candidates are designed to contain well defined attenuating mutations and are generated from cDNA by “reverse genetics”. Candidates are evaluated preclinically in monolayer cell cultures, in vitro models of airway epithelium, rodents, and nonhuman primates. Evaluation of candidate live vaccines in clinical studies with clinical collaborators, as well as wild type viruses in adult volunteers Development of vaccine vectors based on HPIV and avian paramyxoviruses (APMVs) for use against highly pathogenic emerging viruses including SARS Coronavirus 2, avian influenza, and Ebola viruses.

The Qubit fluorometers and accessories are required and being requested for use in supporting the research that is taking place in the Laboratory of Bacteriology (LB), Coxiella Pathogenesis Section (CPS)at Rocky Mountain Labs (RML) in Hamilton, MT focuses on investigating Coxiella burnetii, a bacterium that causes a severe flu-like illness called Q fever. Humans typically acquire the pathogen through inhalation of contaminated material generated by infected domestic livestock. Coxiella is also a recognized category B biothreat with potential for illegitimate use. During infection, the organism invades macrophages, where it directs formation of a lysosome-like parasitophorous vacuole (PV) in which to replicate. A mature PV containing hundreds of organisms can occupy nearly the entire host cell cytoplasm in the absence of obvious cytopathic effect. Coxiella’s resistance to the harsh conditions of its PV also correlates with spore-like environmental stability. The Qubit brand of fluorometers being requested as there was no domestic alternatives identified in market research, and no domestic responses to the full and open, brand name or equal solicitation that was posted on SAM.gov. There are no other known US manufactured brands of fluorometers found available in the commercial marketplace that meet the requirements of NIAID.

The National Center for Advancing Translational Sciences (NCATS) is a part of the National Institutes of Health (NIH), This instrument modification will enable NCATS researchers to gently wash 1536-well microplates using centrifugation, which will greatly increase the throughput assays that require multiple wash steps or prolonged culture of cells that require medium exchange. Centrifugation allows for a contact-free and complete removal of liquid from the well without disrupting adherent cells.  High-throughput screening projects require the washing of 384- and 1536-well microplates. One of NCATS’ goals is to develop methods that advance the science of high throughput screening and informatics by addressing the high incidence of false-positives in target discovery. To this end, it is imperative we reduce errors to minimal.

The BlueWasher will reduce noise in many phenotypic screens we are running as part of our research screenings. ETB requires the addition of 1536-well dispensing capabilities to an existing BlueCat washer, which includes firmware and GUI update, a dispenser comb for 1536w plates with magnetic quick swap and set of 2 plate carriers for 8mm thin 1536w plates.

Overall, this project will support ongoing work for small molecule and combination screening, in silico virtual screening, and discovery work that is part of NCATS’ ongoing work to bring more treatments to all people more quickly.

column5: Oxford Nanopore Technologies GridION Device & Regents
column29:
The GridION sequencer, consumables, and reagents are required for use in supporting the research that is taking place in the Laboratory of Virology (LV), Virus Ecology Unit (VEU) at Rocky Mountain Lab in Hamilton, MT to conducts research involving surveillance for emerging virus pathogens and host evolution of severe pathogenic viruses such as SARS-CoV-2, Nipah, and Ebola viruses. These studies are essential for assessing the risk of zoonotic spillover from novel viruses and the emergence of new variants during ongoing epidemics and pandemics. To efficiently study virus evolution, deep and rapid sequencing of virus genomic material from experimental, clinical, and field samples is required. New instrument must be compatible with GridION software to work without current instruments. The Nanopore GridION Many deep sequencing platforms exist, but they are usually expensive, have a large footprint, require extensive training, and are time consuming to use and generate data. However, Nanopore’s GridION Mk1 sequencing platform is particularly suitable for our purposes. It provides high throughput data generation of up to 250 Gb with at least 420 bases / second in 72 hours. However, any equal brand that meet these brand name or equal requirements will satisfy the need. This instrument allows us to run at least 50-100 samples per session which is what we require for our studies. Also, GridION Mk1 provides real-time processing and analysis of data enabling rapid detection of emergent variants of viruses in real time. These rapid data
will provide information on variants in circulation which is essential to public health control policy decisions. Finally, our lab uses nanopore technology and we already have reagents from this company for sequencing purposes and training on these instruments. Therefore, we need a brand name or equal Nanopore GridION Mk1 for rapid deep sequencing of lab, clinical and field samples.

column5: Miltenyi gentleMACS Octo Dissociators with Heating Units

The IIPS is requesting the above referenced gentleMACS Octo Dissociator with Heaters, manufactured by Miltenyi for use in our long-standing clinical and research projects. The IIPS focuses their research on the innate immune response is rapidly engaged after virus infection and functions to limit virus replication and mobilize adaptive immune responses. Viruses have evolved diverse strategies to suppress this critical host response and facilitate virus dissemination. In part, virus pathogenesis is a function of both the quality of the innate response evoked and the evasion strategies encoded by the invading pathogen. Thus, understanding the mechanisms underpinning immune activation and virus evasion will lead to improved vaccine design and novel therapeutics for treatment of both infectious and inflammatory disease. This gentleMACS Octo Dissociator with Heaters unit is an isolation system that is required to provide flexibility to researchers for multi-tasking and would allow consistent and higher productivity, to keep up with increasing studies and workload. The gentleMACS Octo Dissociator with heaters offers full automation for tissue dissociation and is enabling the dissociation up to eight samples at a time, without cross contamination and meeting the labs requirements by providing the required performance in standardization of sample processing from tissue to single cell suspension with high yield and viability. It also integrates user-friendly connectivity features for maximum operator convenience and traceability, which significantly increases the efficiency and productivity of experimental tasks in the lab. This gentleMACS Octo Dissociator with Heaters is also required to dissociate cells from various organs such as spleen, lymph nodes, brain, kidney etc. and make a single cells suspension and purification for studying the function of specific immune cells population during inflammation, autoimmune disease or after cellular activation with various stimuli. The unique required specifications for this instrument are tubes that bear a unique rotor/stator system for the gentle and efficient tissue dissociation or homogenization and the gentleMACS Dissociators are the only known devices that in combination with the gentleMACS Tubes can be used to dissociate or homogenize various tissues in a closed system, and process multiple samples at once without cross contamination. The only source and instrument known to meet these specifications is the gentleMACS Octo Dissociator with Heating Unit, which is manufactured by Miltenyi Biotec in Germany.

The Rodent Behavioral Core (RBC) is a state-of-the-art research facility designed to provide NIH researchers the capacity to support NIMH and NIH in their behavioral research through facilitating research initiatives by providing assistance in experimental design, training, execution, and analysis of behavioral neuroscience experiments.

The NIMH will need these items to provide instrumentation for the RBC. The RBC provides an invaluable service to NIMH and other institutes by facilitating behavioral neuroscience experiments and supporting research initiatives across various domains supporting the NIMH and NIH mission. To this end, ethologically relevant tests are important for studying the behavior and cognition of rodents in a natural environment and help researchers understand the mechanisms of neuropsychiatric disorders and reduce animal stress. The Smart-Kage system offers a novel, comprehensive testing unit that leverages artificial-intelligence and machine-learning technology to drive the assessment of behaviors in mice. This new system allows for comprehensive assessment of mouse behaviors in a home environment, whereby mice have access to a T-maze, novel-object recognition, operant conditioning, motor coordination and activity as well as food and water consumption. Through its innovative approach to home-cage testing, this product minimizes the need for any food and water restrictions, and it allows investigators to provide a comprehensive test environment for animals without utilizing any invasive testing methodology.

For large sample sizes, testing large numbers of animals on various testing paradigms can take weeks, and requires the coordination and availability of multiple testing systems. With this automated and integrated home-cage design, the Smart-Kage boxes would allow investigators to perform and acquire results for multiple cognition, motor, and affective levels of assessment simultaneously, minimizing the use of multiple pieces of equipment. Furthermore, the AI-driven technology facilitates automated tracking of animals their home-cage environment, which streamlines the data acquisition and analysis streamlining the experimental pipeline. These advances can help researchers in reducing manual errors, increasing reproducibility, and accelerating the pace of research.

The National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke (NINDS), which is an Institute at the National Institutes of Health (NIH), mission is to seek fundamental knowledge about the brain and nervous system and to use that knowledge to reduce the burden of neurological disease for all people.

The NINDS Dendrite Morphogenesis and Plasticity Section (DMPU) applied the Drosophila larval visual circuit and investigates the structural and functional plasticity changes to find the mechanisms underlying the neuronal development. The DMPU is using in-vivo live imaging with the aid of a multiphoton scanning system to study different candidate genes. The primary approach is to understand molecular path to regulate dendrite morphogenesis and structural plasticity, and learn the consequent function change due to deficits on homeostatic neuronal plasticity.

To reach the most function of the NINDS government-owned LSM 980 multiphoton system, the Chameleon Discovery NX or equal will enable a high power laser for multiphoton microscopy to make brighter and deeper live imaging possible in the DMPU’s new and ongoing research studies.

The purpose of this requirement is to procure one (1) Chameleon Discovery NX or equal with trade-in for the NINDS for replacement due to damage by the flood in Building 35. This tunable femtosecond laser system is designed for multiphoton microscopy and shall be used to provide dual laser power to the NINDS government-owned LSM980 multiphoton system used to take live imaging of Drosophila brain with fluorophore probe to obtain deeper and brighter than usual images when NINDS researchers collect in-vivo data, which is used for analysis dynamic change at the early dendrite development stage.

The Department of Health and Human Services (HHS), National Institutes of Health (NIH), National Institute on Drug Abuse (NIDA), Neurobiology of Addiction Section (NOAS) of
the Integrative Neuroscience Research Branch (INRB) is examining drug dependence as a complex psychiatric condition characterized by craving, compulsive drug seeking, excessive drug taking, and the emergence of negative emotional states during withdrawal. With the opioid epidemic recently declared a public health emergency due to the unprecedented high levels of opioid-related overdose deaths, NOAS is working to test currently approved medications for opioid dependence. There are medications approved for the treatment of opioid addiction. However, it is unknown whether these medications protect or make it worse if an individual relapses to drug taking. In order to examine these effects, a whole-body plethysmography system is required to currently approved medications for opioid dependence as well as novel medications for their potential to block or reverse respiratory depression caused by opioids. The required system will allow the study of causes of opioid overdose deaths by comparing the effects of currently approved drugs to treat opioid dependence on opioid induced overdose deaths and studying the potential effects of novel compounds in reversing opioid-induced respiratory depression. The plethysmography system will be crucial to monitor respiratory rate, estimated tidal volume, minute ventilation and events like apneas and deep sighs. Importantly, this system allows us to monitor all these measures in an awake, freely behaving animal and can be used in mice and rats. NIDA
wants to expand the system that currently in use to evaluate eight animals at the same time. The required systems needs to analyze in real time several parameters of respiration patterns, so that pharmacological manipulations could be used to alter these respiration patterns.

The NINDS Division of Intramural Research (DIR) is the internal research program of the NINDS and is among the largest neuroscience research centers in the world. It is
home to more than 500 scientists who conduct leading-edge basic, translational, and clinical research in neuroscience, neurology, and neurosurgery research at NINDS
laboratories in Bethesda, Maryland. The Spatial Navigation and Memory Unit (SNMU) is focused on the neural basis of spatial navigation and memory, with the ultimate goal to uncover the fundamental principle of spatial cognition and the cause of related neurological disorders. The laboratory primarily focuses on the medial entorhinal cortex (MEC), which is heavily involved in spatial cognition. Using genetic mouse models, the lab is addressing several fundamental questions: (1) how is spatial information represented and computed in the MEC circuit; (2) whether and how is spatial memory encoded in the MEC; (3) how the MEC interacts with other brain areas to perform its function; (4) how the physiological function of the MEC is disrupted in Alzheimer's Disease. The laboratory plans to combine in vivo two-photon imaging and two-photon optogenetics at the cellular resolution to investigate the effect of modulating different functional cell types on the MEC network response and behavior. The laboratory will require a one-box, hands-free, turn-key amplified fiber laser system with computer control and software GUI such as the brand name or equal Monaco 1035-40-40 LX laser to achieve the above experiments. The collected data are important to understand the causal role of the MEC circuit in spatial cognition.
